title: Move.toml pins `pas` and `ptb` to branch `main` — non-reproducible dependency
recommendations: Change `rev = "main"` to `rev = "b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc"` (or a later audited tag) in both `pas` and `ptb` entries. Add a CI check that `Move.toml` does not reference a branch. Consider tagging a PAS release in `MystenLabs/pas` and pinning to the tag.

Replace `rev = "main"` with the audited commit SHA (`b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc` currently in Move.lock). Document the dep-bump process so updating these deps is an explicit, reviewed change.
Replace `rev = "main"` with the exact commit SHA already pinned in Move.lock (`b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`) for both `pas` and `ptb`. Rotate the SHA explicitly when upgrading.

Change both rev values to the exact SHA currently in Move.lock (e.g. rev = "b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc"). Promote SHA updates to conscious bumps with a changelog entry and re-run integration tests.

Change both `rev = "main"` entries in Move.toml to the same commit SHA already pinned in Move.lock (b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc). Add a CI check that rejects any manifest with rev equal to a branch name. Document a dep-bump process that requires a regression test run and a re-review of the integration-boundary.
Change root `Move.toml` edition from `2024.beta` to `2024`. Verify with `sui move build` that no beta-only features are used.

Pin `rev` to the same commit hash recorded in `Move.lock` (`b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`). Update via governance-tracked PRs only. Ideally add a CI check that fails if manifest `rev` is not a 40-char hex string.

Replace `rev = "main"` with the current lockfile commit `b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc` in both dependency lines. Update the commit deliberately when upgrading, bumping Hadron version at the same time. Consider documenting the upgrade policy in the PR description.

Change `rev = "main"` to the exact commit SHA (`b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`) that matches the lockfile. Adopt a policy that both lockfile and manifest must carry the same SHA and that bumps are deliberate, reviewed PRs.

Replace `rev = "main"` with the exact commit SHA in the lockfile (`b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`) for both packages, and do the same for any additional environment (mainnet) before that environment is opened.

Replace `rev = "main"` with the pinned SHA from Move.lock (`b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`). Add a CI check that fails if any `git` dep in Move.toml uses a branch name rather than a 40-char SHA. Consider tag-based pinning once PAS publishes versioned releases.
descriptions: Both `pas` and `ptb` git dependencies are declared with `rev = "main"` (a branch name) rather than a commit SHA. Although `Move.lock` currently pins to `b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`, a subsequent `sui move build --lock` or a fresh clone that regenerates the lockfile will rotate to whatever `main` points to at that moment. This silently changes the security surface of the Hadron package — the PAS `policy`, `request`, `send_funds`, `clawback_funds`, `templates`, and `internal::Permit` types all come from that dep.

Both pas and ptb git dependencies are pinned to `rev = "main"` (branch name) instead of a commit SHA. The build lockfile pins `b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`, but the manifest itself is not reproducible from source: anyone regenerating the lockfile against a newer `main` will silently pull different upstream semantics. Upstream PAS behavior changes (e.g., additions to required_approvals action strings, account.owner semantics, send_funds::resolve_balance destination) would be absorbed without notice.

Both PAS-related Git dependencies are pinned to the branch name 'main' rather than a commit SHA. This makes the build non-reproducible: any future commit on upstream MystenLabs/pas@main silently becomes a new dependency input the next time Move.lock is regenerated. The Move.lock currently pins to b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc, but a `sui move build` with `--force` or a lockfile-less environment would silently pull new upstream code. A malicious or accidental upstream change would be shipped without review.

The pas and ptb dependencies are declared with rev = "main". Move tooling treats this as a mutable branch name. The Move.lock currently pins a specific SHA (b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc), but any lockfile regeneration (sui move update-deps, Move.lock deletion, or CI rebuild in a fresh workspace) will silently roll forward to the latest main commit. Upstream PAS is on testnet only and evolves — a breaking change in policy/send_funds/clawback_funds APIs can land without any Hadron-side signal, and the next build will compile against new semantics while reviewers believe the tested SHA still applies.

The pas and ptb git dependencies are pinned to the branch name `main` rather than an immutable commit SHA. Any push to MystenLabs/pas#main silently changes the surface the audited code compiles against, including public functions, required_approvals, clawback semantics, etc. The Move.lock currently pins the testnet env to b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc, which is the trust anchor used by this review — but a `move build` that re-resolves the lock (or a fresh checkout on another environment) will pick up new upstream code without any PR signal.

Hadron's Move.toml declares `edition = "2024.beta"` while `token_template_pas/Move.toml` declares `edition = "2024"`. `2024.beta` is deprecated in favor of `2024` (stable edition). The global user-CLAUDE-md prescribes `edition = "2024"`. The mismatch between the two packages is also ugly — both should be on the same stable edition.

The `pas` and `ptb` dependencies use `rev = "main"`, which is a branch name, not a commit hash. Every fresh `sui move build` re-resolves to whatever `main` currently points to upstream. The `Move.lock` does pin to `b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc` for the `testnet` environment, but the manifest drift means (a) builds in other environments will pick up different code, (b) `sui move manage-package` flows that ignore the lock will drift, and (c) upstream pushes to `main` can silently change the ABI assumed by Hadron (e.g., `send_funds::resolve_balance` semantics).

Both `pas` and `ptb` git dependencies pin `rev = "main"` (a branch name) rather than a commit SHA. This makes Move source builds non-reproducible: running `sui move build` at two points in time may fetch different upstream bytes. The lockfile currently pins both to commit `b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`, but the lockfile pin is advisory and the manifest governs re-resolution.

Both PAS dependencies are pinned to `rev = "main"`, which is a moving branch reference. Upstream PAS can re-point `main` at any time, silently changing the Hadron build output and runtime semantics (type-name hashes, PTB template serialization, witness types) without any change in Hadron's own source. The lockfile currently pins `b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`, so day-to-day builds are stable, but any `sui move build` that regenerates the lock (CI, fresh checkout without lockfile, `update-deps`) will silently roll forward to the current upstream tip.

Both `pas` and `ptb` git dependencies are pinned to `rev = "main"`, which is a mutable branch name. Move's dependency resolver re-fetches per build, so any upstream change (security fix, breaking rename, account/policy refactor) is silently pulled in. The Move.lock does pin to commit `b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`, but the lock only matters for the declared `testnet` env; a fresh checkout on `mainnet` or a future env would re-resolve against the branch.

Both PAS and PTB git dependencies are pinned to `rev = "main"` (a branch ref) rather than a commit SHA. Any commit to `main` upstream silently changes dependency behavior. Move.lock has it pinned to b64f0c58c6... for the testnet environment, so existing builds resolve deterministically, but a fresh clone on a different environment (mainnet) or after a lockfile regeneration will pick up whatever `main` points to at that moment.
impacts: Non-reproducible builds. Upstream PAS changes (e.g., altered approval semantics, added required approvals, changed `resolve_balance` destination) would be picked up silently on the next lock regeneration. This is also a supply-chain risk — anyone with commit access to `MystenLabs/pas:main` effectively has code-execution over Hadron's next build.

Non-reproducible builds; silent upstream drift can break security invariants (e.g., if upstream changes `account.deposit_balance` target or clawback semantics). Makes CI / audit remediation traceability fragile.

Supply-chain / reproducibility risk. Any fresh environment that regenerates Move.lock imports the latest upstream; semantics of PAS policy / request / templates could change under Hadron without code review.
Non-reproducible builds; silent API drift against upstream PAS can break compliance semantics (e.g. required_approvals, resolve order) without a CI signal.

The audited behaviour and the deployed behaviour can diverge without review, defeating the purpose of the PAS integration audit. The integration-boundary findings in this report are only valid against commit b64f0c58c.
Risk of semantic drift between the two packages. `2024.beta` may be removed in a future toolchain release.
Build non-reproducibility and silent ABI drift risk. Not itself exploitable, but a persistent class of supply-chain issues for any reviewer / auditor reproducing the build.

Upstream PAS evolves rapidly. A non-pinned dep means a future CI run, a contributor setup, or a package upgrade could silently pull in breaking upstream changes (e.g. API renames, changed visibility on `pas::account`, new required approvals) that pass compilation but alter runtime semantics. This is especially dangerous because Hadron's `pas_admin`, `pas_supply`, `pas_transfer` cross-reference upstream types and functions (`SendFunds`, `Account::owner`, `Request::approve`) whose shape is load-bearing.

Non-reproducible builds; supply-chain surface on a mutable branch; a silent upstream change could break on-chain `TransferApproval`/`ClawbackApproval` type-name keys that were hashed into the deployed PAS Policy's `required_approvals`, making already-registered PAS assets permanently unresolvable.

A future `sui move build` could produce a binary that transparently diverges from the audited commit — including behavioural changes in `policy::new_for_currency`, `account::deposit_balance`, or the `clawback_funds::resolve` semantics that Hadron relies on. The integration boundary is broken without warning.

Reproducibility and supply-chain risk. Upstream PAS can change semantics (e.g. approval-stamping, clawback validation) without any local code change. Auditors cannot guarantee the audited version matches a future deployment.

title: No tests for any PAS module (pas_admin, pas_supply, pas_transfer, treasury PAS caps)
recommendations: Add Move unit tests that cover at minimum: (a) `supply::mint`/`redeem` aborts with `EPasCoinNotAllowed` on PAS-registered T; (b) `pas_supply::mint` requires recipient whitelisted with `can_receive=true`; (c) `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` rejects non-whitelisted sender and non-whitelisted recipient separately; (d) `pas_supply::seize` does NOT require source whitelisted but DOES require destination whitelisted; (e) `pas_admin::register_pas_asset` rejects non-empty `TreasuryCap`; (f) `set_whitelist_send/receive` after `remove_from_whitelist` aborts with `ENotInWhitelist`.

Add unit tests for: (a) register_pas_asset happy path + double-registration rejection; (b) whitelist add/remove/set_send/set_receive + idempotency/error cases; (c) mint to non-whitelisted account rejected; (d) approve_transfer happy path + rejections for non-whitelisted sender/recipient + zero amount + paused; (e) seize to non-whitelisted destination rejected; (f) burn path with real Request; (g) policy cap lookup for non-PAS coin returns ENotPasCoin.

Add unit tests covering at minimum: `mint<T>` happy path + abort cases (paused, non-whitelisted recipient, amount zero, non-PAS coin), `burn<T>` happy + abort (paused, amount zero, version blocked), `seize<T>` happy + abort (source de-whitelisted is OK; destination de-whitelisted aborts), `approve_transfer` happy + abort cases, `register_pas_asset` double-registration.

Add a tests/pas_admin_tests.move, tests/pas_supply_tests.move, tests/pas_transfer_tests.move covering: (a) register_pas_asset happy path (asserts whitelist DOF created, policy_cap stored); (b) double-register aborts with ECoinAlreadyRegistered; (c) supply::mint aborts with EPasCoinNotAllowed when T is PAS; (d) approve_transfer aborts on unwhitelisted sender/recipient/zero amount/paused; (e) burn/seize witness type matches policy required_approvals (guards against ClawbackApproval<T> address drift on package upgrade).

Add at minimum: (1) pas_admin_tests covering register_pas_asset happy path, double-register abort, whitelist add/remove/set_send/set_receive, update_transfer_template; (2) pas_supply_tests covering mint amount=0, mint to non-whitelisted, burn wrong-policy abort, seize while paused; (3) pas_transfer_tests covering approve_transfer non-whitelisted sender/recipient, amount=0, paused, and the happy path. Use the upstream PAS test helpers (see /Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/tests/e2e.move for patterns).

Add module-level tests covering at minimum: (1) `register_pas_asset` happy path + double-registration abort, (2) `mint` / `burn` / `seize` happy path + amount-zero / paused / not-whitelisted aborts, (3) `approve_transfer` happy path + paused / unwhitelisted sender / unwhitelisted recipient aborts, (4) `supply::mint` / `supply::redeem` abort with `EPasCoinNotAllowed` when T is a PAS coin, (5) whitelist mutators: add+remove, add-already-present, remove-absent, set_send / set_receive on unknown address.

Add a test_only stub of PAS (or use test_scenario with a minimal upstream harness) to cover at minimum: (1) register_pas_asset happy path and double-registration abort, (2) add/remove_to_whitelist lifecycle + duplicate abort, (3) set_whitelist_send/receive + assert_can_send/assert_can_receive on enabled/disabled/unknown addresses, (4) pas_supply::mint with whitelisted and non-whitelisted recipients, (5) supply::mint and supply::redeem abort with EPasCoinNotAllowed when is_pas_coin, (6) pas_transfer::approve_transfer happy path and with paused mint/redeem.

Add at minimum: (a) happy-path `register_pas_asset` + query `is_pas_coin`+`policy_cap`, (b) `mint` positive + whitelist-failure + mint-paused + amount-zero, (c) `burn` positive + amount-zero, (d) `seize` positive + destination-not-whitelisted + source-not-whitelisted (should succeed to extract), (e) `approve_transfer` positive + paused + sender-not-whitelisted + recipient-not-whitelisted + amount-zero, (f) `add/remove/set_whitelist_*` idempotency and wrong-auth failures. Use `treasury::create_for_testing` plus upstream `pas::namespace::init_for_testing` (if exposed) or a mock namespace.

Add at minimum: (a) a happy-path test that creates a treasury, registers a PAS asset, whitelists two addresses, mints into an account, transfers and seizes; (b) negative tests for `add_to_whitelist<RegularCoin>` (must abort with `ENotPasCoin` after R9-005 fix); (c) a test that verifies `supply::mint<PasCoin>` aborts with `EPasCoinNotAllowed`; (d) a test that exercises the `clawback_funds::resolve` boundary to confirm approval witness matching.

Add a dedicated `tests/pas_admin_tests.move`, `tests/pas_supply_tests.move`, `tests/pas_transfer_tests.move`. Use the test-helper scaffolding already present (`treasury::create_for_testing`, `version::init_for_testing`, `auth::create_auth_for_testing`). Cover: (1) register_pas_asset happy path; (2) double-registration aborts with ECoinAlreadyRegistered; (3) non-PAS coin whitelist mutation aborts cleanly; (4) mint to whitelisted/can_receive account; (5) mint to non-whitelisted address aborts with ENotWhitelistedReceive; (6) paused mint aborts with EMintRedeemDisabled; (7) burn with valid Request + matching Policy; (8) seize happy path and to-non-whitelisted abort; (9) approve_transfer happy path; (10) approve_transfer with non-whitelisted sender/recipient aborts.
descriptions: There is no `tests/` directory in the Hadron package and no `#[test]` or `#[test_only]` function in `pas_admin.move`, `pas_supply.move`, or `pas_transfer.move`. All new flows (register_pas_asset, add/remove/set whitelist, mint, burn, seize, approve_transfer) are completely untested. The only test-only helpers are `treasury::create_for_testing` / `share_for_testing`, which are phase-1 code.

There are no `#[test]` functions in `pas_admin.move`, `pas_supply.move`, or `pas_transfer.move`, and the repo has no `tests/` directory. None of the new state transitions (register_pas_asset, add_to_whitelist / remove_from_whitelist, set_whitelist_send/receive, mint/burn/seize, approve_transfer) are exercised by automated tests. Given that PAS integration touches cross-package invariants (policy / account / templates / requests) and is the entire value of this PR, this is a significant gap. Compare with upstream PAS which ships `pas/packages/pas/tests` coverage.

Neither `pas_admin.move` nor `pas_supply.move` nor `pas_transfer.move` contain any `#[test]` or `#[test_only]` code, and the repo has no `tests/` directory. The PR adds ~300 lines of compliance-critical Move logic — mint/burn/seize/transfer-approve with whitelist gating — with zero unit coverage. Phase-1 modules (e.g. `treasury.move`) only expose `create_for_testing`/`share_for_testing` helpers but none of the new PAS code adds coverage.

There is no tests/ directory in the Hadron package, and no #[test] or #[test_only] functions exist in the new modules pas_admin, pas_supply, or pas_transfer (verified by grep across sources/). All three modules depend on upstream PAS behaviour (policy creation, required_approvals matching, template command shape, request.approve order, clawback/send resolve semantics) that is not pinned by a test. Regression risk is concentrated in the exact places where upstream API drift is most damaging: set_required_approval approval-type key, register_transfer_template command shape, and Request.approve witness matching.

There are no `#[test]` functions anywhere in `sources/pas_*.move`, and the repo has no `tests/` directory at all. Every critical path is exercised only via the e2e TypeScript script in scripts/src/examples/full-setup.ts. That script runs on a live testnet, is slow, and does not exercise failure paths (amount=0, non-whitelisted sender, double-register, paused mint, cross-coin Request/Policy). Phase-1 code may have its own audit, but the new PAS surface is the highest-risk addition and has zero in-repo coverage.

`sources/pas_admin.move`, `sources/pas_supply.move`, and `sources/pas_transfer.move` contain zero `#[test]` / `#[test_only]` functions. The modified PAS-specific functions in `sources/treasury.move` (`add_pas_coin_caps`, `is_pas_coin`, whitelist table CRUD) have no test coverage either. All critical invariants (whitelist gating of mint/transfer/seize, PAS vs non-PAS coin segregation in supply::mint/redeem, template registration/refresh, multi-institution template overwrite) are unverified.

None of the three new modules (`pas_admin`, `pas_supply`, `pas_transfer`) have any `#[test]` functions. The modified treasury PAS paths (`add_pas_coin_caps`, whitelist mutators, `is_pas_coin`, `policy_cap`, whitelist predicates) are also untested. The `is_pas_coin` guard added to `supply::mint`/`supply::redeem` is untested. Critical compliance paths — mint into account, seize via clawback, transfer approval, whitelist lifecycle — rely on PAS upstream mock setup which the repo does not provide.

No `#[test]` or `#[test_only]` functions exist in `pas_admin.move`, `pas_supply.move`, or `pas_transfer.move`. There is also no `tests/` subdirectory for the new PAS functionality. Given this PR is the Move-consultation phase of SOLENG-653, the lack of unit tests for critical flows (register, mint, burn via clawback, seize, approve_transfer with all pause/whitelist permutations) is a significant gap.

`pas_admin.move`, `pas_supply.move`, `pas_transfer.move` contain zero `#[test]` or `#[test_only]` functions. Treasury's new PAS helpers (`add_pas_coin_caps`, whitelist table management) are also untested. Given the multi-dependency nature (hadron ↔ pas ↔ ptb) and the criticality of whitelist enforcement, automated tests are essential for regression.

Scanned for `#[test]` annotations in the new PAS modules — none found. Coverage gap for: register_pas_asset happy-path and double-registration; add/remove/set whitelist transitions; mint with non-whitelisted account owner; mint with paused mint/redeem; burn with valid/invalid Request; seize to non-whitelisted recipient; approve_transfer with non-whitelisted sender/recipient; approve_transfer when paused; post-upgrade update_transfer_template flow.
impacts: High-stakes compliance logic (whitelist gating, seize path, clawback-based burn, PAS policy integration) has no regression coverage. Any upstream PAS change or Hadron refactor can silently break the authorization surface. Reviewers (and CI) have no guard against regressions in critical invariants such as `!is_pas_coin` guards in `supply::mint/redeem`, whitelist send/receive independence, or request-approval ordering.
Refactors and upstream PAS upgrades will silently break invariants. Review-only verification is insufficient for the blast radius (mint, burn, seize, transfer approval).
No automated guard against regression in the compliance core — e.g. future code that reorders approve/assert calls, or drops a whitelist check, would not be caught.
Any upstream API drift goes undetected until full-setup.ts hits testnet. Internal regressions (e.g. pause-flag semantic change, auth.is_coin_registered vs is_pas_coin) are not unit-testable.

Regressions on the PAS surface are invisible in CI. Integration-boundary assumptions (e.g. the shape of Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>> or required_approvals keys) cannot be detected if upstream changes.
Regressions on this PR or any follow-up are undetectable by CI. Integration-boundary breakage from upstream PAS changes will not be caught until production.

All bugs in PAS integration (including every other finding in this review) must be caught by manual PR review or live testnet runs. Regressions from upstream PAS changes, whitelist state mistakes, and permission-coverage gaps have no safety net.

No automated regression barrier against: (1) upstream PAS shape changes, (2) internal refactor of whitelist storage, (3) accidental pause-conflation flips, (4) generic-parameter drift. Manual review alone cannot substitute.

Behavioural changes in upstream `pas::policy`, `pas::templates`, or `pas::account` will only be caught by integration testing on testnet. Version-gate regressions and permission escalations (see R9-005, R9-008) would slip through.
Zero regression safety. Any refactor, upstream-PAS change, or follow-up phase-3 feature could break PAS flows without detection.

title: scripts/deployPasTokenTemplate: coinType synthesized from symbol without OTW validation
recommendations: Parse the type string with a proper regex that includes the exact coin type argument, e.g. `^0x2::coin::TreasuryCap<${coinType}>$` after constructing coinType earlier. Alternatively, use the `@mysten/sui` coin-registry helpers to query the exact cap for a given coin type. Add assertions that exactly one match is found.

Instead of inferring, use `obj.objectType` from the transaction response and parse the `<inner>` type parameter with a regex like `/::coin::TreasuryCap<(.+)>$/`. Store the full coinType after extraction. Same for MetadataCap. Don't construct coinType from symbol.
Prefer matching against fully qualified type tags `0x2::coin::TreasuryCap<${coinType}>` once `coinType` is known, or use the object's `objectChanges` structured type.

Prefer exact type-prefix matching against `${SUI_FRAMEWORK_ADDRESS}::coin::TreasuryCap<` and `${SUI_FRAMEWORK_ADDRESS}::coin_registry::MetadataCap<`. Or better: match by the Move resource's canonical type name (normalizeStructTag) instead of substring.
Extract the OTW/module name derivation into a single exported helper (e.g. `deriveOtwAndModule(symbol)`) used by both patchConstants and deployAndRegisterPasAsset. Add a unit test.

Use a more structural check: parse the type string, require it to start with `0x2::coin::TreasuryCap<` / `0x0000...0002::coin::TreasuryCap<` (normalize) and match the generic arg to the just-published package id + symbol-derived OTW. Or, add an assertion that exactly one of each expected type was found and throw otherwise.

Use a regex / structured type parse that matches exactly `0x2::coin::TreasuryCap<...>`, `0x2::coin_registry::MetadataCap<...>`, `0x2::coin_registry::Currency<...>` and asserts exactly one match per key. Surface an explicit error when matching produces 0 or >1 results.

Derive coinType directly from the effects/type_metadata by finding the TreasuryCap<C> and using C. Alternatively, assert that `moduleName === symbol.toLowerCase()` at the start of `deployAndRegisterPasAsset`, or extract the module name from the patched bytecode rather than from user-supplied fields.

Change `const symbol = tokenValues.symbol.toLowerCase();` to `const symbol = tokenValues.symbol.toUpperCase().replace(/[^A-Z0-9_]/g, "_").toLowerCase();` (mirroring the derivation in `patchConstants.ts`). Also consider centralizing this derivation in a single helper imported from `../deployTokenTemplate/patchConstants` to prevent future drift. Note: the same bug exists in the pre-existing `coin.ts` (phase-1 / out of scope), which is where the pattern was copied from.

Replace substring matching with a structured check: parse the object type via `parseStructTag` (available in `@mysten/sui/utils`) or `structuredType` and compare `.address === '0x2'`, `.module === 'coin'` / `'coin_registry'`, `.name === 'TreasuryCap'` / `'MetadataCap'` / `'Currency'` explicitly. Alternatively, use the same extraction helper as `deployAndRegisterCoin` (phase-1, out of scope) so drift is centralized.

Instead of reconstructing, extract the coin type from `objectTypes[treasuryCapObjectId]` — the type is literally `0x2::coin::TreasuryCap<0xPKG::module::OTW>`, parseable by regex or `@mysten/sui` type utilities. The same pattern already exists for detecting the cap (line 76). Use that as the source of truth.

Prefer parsing the structured `type` property of each object and comparing components (package = `0x2`, module = `coin`, name = `TreasuryCap`) rather than substring-match. The `@mysten/sui/utils` `parseStructTag` helper is the supported API.

Extract from parsed type structure instead of substring matching: use `@mysten/sui/utils::parseStructTag(type)` and match on `address === '0x2' && module === 'coin' && name === 'TreasuryCap'`. More robust and self-documenting.
descriptions: Object ID extraction for TreasuryCap/MetadataCap/Currency uses `t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')`, `t.includes('::coin_registry::MetadataCap<')`, and `t.includes('::coin_registry::Currency<')`. Substring matching on fully-qualified type names is fragile: (a) the framework could rename a module in a future upgrade; (b) a user-defined type could accidentally contain the same substring; (c) the match does not validate the type argument inside the angle brackets matches the deployed coin type. A deploy yielding multiple caps (or a caps-bearing object with a surprising type) can silently pick the wrong object.

The script identifies the TreasuryCap, MetadataCap, and Currency objects with `t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')` and `t.includes('::coin_registry::MetadataCap<')`. This matches by substring on fully-qualified type names. If sui framework ever renames modules or introduces a differently-named inner type, the match will silently fail and the post-condition `if (!treasuryCapObjectId || ...) throw` triggers. Beyond brittleness: the script constructs coinType by splitting symbol with `.replace(/[^A-Z0-9_]/g, "_")` and assuming module name is `symbol.toLowerCase()`. If the Move module name is NOT `symbol.toLowerCase()` (e.g., custom module_name in TokenTemplateValues), the coin type is wrong and downstream calls fail opaquely.

Extraction of `treasuryCapObjectId`, `metadataCapObjectId`, `currencyObjectId` uses `t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')` etc. This is fragile: (a) substring match can yield false positives if future Sui framework renames types or adds wrappers, (b) ordering sensitivity — the first matched object wins, even if multiple capabilities are created.

The deploy result is scanned with `t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')` / `::coin_registry::MetadataCap<` / `::coin_registry::Currency<`. This is fragile: (a) matches any type whose fully-qualified name contains those substrings (unlikely but possible); (b) fails silently if Sui renames/versions coin_registry package; (c) breaks if multiple TreasuryCap objects appear in the same deploy output (the last one wins). The substring match also ignores the package address prefix — matches against any '::coin::TreasuryCap<' regardless of which package defined it (the sui framework one is the intended target, but nothing enforces that).

The coin type is reconstructed as `${coinPackageId}::${symbol}::${otw}` where `otw = tokenValues.symbol.toUpperCase().replace(/[^A-Z0-9_]/g, "_")`. This duplicates the transformation logic in `patchConstants.ts` line 62-63 and relies on them being identical. Any drift (e.g. adding underscore collapsing in one but not the other) yields a coin type string that does not match the on-chain OTW, causing registerPasAsset to fail with a misleading error (bad type argument).

Classifying created objects by `t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')` / `::coin_registry::MetadataCap<` / `::coin_registry::Currency<` is fragile. A well-crafted user-coin name that contains one of these substrings in its generic parameter (e.g. a nested generic like `OuterType<sui::coin::TreasuryCap<Inner>>`) would misclassify. For the Mysten-template bytecode this never happens, but if the template ever emits extra object types or the coin-registry API shape changes, this silently misidentifies objects. Combined with no runtime type validation (e.g. `.startsWith(...)` + exact match), this is a silent-failure risk.

The code identifies TreasuryCap / MetadataCap / Currency from `deployResult.effects?.changedObjects` by string-containment check on the object's type string (e.g. `t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')`). This is brittle: (a) if the framework renames or namespaces these types in a minor release, the script silently returns empty IDs; (b) ambiguous matches (e.g. a user-defined type that contains `::coin::TreasuryCap<` in its name) would be mis-assigned; (c) first-match-wins iteration order depends on effects ordering. A more robust approach parses the generic argument and asserts the package address is `0x2`.

The coinType is reconstructed as `${coinPackageId}::${symbol}::${otw}` where `symbol = tokenValues.symbol.toLowerCase()` and `otw = tokenValues.symbol.toUpperCase().replace(/[^A-Z0-9_]/g, "_")`. This assumes the template's MODULE name matches `symbol.toLowerCase()` (it does for `hadron_bond` / `HBOND`? — actually `symbol` is `HBOND` lowercased to `hbond`, but the module name comes from bytecode patching and may be `moduleName: 'hadron_bond'`). The example invocation passes `{ moduleName: 'hadron_bond', symbol: 'HBOND', ... }` — symbol.lowercase() is `hbond`, which does NOT match `hadron_bond`. This inconsistency will produce an invalid coinType unless the patcher renames the module to match `symbol.toLowerCase()`, which is not obvious from the code here.

`deployAndRegisterPasAsset` computes `const symbol = tokenValues.symbol.toLowerCase();` and uses it verbatim as the module name in the coinType: `${coinPackageId}::${symbol}::${otw}`. Meanwhile `scripts/src/deployTokenTemplate/patchConstants.ts` derives the module name as `otw.toLowerCase()` where `otw = symbol.toUpperCase().replace(/[^A-Z0-9_]/g, "_")`. If `tokenValues.symbol` contains a non-alphanumeric character (hyphen, space, period), the bytecode's module name will have it replaced with `_`, but the coinType constructed in `pas.ts` will contain the original character. The resulting `coinType` will not match the published module, and every downstream call (whitelist, mint, transfer) will fail with `ECoinNotRegistered` or a move-call resolution error.

Object-ID extraction loops `createdObjects`, reads `objectTypes[obj.objectId]`, and uses substring matching: `t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')`, `t.includes('::coin_registry::MetadataCap<')`, `t.includes('::coin_registry::Currency<')`. This is fragile: (a) if the framework ever renames the module from `coin_registry` to something else, the match silently fails and the subsequent `if (!treasuryCapObjectId || ...)` abort fires; (b) if multiple objects of the same type are created (unlikely here but possible if the template is expanded), only the last one wins.

The script reconstructs the coin type from `tokenValues.symbol`: `symbol.toLowerCase()` for module name, `symbol.toUpperCase().replace(/[^A-Z0-9_]/g, '_')` for OTW. This reconstruction relies on `patchConstants` baking the same derivation into the bytecode. If `patchConstants` ever diverges (e.g. truncates symbol at byte boundaries, or normalizes differently), the reconstructed `coinType` will not match the on-chain type, causing `register_pas_asset` to abort or — worse — to register a different T than expected.

Object-ID extraction uses `t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')`, `::coin_registry::MetadataCap<`, `::coin_registry::Currency<`. If a future SDK version changes the fully-qualified type string format (e.g. adds whitespace, reorders, uses `TreasuryCapV2`), the matcher silently fails and the returned `treasuryCapObjectId` remains `''`. The later guard `if (!treasuryCapObjectId || ...)` catches zero-results but not *wrong* results.

Object ID extraction relies on substring matching framework type strings (`t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')`, `t.includes('::coin_registry::MetadataCap<')`, `t.includes('::coin_registry::Currency<')`). If Sui framework ever moves these types to a different module path or renames them (unlikely but possible), the script silently misidentifies objects and the resulting IDs get passed to the wrong arguments in the PTB.
impacts: Wrong IDs extracted from the deploy result under edge cases (multi-cap deploys, future framework renames). Subsequent `registerPasAsset` call silently passes the wrong cap object and aborts on-chain, or — worse — registers the wrong coin.
Fragile ID extraction; module-name mismatch produces wrong coinType and wrong generics, causing all subsequent operations to abort with type-mismatch errors.
Deploys may mis-extract IDs if framework type names drift; currently low risk because phase-1 regulated flow uses the same pattern.

A future change to Sui framework module names or a concurrent framework-level deployment could silently pick a wrong object ID and pass it to register_pas_asset, which would abort with a cryptic error.
Integrator footgun: a symbol containing characters that get replaced differently causes silent misregistration.
If substring match misfires, the wrong object id is fed to `registerPasAsset`, which then aborts with a deep Move error about type mismatch.
Brittle deploy script; a cosmetic framework rename can break PAS token registration with no clear diagnostic.
Deploy script likely produces wrong coinType on realistic inputs, leading to downstream Move-call abort (coin type not registered).

Users who deploy a PAS coin with a symbol like `HBOND-EU`, `FOO 1`, or `MY.COIN` will hit a confusing mismatch error after deployment. Funds are not at risk, but the scripted onboarding flow is broken for any non-alphanumeric symbol.
Moderate fragility. Any framework rename (not unheard of during Sui's evolution) silently breaks PAS onboarding. The error message is also opaque — points at which ID is missing but not why.

Fragile string handling that silently breaks PAS registration for non-ASCII or long symbols. The `coinType` is then threaded into `pasAdmin.registerPasAsset` type arguments; a mismatch aborts the PTB with an opaque Move type error.

Fragile parsing tied to undocumented framework string format. If PAS token template package ever exports its own type with a similar tail (e.g. `MyTreasuryCap<T>`), the matcher could even pick the wrong object.

If framework moves types, `treasuryCapObjectId` etc. end up empty strings and the guard at L81-87 catches it with a thrown error. But if one of the substrings matches by accident (e.g. a future type named `TreasuryCap2`), the wrong object is silently bound.

title: register_transfer_template uses TransferApproval<T>'s address_string — stale on package upgrade
recommendations: Key the template on the current (latest) package address, e.g. use `@hadron` resolved at compile time in the current upgrade, or accept an explicit `package_id: address` parameter so operators can pass the upgraded address. Document clearly the post-upgrade semantics. Confirm with the PAS team whether `set_template_command` against the same address is intentionally idempotent.
Emit an event from register_transfer_template (e.g. PasTemplateRegistered<T> { package_id }) so indexers can track template staleness. Document the upgrade runbook in pas_admin.move.

(1) Document in `pas_admin`'s module doc header the mandatory post-upgrade runbook: `update_transfer_template<T>` for every registered PAS coin. (2) Consider emitting a `PasTemplateAddressRecorded` event with the captured package address so indexers can detect stale templates. (3) Consider `@hadron`/`current_package()` pattern if available to derive dynamically per-call.

Add a compile-time-ish guard: e.g. a `#[test_only]` test that round-trips the template through an SDK-style decoder, or a comment above `approve_transfer` that flags it as template-bound and must-not-rename. Prefer using a single module-level constant with the function name and referring to it from both sites.

Either (a) use type_name::with_original_ids<T>() for the package id (this returns the latest package address if the type is tracked across upgrades — verify against ptb::ext_input, which uses with_original_ids), or (b) accept the package id as an argument to update_transfer_template so operators can explicitly re-target post-upgrade. Add a test that simulates package upgrade + template re-registration.

Align the id form for TransferApproval<T> across all on-chain and documented off-chain paths. Document explicitly in a module doc-comment that template keys and Request approvals use with_defining_ids, and that the ext_input<PAS> namespace uses with_original_ids — so upgrade operators know what to verify.

Add a comment to version.move `migrate` noting: 'the Version object UID MUST NOT change across upgrades — `pas_admin::register_transfer_template` embeds the UID in the off-chain PAS template'. Alternatively, use `object_by_type<Version>()` so the template remains correct even if the UID is swapped.
No action. Keep a test that calls the template end-to-end to catch any future reorder.

Compute the package_id from a source that tracks the latest upgraded package. Options: (a) record the package address explicitly in the Treasury/Namespace at publish/upgrade time and read it here; (b) accept `package_id: String` as a parameter to `update_transfer_template` so the governance-signed operator provides the new address after migrate; (c) write the hadron V1/V2 `approve_transfer` functions to be version-tolerant enough (e.g. allow the function to be called at V1 even after migration if the logic is still semantically identical). Also fix the misleading doc-comment that claims the function is "Useful after package upgrades to point to the new package address."

Document the upgrade runbook prominently (in module docs and in an ops README): after every package upgrade, the operator MUST iterate over all PAS-registered coins and call `update_transfer_template<T>` for each. Alternatively, expose a bulk batch entry-point or add an event on upgrade that enumerates stale templates. Consider also emitting a per-coin event when the template is registered so operators can reconstruct the set.

Document the upgrade procedure prominently (release runbook): after every Hadron package upgrade, an admin with RegisterCoinPermission must call `update_transfer_template<T>` for every registered PAS coin T. Consider exposing a batched helper that re-registers all PAS coins in one transaction, and/or emit an event at upgrade time that lists stale templates. Confirm with upstream PAS team whether Policy versioning can gate on package version.

Document that `update_transfer_template<T>` MUST be re-invoked after any Hadron upgrade that changes `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` semantics. Consider emitting a `TransferTemplateRegistered` event to give off-chain observers a breadcrumb for upgrade coordination. Also note this caveat in README/integration docs.
descriptions: `register_transfer_template` keys the package address for the template using `type_name::with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>().address_string()`. Per the Move stdlib contract (sui-framework move-stdlib `type_name.move` L42-45), `with_defining_ids` returns the 'defining ID (the ID of the package in storage that first introduced the type)'. That is the v1 original publication of Hadron. When Hadron is upgraded to v2, the TransferApproval type still has the v1 defining ID, so calling `update_transfer_template` in v2 writes the SAME address back — giving the appearance of an update without any observable change. The operation is effectively a no-op after upgrade.

`register_transfer_template` resolves `type_name::with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>()` and uses `address_string()` for the package_id in the ptb::move_call command. This binds the template to the **defining package address** at registration time. After a package upgrade, the defining address stays the same (defining IDs are rooted at v1), so the template will continue to dispatch to v1's pas_transfer::approve_transfer. That is likely desired (stable autoresolve binding), but it also means an upgraded pas_transfer logic will NOT be picked up until `update_transfer_template` is called AND — critically — the address_string continues to be the v1 address. Any upgrade to pas_transfer logic is therefore dead-code unless update_transfer_template is invoked after upgrade. Additionally, the template path is not guarded by is_pas_coin.

The template's `package_id` is derived from `type_name::with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>().address_string()` — this resolves to the Hadron package address *at registration time*. After a Hadron package upgrade (which produces a new package ID), the registered template continues to reference the OLD package address. All auto-resolved PAS transfers will route to the old version of `hadron::pas_transfer::approve_transfer`, which may have a stale logic or (if the old package is removed / blocked) fail entirely. `update_transfer_template` is provided to remedy this, but its invocation is a manual operational step.

The template registers the call as `hadron::pas_transfer::approve_transfer<T>` with arguments `[object_by_id(treasury), ext_input<PAS>("request"), object_by_id(version)]`. But the on-chain Move signature is `approve_transfer<T>(treasury: &Treasury, request: &mut Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>>, version: &Version)` — so the expected order is `[treasury, request, version]`. The template passes them in that order, which is correct. However, `pas::templates::set_template_command` stores this command keyed by `TransferApproval<T>`, and the PAS autoresolve client uses it to build the PTB. If the Move signature is ever reordered in a future edit (e.g. to insert a parameter), the stored template silently becomes wrong — the BCS-encoded command is opaque on-chain and no compiler check links them.

register_transfer_template uses type_name::with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>().address_string() to get the package address, which is the CURRENT Hadron package address at the time of registration. After a Hadron package upgrade, the defining-ids address is unchanged (defining-ids is immutable post-publish), but `approve_transfer` will exist in the *new* package version. Clients building PTBs off the template will still call the old package's approve_transfer, which (per version.move's VERSION constant) will become invalid after migrate(), aborting with EInvalidPackageVersion. update_transfer_template re-registers the template, but because with_defining_ids is stable, calling it again from the new package produces the SAME address. The template cannot be updated to point to the new package without changing to with_original_ids or hard-coding the new package address.

In the template command, the package_id is derived from with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>(), while ptb::ext_input<PAS>(...) (upstream ptb.move L270-274) uses with_original_ids on the namespace. The approve_transfer template will be keyed off the PAS template-registration dispatch (which uses with_defining_ids per templates.move L35), but the off-chain resolver may compare or join paths that use original_ids. Inconsistency between defining_ids and original_ids can cause the SDK to fail to resolve ext_input tokens or to dispatch to the wrong module. This is not an immediate bug — upstream templates.move correctly uses with_defining_ids — but the mixed use of defining vs original is fragile across package upgrades.

The PTB template captures `ptb::object_by_id(object::id(version))` which is the Version object id (shared, not versioned). This is stable across `version.migrate` calls because `migrate` mutates the inner `version: u64` field, not the object id. So the template survives upgrades — good. However, if a future upgrade *replaces* the Version object (new UID), the template becomes stale and all autoresolve transfers abort at input-fetch. This is why `update_transfer_template` exists. Worth ensuring the upgrade-path documentation in version.move spells out that the Version UID must never be replaced.

The PTB template registers the arguments to `pas_transfer::approve_transfer<T>` in the order: (1) `object_by_id(treasury)`, (2) `ext_input<PAS>("request")`, (3) `object_by_id(version)`. But the Move function signature in pas_transfer.move line 33-37 is `approve_transfer<T>(treasury: &Treasury, request: &mut Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>>, version: &Version)` — i.e. treasury, request, version. So the order matches (treasury, request, version). Cross-check confirmed: the argument order is consistent. NO BUG — this finding is a refute of a suspected order-mismatch; filing as info so the consolidator can close the concern.

`register_transfer_template` derives the template's `package_id` from `type_name::with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>().address_string()`. Per `std::type_name` (see `/Users/alilloig/workspace/sui/external-crates/move/crates/move-stdlib/sources/type_name.move` L41-43 — "Package IDs that appear in fully qualified type names in the output from this function are defining IDs (the ID of the package in storage that first introduced the type)"), this is ALWAYS the package that first defined `TransferApproval` — i.e. the V1 hadron package. After a hadron upgrade, the autoresolve template still routes `approve_transfer<T>` to V1. V1's `hadron::version::check_is_valid` hardcodes `VERSION = 1` and asserts `self.version == VERSION`; once `version::migrate` bumps the shared `Version` to V2's constant, the V1 entry point aborts, breaking every PAS transfer. Worse, `update_transfer_template` re-runs the SAME defining-id resolution, so calling it does NOT rotate the address — the operator has no on-chain way to fix the template after migrate.

`register_transfer_template<T>` computes `approval_type.address_string()` from `type_name::with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>()`, which is the **current** Hadron package address. It registers a PTB command onchain that points to `hadron::pas_transfer::approve_transfer<T>` under that specific address. After a Hadron package upgrade, the on-chain `TransferApproval<T>` type-name still has the v1 defining address, but clients doing autoresolve through `templates` will keep calling the old package's `approve_transfer`. If the old version is not paused via `version.check_is_valid()`, users continue to transact through the old code path silently; if the old version is invalidated, transfers break until an operator manually calls `update_transfer_template<T>` **for every PAS coin registered**.

`register_transfer_template` constructs the template MoveCall with `package_id = approval_type.address_string()`, which resolves to the Hadron package address *at the time of registration*. After a Hadron package upgrade, the registered template continues to point to the OLD package ID. Upstream SDK autoresolve will happily route transfers to the old package, which either (a) still works because upgrades produce a new package ID but the old one remains live and its `approve_transfer` still holds the correct logic at that pinned version, or (b) breaks if the old package is semantically incompatible or was produced by a buggy version that the upgrade was meant to fix. The code provides `update_transfer_template` to refresh, but this is an operational foot-gun.

`register_transfer_template` computes the package ID via `type_name::with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>().address_string()`. This is the *current* Hadron package ID at registration time. After a Hadron package upgrade, the defining-ids type name still points at the original package (where `TransferApproval` was first defined), which is the intended stable address. However, if a future upgrade introduces a NEW approval witness or renames the entrypoint, the template will autoresolve into the *original* package's `approve_transfer` — not the upgraded one. Consumers who assume template autoresolve 'tracks the latest Hadron upgrade' will be surprised.
impacts: If the team later adds new logic to `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` (e.g., a KYC registry check, rate limiting, new validation) in a package upgrade, the SDK-side template will keep pointing at the v1 package whose logic is unchanged. Sui's PTB executor resolves the package to any on-chain version with a matching function signature, so the NEW logic may still run depending on runtime resolution — but the design invariant 'update_transfer_template can fix a broken template after upgrade' is not satisfied by the current implementation.
Package upgrades to pas_transfer approval logic require a manual follow-up call; easy to forget. Stale templates could leave an institution running old validation logic in perpetuity.
Operational: after package upgrade, transfers silently break or run old logic until an admin calls `update_transfer_template` for every PAS coin. Could be easily missed.

A future edit that reorders `approve_transfer` parameters (e.g. moving `version` before `request`) compiles cleanly but breaks autoresolve in production. Same risk for renaming the function or module.

After a Hadron package upgrade + migrate, all in-flight PAS transfers break until update_transfer_template is called — but update_transfer_template uses the same defining-ids address and cannot target the upgraded package.

After an upgrade, off-chain PTB resolvers may key the template incorrectly if they use with_original_ids while the template is registered under with_defining_ids. Potential autoresolve breakage post-upgrade.
Upgrade footgun only if future devs replace Version's UID.
None — order matches. Filed to document the cross-check outcome.

Permanent DoS of all PAS transfers after any hadron upgrade. The documented recovery path (`update_transfer_template`) is a no-op; operators will discover this only after migration and will have no on-chain remedy short of re-publishing and re-registering the asset.

Post-upgrade operational burden scales linearly with the number of PAS assets. A forgotten coin leaves its autoresolve template pointing at stale code. The only mechanic that limits damage is `version.check_is_valid()` inside `pas_transfer::approve_transfer`, which will cause a hard abort once versions diverge — but the user-visible failure is opaque and per-coin.

Upgrade hygiene gap: after a package upgrade, auto-resolve transfers continue to execute against the old code until an admin explicitly calls `update_transfer_template`. In the worst case, a bugfix upgrade fails to take effect for existing registered coins.

After upgrade, SDK autoresolve may execute the *old* `approve_transfer` logic (which still works because original-id calls are routed to the latest version by the loader, but this is opaque to reviewers). If the original module is ever disabled via `version` gate, autoresolve breaks silently.

title: ClawbackApproval witness is public struct — any caller with RedeemPermission can auto-approve any clawback Request
recommendations: Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin)` at the top of `pas_supply::mint`, `burn`, and `seize`. Mirrors the `EPasCoinNotAllowed` guard in `supply::mint/redeem` and makes the error path symmetric.

Remove `treasury.assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>()` from `seize`. Seize should be governed solely by the `SeizePermission` role and the PAS Policy's clawback approval — not by the app-layer mint/redeem pause. If a distinct pause-for-seize control is desired, introduce a separate `SeizePausedKey<T>` rather than reusing mint/redeem pause.
Either (a) pass `version: &Version` to mint/burn/seize and check at the top, or (b) document the reliance on auth-side version check explicitly in a module-level doc comment.

Document explicitly in the module header that SeizePermission is unrestricted with respect to source. Consider emitting which policy-driven justification the seize is operating under (e.g., a bytes32 reason code in PasSeized), for audit trails.

Either (a) add `version: &Version` parameter and explicit `version.check_is_valid()` at top of `mint`/`burn`/`seize` for consistency with `pas_transfer`, or (b) document the implicit invariant in the module header.

Add at minimum a round-trip unit test that registers a PAS asset and then burns via `pas_supply::burn`, asserting resolve succeeds. Include a test for `mint<T>` into an unwhitelisted account asserting the expected `ENotWhitelistedReceive` abort.

Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), EPasCoinRequired);` as the first check in `burn` and `seize` (mirroring the guard added to `supply::mint`/`supply::redeem`). Cheap check, tightens the authorization boundary.

Add `pas_supply_mint_tests.move` with at least one test per abort branch plus the happy path, using `treasury::create_for_testing` and a mocked Account. Verify the `PasMinted<T>` event is emitted with the right recipient and amount.

Clarify the intended semantics. Options: (1) require the source address to be whitelisted to send (so de-whitelisted addresses must go through `seize`, preserving audit trail); (2) require a user-provided `pas::account::Auth` to gate user-requested redemptions, with a separate `force_burn` for admin clawbacks that carries its own permission; (3) if intentional, document the authority explicitly in the module doc and event.

Add tests using `sui::test_scenario` that exercise the full PAS flow (create namespace + treasury + PAS coin → mint → whitelist → approve_transfer → burn → seize) against the upstream PAS package pinned in Move.lock.

Add #[test] coverage (with test_only upstream stubs) that executes mint → balance check, burn (full clawback path), seize (clawback + redeposit), with amount-zero aborts and paused-state aborts. Additionally snapshot-test the type-name strings for `ClawbackApproval<T>` and `TransferApproval<T>` so rename regressions are loud.

Document in the `pas_supply::mint` function comment that `account.owner()` may be either a wallet address or `object::id(&owning_object).to_address()`, and point to the PAS `account::new_auth_as_object` API. Optional: expose a helper in `pas_admin` to compute the address-to-whitelist from an object ID.

Write Move unit tests that construct a test Treasury + test PAS Namespace, register a PAS asset, whitelist test addresses, then exercise each branch of `mint`, `burn`, and `seize`. Where upstream PAS provides `#[test_only]` helpers, use them; otherwise, add local `#[test_only]` wrappers that mint test Requests.
Document in module-level comment that `seize` deliberately does NOT whitelist-check the source, enabling extraction from de-whitelisted addresses.

Introduce a dedicated `PasBurnPermission` (or reuse `SeizePermission` since it is the same clawback mechanic) and require it in `pas_supply::burn`. Align with the spec's treatment of seize as a distinct compliance permission.

(1) Document in the module header that RedeemPermission for PAS is issuer-initiated destruction (clawback-burn), not user-redeem — and thus requires stricter role-assignment than a regulated-coin redeem. Consider renaming `burn` to `clawback_burn` to match upstream terminology. (2) Emit the initiator address in PasBurned (currently only emits `from` and `amount`). (3) Consider whether the caller should need both RedeemPermission AND the source `from` to be whitelisted (or at least emit an event with the source's whitelist status at time of burn).

Option A: leave as-is but document that only pas_supply may construct ClawbackApproval<T>. Option B: mirror pas_transfer's pattern — make ClawbackApproval construction require an `internal::Permit<ClawbackApproval<T>>` that only pas_supply can issue, so any other module trying to stamp clawback requests is blocked at compile time. Option B aligns with upstream examples (kyc_registry uses permit for TransferApproval).
descriptions: `pas_supply::mint<T>`, `::burn<T>`, and `::seize<T>` rely on `&Auth<_, T>` as the caller-side gate, but `auth::new_auth` only asserts `treasury.is_coin_registered<T>()` — true for BOTH regulated coins (registered via `admin::register_coin`) and PAS coins. A permission-holder for MintPermission/RedeemPermission/SeizePermission on a regulated (non-PAS) coin can call `pas_supply::mint<T>` and will only abort deep inside `assert_can_receive<T>` (dynamic_object_field::borrow) because the whitelist DOF does not exist. `burn` and `seize` additionally require a `Policy<Balance<T>>` which is only constructible for PAS-registered T, so the non-PAS path for those two aborts at Request construction time — but the error surface is opaque.

`seize` calls `treasury.assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>()`, meaning a compliance team cannot seize funds while mint/redeem is paused. The standard operational intent is the opposite: pausing mint/redeem during an incident SHOULD NOT lock out the compliance team's seize capability — if anything, pausing is often accompanied by seizure to contain damage. The `SeizePermission` is separate from MintPermission/RedeemPermission for exactly this reason, but gating seize on the mint/redeem pause flag collapses the two concerns.

pas_supply::mint never calls `version.check_is_valid()`. It relies on the version check inside `auth::new_auth` when `Auth<MintPermission, T>` was minted. Since `Auth` has `drop`, re-use in the same PTB is not a direct threat, but the pattern is inconsistent with pas_transfer::approve_transfer which explicitly re-checks version. If auth construction were ever refactored or if a future non-auth path was added, mint would silently bypass version gating. The same concern applies to burn and seize, neither of which receives a Version.

Per design (spec §7.2), `seize` intentionally does not assert `assert_can_send<T>(from)` — seize is how a regulator extracts funds from a de-whitelisted address. It does require `assert_can_receive<T>(to.owner())`. Correct and documented. However, there is no event or assertion that the source address is actually deregistered from or present in the whitelist — so seize can be applied to an arbitrary (non-whitelisted, never-whitelisted, or currently-whitelisted) address indiscriminately. This gives a SeizePermission holder the ability to clawback funds from any PAS account, not only from flagged / de-whitelisted addresses.

None of the three `pas_supply` entrypoints (`mint`, `burn`, `seize`) takes or checks a `&Version`. They rely on (a) Auth created via `auth::new_auth` having checked version at creation time, and (b) upstream pas policy resolve functions (`clawback_funds::resolve`) asserting via `policy.versioning().assert_is_valid_version()`. Both hold today, but this is a coupled invariant — if `new_auth`'s version check was removed, `mint` would run version-blind. `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` does take and check `&Version` explicitly; the asymmetry is a code smell.

Compliance-critical paths in `pas_supply` have no coverage. In particular: the ClawbackApproval witness type is constructed in-module but its `type_name::with_defining_ids` identity must exactly match what `pas_admin::register_pas_asset` registered via `policy.set_required_approval<_, ClawbackApproval<T>>`. A change to the module path or struct layout would silently break `clawback_funds::resolve` at runtime.

Unlike mint (which indirectly checks via `assert_can_receive<T>`, whose DOF borrow aborts if T is not PAS-registered), burn does not check `treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()` or read any PAS-specific DOF before calling `treasury.treasury_cap_mut<T>()`. If T is registered as a regulated coin (not a PAS coin) and the caller has RedeemPermission for T plus a Request<ClawbackFunds<Balance<T>>> from an upstream policy, the function would attempt to burn via the TreasuryCap stored under the regulated registration. Today PAS policy registration prevents forging such a request in practice, but the safety of burn should not depend on invariants held by external code.

The mint path has four abort modes (amount=0, mint/redeem paused, receiver not whitelisted, coin not registered as PAS) and a happy path (balance deposited, event emitted). None is covered by in-repo tests.

`burn` accepts a `Request<ClawbackFunds<Balance<T>>>` created by `pas::account::clawback_balance` (upstream `account.move` L99-106 — "This takes no Auth, as it's an admin action"). Any RedeemPermission holder can consume such a Request, approving `ClawbackApproval<T>` and burning the balance. There is no whitelist/ownership check on `from` and no separate permission distinguishing "user-initiated redemption" from "admin-initiated forced burn". The spec says `burn` "checks: not paused, amount > 0" — which matches the code but also matches what would be called a forced clawback-and-burn elsewhere. In practice this means a single RedeemPermission role can wipe any holder's balance without their co-signature.

Zero `#[test]` functions in `pas_supply.move`. The ClawbackApproval witness emission, amount>0 guards, and `assert_mint_redeem_enabled` gating on each entry point are untested. No test validates that `ClawbackApproval<T>` (defined here) matches the approval type registered in `pas_admin::register_pas_asset` via `policy.set_required_approval<_, ClawbackApproval<T>>`.

`pas_supply::mint/burn/seize` all touch the PAS Account API (`deposit_balance`, `Request` approval + resolve) and the Hadron treasury cap. None are tested. `ClawbackApproval<T>` is constructed on the fly and consumed by `request.approve`; a regression in its type-name (e.g. renaming the struct or moving the module) would break clawback silently for all existing PAS coins, since PAS policy keys approvals by `type_name::with_defining_ids`.

`pas_supply::mint` calls `treasury.assert_can_receive<T>(account.owner())`. Upstream `pas::account::Account::owner()` can be a wallet address OR an object's UID-derived address (via `account::new_auth_as_object` / `uid.to_inner().to_address()`). This means whitelisting a contract-owned account requires the operator to know the synthetic address derived from the object's UID — a non-obvious calculation.

No tests exist for any of the three value-moving functions in `pas_supply.move`: `mint` (deposits balance into a PAS Account), `burn` (consumes a ClawbackFunds request and destroys balance), `seize` (reroutes balance between accounts). Each function has multiple guard branches (`amount > 0`, pause, whitelist receive) and integrates directly with the upstream PAS `Account`, `Request`, and `clawback_funds::resolve` surfaces. Any upstream signature drift will silently break runtime and be caught only at mainnet.

`seize` extracts from `request.data().owner()` (the source) but does not assert the source was EVER whitelisted for T. This is intentional per spec (seize must work against de-whitelisted addresses — e.g. revoked wallets), but means seize power extends to addresses that were *never* onboarded. Combined with the upstream constraint that only account holders can originate a ClawbackFunds request — actually, per `pas::account::clawback_balance` (`/Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/account.move` L99-106), clawback takes no Auth and can be initiated by anyone given `&mut Account`. That raises an orthogonal question about upstream seize authority — but that is upstream behaviour, not Hadron's responsibility.

`burn` is authorised by `Auth<RedeemPermission, T>`. Semantically the operation is a clawback-driven burn (funds are extracted via `ClawbackFunds`, not returned by the holder). Reusing RedeemPermission conflates the two: a role with `RedeemPermission` is intended for user-redemption flows (where a holder sends coin back to the treasury via `supply::redeem`), but for PAS it now also grants clawback-burn power without the holder's action. This is a silent privilege escalation when migrating roles between regulated and PAS assets.

Upstream `account::clawback_balance<C>` (account.move L99-106) takes NO auth — anyone can initiate a clawback Request against any account. The Request cannot be dropped (no `drop` ability), so the PTB must resolve it. Hadron's `burn` accepts any such Request as long as the caller also has `Auth<RedeemPermission, T>`. This means: a Hadron-issued RedeemPermission holder can construct a PTB that calls `account::clawback_balance` on ANY account (including unfriendly users) and then `pas_supply::burn` to destroy those funds. There is no check that the source `from` address in the Request was authorized or is even whitelisted. The design intent (from the orchestrator LEADS) is that burn is an admin operation and the redeem-holder is trusted. Still, this reads as "RedeemPermission = burn-any-user's-balance", which may surprise operators who expect redeem-style symmetric semantics (user-initiated).

`ClawbackApproval<phantom T>() has drop;` is declared `public struct`. While the witness can only be constructed inside `pas_supply` (Move privacy on the constructor `ClawbackApproval<T>()`), making it `public struct` means any module in the package could add the same constructor syntax if they share the package. More importantly, the permit pattern used for `TransferApproval` (via `internal::permit<TransferApproval<T>>()` in pas_transfer) is NOT used for `ClawbackApproval`. This asymmetry is subtle.
impacts: Opaque abort rather than a clean error message. A legitimate admin accidentally targeting the wrong flow (regulated vs PAS) has no precise diagnostic. Not exploitable (no balance or authorization bypass), but clutters monitoring/debug and mirrors the guard added in `supply::mint/redeem` asymmetrically.

During an incident response, pausing mint/redeem also freezes seize. The incident response playbook has to unpause → seize → repause, which is both a race-condition window (an attacker can move funds in the unpause window) and an accounting mess.
Weak defense-in-depth for emergency pause / version-block. Not critical because auth construction currently checks version.
Designed behavior, but regulatorily sensitive: seize is unrestricted; a compromised SeizePermission holder can move funds to any whitelisted receiver.
Low — defense-in-depth gap, not an exploit today.
Rename/restructure of `ClawbackApproval` would pass Move compilation but fail PAS resolve in production.
Defensive gap. If upstream policy or account invariants change, burn could be used as a bypass to burn a regulated (non-PAS) coin via the PAS clawback path.
Silent regression risk on primary mint path.

Design ambiguity. If the intent is "user redeemed off-chain, admin burns on-chain," then an additional off-chain attestation / on-chain user signature is assumed. If the intent is forced burn, this is a very strong authority with no audit trail distinguishing it from `seize`.
Possible silent type-name mismatch between `set_required_approval` and `request.approve` would only surface at first real clawback in production. Integration-boundary contract is entirely unverified.

Breaking changes to the `ClawbackApproval` / `TransferApproval` witness names or their defining module will not be caught at compile time — only at first clawback or transfer. Because upstream `Request::approve` uses `VecSet.insert`, duplicate approvals abort; this is also untested.
Documentation / ergonomics. If compliance operators assume `account.owner()` is always a wallet, they may fail to whitelist object-owned accounts and mint will abort.
Zero coverage on the most impactful functions in the PR. Value-moving paths (mint/burn/seize) deserve the strongest automated assurance.
Design intent; noted as info per shared-context LEAD#9. No behavioural change requested.

A role designed for a regulated coin's redeem window inherits destructive clawback rights when the same treasury later registers a PAS asset with the same type parameter in-scope. Separation-of-duties is weakened.

A compromised or malicious redeem role can erase any holder's PAS balance without their consent. This is likely the intended compliance behavior (an issuer-initiated destruction) but the naming `redeem` and the lack of event detail (only `from`/`amount` is emitted; no initiator address) muddy the audit trail.

Within-package it's fine — only pas_supply constructs the witness. But if a future developer adds a module that stamps `ClawbackApproval<T>()` (perhaps as a quick fix), the witness is leaked without a centralized review point. The transfer path is protected by internal::Permit; clawback is not.

title: update_transfer_template<T> lets any institution overwrite another institution's template
recommendations: In `treasury::add_to_whitelist/remove_from_whitelist/set_whitelist_send/set_whitelist_receive`, add `assert!(is_pas_coin<T>(treasury), ENotPasCoin)` as the first line before the DOF borrow.

Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin);` at the top of each whitelist mutator in pas_admin (or — cleaner — in the treasury::add_to_whitelist / remove / set_send / set_receive functions).
Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin);` at the top of update_transfer_template.

In each whitelist mutator in `pas_admin` (or at top of the matching `treasury.add_to_whitelist<T>` / `remove_from_whitelist<T>` / `set_whitelist_*<T>` functions) add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin);` before mutating the DOF.

Add a treasury.is_pas_coin<T>() assertion at the top of add_to_whitelist / remove_from_whitelist / set_whitelist_send / set_whitelist_receive in treasury.move (public(package) side), or add it at the pas_admin entrypoints with the existing ENotPasCoin. Consider specializing the Auth constructor with an is_pas_coin check for PAS-specific permissions.
Store the blessed PAS namespace ID inside the Hadron Treasury (or Namespace) at registration time, and assert match in register_pas_asset. Cheap and defensive against upstream changes.

In `update_transfer_template<T>` (and `register_transfer_template` / `register_pas_asset`), assert `treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()` before writing the template. This ensures only the institution that originally registered T can update its template.

Change whitelist admin functions to take `&TreasuryAuth<WhitelistManagePermission>` and call `auth.assert_is_valid_for_treasury(treasury.treasury_id())` — matching the pattern used by `register_pas_asset` and `update_transfer_template`. If the current coin-scoped shape is intentional, add an explicit assertion that the auth's embedded identity is allowed to operate on this treasury (e.g. re-check `treasury.roles().assert_has_permission<WhitelistManagePermission>(auth.addr())`).
At the top of each treasury-side whitelist mutator, add `assert!(is_pas_coin<T>(treasury), ENotPasCoin);` mirroring the guard already present in `policy_cap<T>` (line 273).
descriptions: The whitelist mutators gate on `&Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>`, whose construction (auth::new_auth) only requires `treasury.is_coin_registered<T>()`. A non-PAS coin that is also registered via `admin::register_coin` would allow a WhitelistManagePermission holder to obtain an Auth for T; the subsequent `treasury.add_to_whitelist<T>` aborts opaquely inside `dynamic_object_field::borrow_mut` because the whitelist DOF does not exist for a non-PAS T.

`Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>` is built via `auth::new_auth<P, T>`, which only asserts `treasury.is_coin_registered<T>()`, not `treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()`. A coin registered via `admin::register_coin` (regulated, not PAS) would satisfy is_coin_registered, allowing an address with WhitelistManagePermission (on paper for PAS) to mint an `Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, RegulatedCoin>` and call `add_to_whitelist`. This then aborts deep inside `dynamic_object_field::borrow_mut` at `treasury::add_to_whitelist` because the whitelist DOF was never created for that coin — opaque abort rather than a clean error.

update_transfer_template<T> takes `&Treasury` (immutable) and re-registers the template for type T without asserting that T is a PAS coin in this treasury. A caller with RegisterCoinPermission could invoke update_transfer_template<SomeRandomT> and it would still succeed (it only writes to the Templates registry, keyed by TransferApproval<T>). This is unlikely to be harmful because Templates is a PAS-level registry and the worst outcome is a stale template entry for a type that doesn't exist on-chain, but it's silent misconfiguration.

`add_to_whitelist`, `remove_from_whitelist`, `set_whitelist_send`, `set_whitelist_receive` all take `&Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>`. `auth::new_auth<P, T>` only asserts `is_coin_registered<T>()` (TreasuryCapKey exists), not `is_pas_coin<T>()` (PolicyCapKey exists). A regulated (non-PAS) coin passes the auth check; the call then reaches `treasury.add_to_whitelist<T>` which `dynamic_object_field::borrow_mut` on a missing WhitelistKey and aborts with a generic DOF error rather than a clean `ENotPasCoin`.

add_to_whitelist / remove_from_whitelist / set_whitelist_send / set_whitelist_receive require an Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>. In hadron::auth::new_auth, the check is assert!(treasury.is_coin_registered<T>()) — this is true for both PAS coins and regulated (non-PAS) coins. If a non-PAS coin T is registered via admin::register_coin, a holder of WhitelistManagePermission can successfully construct Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T> and then call add_to_whitelist<T>, which aborts deep inside dynamic_object_field::borrow_mut (whitelist DOF absent) with an opaque framework error rather than the intended ENotPasCoin.

`register_pas_asset` takes `pas_namespace: &mut Namespace` as a parameter. Upstream PAS has exactly ONE global Namespace per deployment (see /Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/namespace.move L24-27 'singleton'), so there is effectively only one to pass. However, nothing in Hadron asserts this — a malicious caller with a custom test namespace (created via `namespace::create_for_testing`) could attempt to register assets against a fake namespace, potentially separating accounts (which are derived from the fake namespace) from the real whitelist (which is keyed by wallet address). The subsequent `policy.share()` would publish a policy object derived from the fake namespace. This only matters in a test context today.

`update_transfer_template<T>` only checks that the caller's `TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>` is valid for the caller's own `treasury`. It does NOT assert that `treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()` — i.e. that coin T was actually registered in THIS treasury. Meanwhile the `Templates` object is a singleton shared across all hadron institutions (keyed by `type_name::with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>()`, which is coin-independent-of-treasury). Consequence: Institution B — with any RegisterCoinPermission — can call `update_transfer_template<Foo>` for Institution A's coin `Foo`, and the new template will call `approve_transfer<Foo>(B_treasury, ..., version)`. Since B's treasury has no `WhitelistKey<Foo>` DOF, every legitimate transfer of Foo will abort inside `assert_can_send<Foo>` / `dynamic_object_field::borrow`.

`add_to_whitelist<T>`, `remove_from_whitelist<T>`, `set_whitelist_send<T>` and `set_whitelist_receive<T>` authorize with `Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>`. `Auth<P, T>` in `auth.move` has no `treasury_id` binding (only `addr`) — `new_auth<P,T>` creates the proof after checking the caller's permission in the *creating* treasury, but the resulting proof can then be consumed by **any** `Treasury` in the same PTB that also has T registered as a PAS asset. An attacker with WhitelistManagePermission on one Hadron Treasury can mint an `Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>` and then drive whitelist mutations on a different Treasury that happens to have registered the same PAS coin type T. For coin types that are unique per institution this is unlikely, but nothing in the type system or the registration flow prevents two Treasuries from registering the same `T` (they live in separate Namespaces and derive separate Policy objects — but Hadron's `add_pas_coin_caps` does not cross-check).

The four whitelist mutators (`add_to_whitelist`, `remove_from_whitelist`, `set_whitelist_send`, `set_whitelist_receive`) gate on `Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>`, which only asserts `is_coin_registered<T>()` inside `auth::new_auth`. A holder of `WhitelistManagePermission` can construct an Auth for a *regular* (non-PAS) coin and call these mutators; the call then proceeds into `treasury::add_to_whitelist<T>` which `dynamic_object_field::borrow_mut`s a non-existent DOF, aborting with a framework error, not a Hadron error.
impacts: Poor error surface. A compliance officer operating on the wrong coin type gets an uninformative framework abort instead of `ENotPasCoin`.

Opaque aborts on misuse; no explicit check that the coin is actually a PAS coin when managing whitelist. Adversary cannot subvert compliance, but DX is poor and the guard is a latent DOF-borrow error.
Operators can accidentally register templates for non-PAS types, polluting the Templates registry. No security impact because downstream validation still happens in approve_transfer.
Opaque abort instead of a clean error. Operationally annoying; not a security bypass (the final DOF borrow does abort), but makes debugging harder and signals a missing invariant check.
Ambiguous error surface, and the Auth/phantom-T contract does not express the real precondition. Adds friction and foot-guns for institutions that mix PAS and regulated coins.
