Mostly theoretical because mainnet/testnet PAS has only one Namespace and it's not user-creatable. But there is no Hadron-side bond between 'the' PAS namespace and this Hadron deployment, so a future PAS feature that allows user-created namespaces would silently break the whitelist guarantee.

Any institution administrator in the hadron deployment can grief any other institution's PAS transfers. This is a cross-institution authorization-boundary break: per the Hadron Blueprint, institutions should be isolated.

Cross-treasury privilege escalation on whitelist state; an admin of treasury A could manipulate treasury B's whitelist for the same coin type. Same class of concern affects phase-1 `Auth<P,T>` too, but the PAS whitelist additions broaden the attack surface to compliance-sensitive state.

Confusing error surface and a defence-in-depth gap: the type parameter T is not proven to be a PAS coin. While the actual state change is blocked, the error message is unhelpful and wastes gas. If the DOF layout ever changes (e.g. PolicyCap is added to `add_coin_caps` for some reason), the guard collapses.

title: deployPasTokenTemplate passes 0x1 and 0x2 only — relies on implicit framework-dep resolution
recommendations: Extract the dependency list from the bytecode itself (parse the `ModuleHandle` entries) and assert the set equals `{0x1, 0x2}`. Or use `@mysten/sui`'s higher-level publish helper that inspects the bytecode's declared imports.

Confirm the full dependency list by running a dry-run publish against the target network in CI, or explicitly enumerate the deps by walking the bytecode's module handles and including each unique address.

Either rebuild bytecode from source and confirm the deps list, or add a comment clarifying why only 0x1/0x2 are needed. For defense-in-depth, include `normalizeSuiObjectId("0xc")` if upstream Sui framework resolves `new_currency_with_otw` against the registry anywhere in its call chain.

Replace '0x1' / '0x2' / '0xc' string literals with named constants from @mysten/sui/utils (SUI_FRAMEWORK_ADDRESS, MOVE_STDLIB_ADDRESS). Pull CoinRegistry shared-object ID from the network-specific deploy output, not a hardcoded 0xc.

Either add `normalizeSuiObjectId('0xc')` (and possibly `0xa` for AccumulatorRoot if used) to the dependencies array, or explicitly document (via a comment) that only 0x1/0x2 are needed because coin_registry is reachable transitively and 0xc is accessed at runtime. Run a publish against testnet to confirm before merge.

Source the CoinRegistry address from `@mysten/sui` constants (or from `SUI_CLOCK_OBJECT_ID`-style framework exports) rather than hardcoding `'0xc'`. Add a code comment referencing the framework doc at `references/sui-framework-reference.mdx`.

Confirm that `0x1` and `0x2` are sufficient by running the publish on testnet. If desired, add a comment explaining that `sui::coin_registry` resolves via `0x2` and that no additional system-package dep is required. Consider regenerating the dependency list from the compiled module rather than hardcoding it.

Either (a) make `patchConstants` actually use `tokenTemplateValues.moduleName` (with appropriate sanitization) as the module-name identifier, or (b) remove `moduleName` from `TokenTemplateValues` entirely and document that the module name is derived from the symbol.
descriptions: `tx.publish` declares dependencies `[0x1, 0x2]` (MoveStdlib, Sui framework). `coin_registry` lives at `0x2`, so that is implicitly covered. However the upstream `sui::coin_registry` module also imports `sui::derived_object` and `sui::transfer` (both in 0x2) and the template code imports only `sui::coin` and `sui::coin_registry` — so 0x1+0x2 appears sufficient. But this inventory is fragile: if the token_template source later uses `sui::clock` (which is fine, still in 0x2) or any address-based stdlib, 0x1+0x2 still work; but if it adds `sui::zklogin_verified_id` or any new framework package, a new dep entry would be needed. There is no assertion that the declared deps cover the bytecode's actual imports.

The publish dependencies list in deployPasTokenTemplate.ts is `[0x1, 0x2]`. The Move source uses `sui::coin_registry::{Self, MetadataCap}` and `sui::coin::TreasuryCap`. coin_registry lives at 0x2 (sui framework) in current sui framework, so the listed deps may be sufficient. However, many template examples include 0xa (accumulator) / 0xc (coin_registry object ID, not package ID — this is where confusion lies). Verifying this once via a devnet publish dry-run is necessary.

The bytecode publish lists only `0x1` (std) and `0x2` (sui framework). But `token_template.move` calls `sui::coin_registry::new_currency_with_otw` which returns a `CurrencyInitializer` that must later be finalized via `coin_registry::finalize_registration(&mut CoinRegistry /* 0xc */, ...)`. The publish itself may not need `0xc` (because `new_currency_with_otw` creates the Currency object without touching the registry object in init), but confirm this matches what `sui move build` of `token_template_pas/Move.toml` declares. More importantly, the `deployAndRegisterPasAsset` TS flow does call `coin_registry::finalize_registration` with `0xc` explicitly — so the registry object is needed at runtime but not at publish-time. Verify that the bytecode's `init` does not reach 0xc; if it does, the publish will fail unresolved at runtime.

tx.publish declares dependencies: [normalizeSuiObjectId('0x1'), normalizeSuiObjectId('0x2')]. But token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move uses sui::coin_registry::new_currency_with_otw and MetadataCap — which live at 0x2 (sui-framework). The module itself is sui::coin_registry so 0x2 is correct. However, the new-style coin_registry requires the CoinRegistry shared object at 0xc to be passed at call-time (see full-setup scripts: COIN_REGISTRY = '0xc' is invoked separately in finalize_registration). The publish command itself may not need 0xc, but the deploy flow's deployAndRegisterPasAsset does reference 0xc hard-coded — which is a mainnet/testnet shared-object ID that can change. Confirm with the coin_registry upstream. Separately: normalizeSuiObjectId('0x1')/'0x2' is brittle; prefer SUI_FRAMEWORK_ADDRESS / MOVE_STDLIB_ADDRESS constants from @mysten/sui/utils for clarity and to catch typos at lint time.

The publish command passes only `0x1` (MoveStdlib) and `0x2` (Sui framework) as dependencies. The template calls `sui::coin_registry::new_currency_with_otw` which lives in the Sui framework (so covered by 0x2), but `finalize_registration` reads the shared CoinRegistry at `0xc`. The Sui framework may or may not require `0xc` as a publish-time dep. The regulated `deployTokenTemplate` (in phase 1 — out of scope for findings but readable for reference) likewise doesn't include 0xc. Worth confirming once more with the compiler since the bytecode is hard-coded.

`tx.publish({ modules: [...], dependencies: [normalizeSuiObjectId('0x1'), normalizeSuiObjectId('0x2')] })` declares only `0x1` (std) and `0x2` (sui-framework) as publish-time deps. `sui::coin_registry` lives inside `0x2` so that is actually correct at module-dep level; however, the subsequent `finalize_registration` call in `pas.ts` passes `COIN_REGISTRY = '0xc'` as a shared object. This is a runtime-arg object (shared registry), not a package-level dep, so not strictly wrong — but the hard-coded `'0xc'` is opaque and should probably come from the framework SDK rather than be hand-written.

`tx.publish` declares only `0x1` and `0x2` as dependencies. The `token_template_pas` module imports `sui::coin_registry`, which lives inside the `sui` framework package (`0x2`). This is fine today, but `sui::coin_registry` is a relatively new framework module — if a specific framework version is required, it is not enforced here.

The `TokenTemplateValues` type (imported from `../deployTokenTemplate/patchConstants`) includes a `moduleName: string` field, but `patchConstants.ts` derives the module name from `otw.toLowerCase()` — it never reads `tokenTemplateValues.moduleName`. The `moduleName: 'hadron_bond'` passed in `full-setup.ts` is silently ignored.
impacts: Brittle publish-time dependency list. A future edit to the token_template source that adds an import from a new framework package will fail to publish with an obscure 'Missing dependency' error. Also, the Sui framework `0x2` uses address-level ABI versioning that could change across runtime versions.
If a newer sui framework splits coin_registry out of 0x2 or requires additional published modules as deps, the publish call will abort with a dependency-missing error at runtime.
If 0xc is reached by init, publish aborts with missing dependency. Even if not, the dep list should match the source-level Move.toml for reviewer transparency.

If the bytecode actually references coin_registry symbols defined in 0x2 at publish time, it works; but any shift to another package address or new dep at publish time silently breaks. Hardcoded 0xc is a cross-network assumption (testnet, mainnet, devnet all use 0xc for CoinRegistry? — verify).
If Sui's publish dependency graph requires 0xc when using coin_registry APIs, the publish will fail. If not, currently fine. LEADS §16 surfaces this too — needs to be confirmed on a testnet publish.
Maintainability. If the shared registry address ever changes (unlikely), the scripts silently break.

Very low. Publishing should still succeed because `coin_registry` is part of `0x2`. The risk is purely forward-compatibility: if `coin_registry` is ever extracted to its own system package (e.g., `0xa`), this list would need updating.
Confusing API. A maintainer reading the call-site assumes `moduleName` is honored; it is not. The runtime module name is always `<symbol.lowercased>`.

title: No tests for pas_transfer whitelist / pause / version logic
recommendations: Add branch-covering unit tests for `approve_transfer<T>`: (a) both whitelisted — OK; (b) sender not whitelisted — abort `ENotWhitelistedSend`; (c) recipient not whitelisted — abort `ENotWhitelistedReceive`; (d) mint/redeem paused — abort; (e) amount = 0 — abort; (f) version blocked — abort.

Add unit tests for all guard combinations: (1) both whitelisted + unpaused + amount > 0 ⇒ emits PasTransferApproved and sets approval, (2) sender lacks can_send ⇒ aborts ENotWhitelistedSend, (3) recipient lacks can_receive ⇒ aborts ENotWhitelistedReceive, (4) mint/redeem paused ⇒ aborts EMintRedeemPaused, (5) amount zero ⇒ aborts ETransferAmountZero, (6) version invalid ⇒ aborts at check_is_valid, (7) non-PAS coin treasury ⇒ opaque framework abort (becomes a clean abort after R8-004).
descriptions: `approve_transfer` implements the permissionless autoresolve entrypoint with whitelist, pause and version checks. There are no tests exercising any branch. Given this is the only code path that actually approves third-party PAS transfers, it is the highest-value testing target in the PR.

`approve_transfer` is the permissionless autoresolve hook — the one surface an external SDK will call on every PAS transfer. Its correctness depends on the ordering of whitelist, pause, amount-zero, version, and approval checks; any regression in this file changes the observable behaviour of live transfers. Yet there are no unit tests covering sender-only-whitelisted, recipient-only-whitelisted, paused-state, post-version-bump behaviour, zero-amount, or the order of checks.
impacts: Regressions in any branch (pause conflation, whitelist-send vs whitelist-receive mix-up, amount zero, version) would ship unnoticed.
Zero coverage on the single most-called PAS function. Any regression ships to mainnet undetected.

title: Mint/redeem pause also disables PAS transfers — spec ambiguity between two distinct kill switches
recommendations: Introduce a separate `TransferPausedKey<T>` DF and `pause_transfer` / `unpause_transfer` flows (with their own permission types), and gate `approve_transfer` on the new pause flag. Keep the mint/redeem pause orthogonal.

Expand the module-level doc comment to explicitly state: 'approve_transfer is permissionless by design — the compliance gate is the whitelist check, not caller identity. A request can only be approved once per type, so duplicate invocations abort in pas::request.' Link to the PAS docs section 'How clients use templates'.

Either add a separate `TransfersPausedKey<T>` + `PauseTransfersPermission`, or document that pausing mint/redeem also freezes PAS transfers by design. Mention explicitly in the module-level comment of pas_transfer.

Introduce a dedicated `PasTransferPaused<T>` flag (or a combined `assert_transfer_enabled`). Alternatively, keep the conflation but document it prominently in the module header and use an error code like `ETransfersPaused` distinct from mint/redeem semantics.

Introduce a distinct PAS transfer pause (e.g. TransferPausedKey<T> via a new Pause permission) or document explicitly that the mint/redeem pause also pauses transfers and is the intentional policy surface. If it must be unified, rename the pause to something non-operation-specific.
Either introduce a distinct `TransferPause` flag with its own permission, or at minimum rename the guard/error to `ETransfersPaused` and document that mint/redeem pause also pauses transfers.

Add tests covering: (a) sender not whitelisted (abort), (b) recipient not whitelisted (abort), (c) sender with can_send=false (abort), (d) recipient with can_receive=false (abort), (e) amount=0 (abort), (f) paused (abort), (g) happy path (event emitted with right sender/recipient/amount).
No action. Consider adding a comment noting that callers outside the Hadron package cannot use this helper because `internal::permit` is tied to the defining module of the phantom type.

Introduce a distinct per-coin pause state for PAS transfers (or gate `approve_transfer` on a `PasTransferPausedKey<T>` instead of reusing mint-redeem pause). Add matching `PausePasTransferPermission` / `UnpausePasTransferPermission` if granular control is desired.
Write unit + scenario tests for each abort branch listed above. Include a test that flips `can_send` / `can_receive` independently to verify the flags actually gate the right direction.

Either (a) introduce a dedicated `PauseTransferPermission` + per-coin transfer pause state and gate `approve_transfer` on it, decoupling from mint/redeem; or (b) if the coupling is intentional, update the error constant to something like `ETransferPaused` with message `"PAS transfers are paused for this coin"` and document the coupling explicitly in the module-level comment.

Document explicitly in the module-level comment that `approve_transfer` enforces ONLY whitelist + pause, and that rate/time-based controls would need to be added as additional `assert!` rules inside this function (plus a matching template update). Consider whether `WhitelistEntry` should grow fields (daily_cap, holding_period_end) in a V2.

Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ETreasuryNotPasForCoin);` at the top of `approve_transfer`, with a distinct error constant. This enforces that the treasury argument passed into the autoresolve template matches the one that registered T.

Either (a) document the conflation prominently in the module doc and in the PauseMintRedeem operator runbook, or (b) split into a dedicated `PauseTransferPermission` with its own DF key and guards in `approve_transfer`. Option (b) is cleaner and matches the spec's multi-axis pause philosophy.

Introduce a `PauseTransferPermission` / independent `transfer_paused_key<T>()` DOF, or at minimum document that the PAS transfer gate is deliberately coupled to the mint/redeem gate so downstream operators do not assume orthogonality.

Option A (minimal): rename the error to `EMintRedeemOrTransferPaused` and update the string to reflect that pausing mint/redeem also halts PAS transfers; document the conflation in the module header. Option B (cleaner): introduce a `TransferPausedKey<T>` and `PauseTransferPermission` separate from mint/redeem pause so compliance can halt issuance without freezing circulation.
descriptions: `approve_transfer` checks `!treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>()` to gate transfer approvals. This is a design choice: pausing mint/redeem also pauses every PAS transfer between whitelisted accounts. The Notion spec (§7.2) says transfers should be gated on 'not paused' — but it does not specify whether that is the mint/redeem pause or a distinct transfer pause. Treating them as the same pause removes operational flexibility (e.g., pause issuance while allowing existing holders to continue transacting among whitelisted accounts).

`approve_transfer` is `public fun` with no auth proof — anyone holding a `&mut Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>>` can call it. This is intentional per the PAS autoresolve design: the caller already demonstrated ownership of the source funds (they were withdrawn from an Account via `send_balance`), and the approval logic is purely a compliance check. But the permissionless nature is worth an explicit doc note so reviewers don't later assume auth is missing. Additionally: since Hadron emits a `PasTransferApproved<T>` event, any observer can build an event-spam dashboard by repeatedly invoking approve_transfer against the same in-flight Request — duplicate `request.approve` will abort (`VecSet::insert` rejects duplicates, pas/request.move L26), so this is not exploitable.

approve_transfer is invoked as part of PAS autoresolve and is effectively permissionless — anyone with a valid Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>> can call it. It gates on `!is_mint_redeem_paused<T>()`. This conflates three distinct pause concepts: (1) pause mint/redeem only, (2) pause transfers only, (3) emergency halt. The current code uses a single flag for all, so a compliance officer who only wants to stop new issuance (but keep transfers flowing to allow existing holders to exit) cannot do so. Conversely, a temporary transfer freeze would also block mint/redeem. This is a design decision, not a bug, but it should be documented clearly and considered against the permission model.

`approve_transfer` asserts `!treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>()`. This conflates three distinct operations — mint, redeem, transfer — under a single pause flag. The Hadron design document separates application pause (mint/redeem) from protocol pause (`GlobalPausePermission` via DenyCapV2). For PAS coins there is no DenyCapV2 (no regulated compliance), so pausing transfers requires abusing the mint/redeem pause. A treasurer who wants to pause only minting will also block all PAS transfers — which may be unintended.

approve_transfer gates on !treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>() using the EMintRedeemPaused error. This conflates two distinct pause semantics: the Hadron phase-1 design defines PauseMintRedeemPermission as an application-layer pause specifically for mint/redeem (not transfers), with a protocol-layer GlobalPausePermission (DenyCapV2) reserved for a full stop. Reusing the mint/redeem pause flag for transfer approval means compliance officers cannot pause mint/redeem without also freezing settlement flow for all holders, which is operationally counter-intuitive and may contradict spec intent from Notion (`PauseMintRedeemPermission` is scoped to mint/redeem).

approve_transfer aborts with EMintRedeemPaused when `is_mint_redeem_paused<T>()` is true. Operators might wish to pause minting while leaving secondary transfers alive, or vice-versa. By overloading one flag, the system forces a single coarse-grained pause. Also note the error name/message is `EMintRedeemPaused` — which is misleading for a transfer path.

approve_transfer is the permissionless autoresolve entry — arguably the most-called PAS function in production. It has no tests. The upstream PAS tests exist but cannot guarantee the Hadron-specific whitelist semantics here.

`transfer_approval_permit<T>` is `public(package)` and simply returns `internal::permit<TransferApproval<T>>()`. Per upstream docs (see pas::templates.move L30-40), this permit is the proof that the defining module authorizes setting a template for the given approval type. Since `TransferApproval<T>` is defined in this module (`pas_transfer`), the permit is only constructable here — upstream would reject any attempt from another module because `internal::permit<A>()` checks the defining module. The `public(package)` visibility correctly restricts callers to the Hadron package (only `pas_admin::register_transfer_template` uses it). OK.

`approve_transfer` asserts `!treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>()` using the same `MintRedeemPausedKey<T>` flag that `supply::mint` / `supply::redeem` and `pas_supply::mint/burn/seize` gate on. Pausing mint/redeem therefore also pauses all user-to-user PAS transfers. There is no separate "pause transfers" switch, and there is no separate `PausePasTransferPermission`. A compliance officer who wants to stop new issuance while letting existing holders continue to transfer cannot express that intent.

The permissionless autoresolve entrypoint `approve_transfer` is the main compliance surface for user-to-user PAS transfers. It is untested. Critical paths unverified: (a) `ETransferAmountZero` abort; (b) `EMintRedeemPaused` abort; (c) `ENotWhitelistedSend` / `ENotWhitelistedReceive` when sender or recipient are not in whitelist or have their `can_send`/`can_receive` flag cleared; (d) correct `PasTransferApproved` event emission ordering; (e) request-approval type matches `TransferApproval<T>` as registered in policy.

`approve_transfer` gates on `treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>()`. This conflates the mint/redeem pause (application-layer, controlled by `PauseMintRedeemPermission`) with the transfer pause. Per Hadron's design intent (two pause layers, distinct operator controls), an operator who wants to freeze issuance while still allowing existing holders to transact would find that pausing mint/redeem **also silently pauses transfers**, with no dedicated error or event to communicate the coupling.

`pas_transfer::approve_transfer` takes no `Auth` — it is intentionally permissionless (called by the PAS SDK during autoresolve). The only gating is `version`, `amount > 0`, not-paused, and whitelist send/receive checks. There are no per-sender or per-recipient rate limits, no holding-period enforcement, no daily-cap enforcement. The design intent (per upstream docs and Notion spec) is that compliance logic is entirely captured by the whitelist Boolean. If Hadron later needs rate limits or holding periods, they must be added here.

`approve_transfer` is the permissionless auto-resolve entrypoint. It reads the SendFunds data and calls `treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>()`, `treasury.assert_can_send<T>(sender)`, and `treasury.assert_can_receive<T>(recipient)`. None of these cleanly abort with `ENotPasCoin` if `T` was never PAS-registered in this treasury — the whitelist DOF lookup inside `assert_can_send/receive` will fail with a framework abort. While the upstream PAS Policy creation for `T` guarantees a corresponding on-chain Policy exists, nothing prevents an adversary from constructing a different `Treasury` address argument (still shared) and passing it alongside a Request for a coin that IS a PAS asset under upstream Namespace but is NOT a PAS asset under this Hadron Treasury.

`approve_transfer` gates P2P transfers on `!treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>()`. This means a compliance officer calling `PauseMintRedeemPermission` to pause issuance/redemption also freezes all whitelisted P2P transfers for that PAS asset. The existing two-axis pause model (phase-1 `PauseMintRedeem` for issuance + `GlobalPause` via DenyCapV2 for protocol) does not obviously cover transfer-specific pause. Spec (shared context §7.2 LEAD#4) flags this as a design choice, not a bug.

The transfer approval check uses `is_mint_redeem_paused<T>` as the transfer-pause gate. There is no independent `transfer_paused<T>` flag. Because transfers in a PAS system are usually the *last* operation to pause (you want to stop mint while letting existing holders unwind), this conflation forces an all-or-nothing pause that contradicts standard issuer playbooks.

Transfers are blocked when mint/redeem is paused for the PAS asset. The spec language and the error message `EMintRedeemPaused: "Mint and redeem operations are paused for this PAS asset"` describe a mint/redeem pause, but the check also disables user-to-user transfers. This is a design choice but: (1) the error text misleads — a user hitting this won't know why their transfer failed if the intent was to pause only issuance; (2) there is no independent "transfer pause" lever. If an operator wants to halt issuance while letting existing holders transfer out, they cannot.
impacts: Institutions cannot pause issuance (mint/redeem) without also halting secondary transfers. A common regulatory ask is the inverse: freeze transfers while preserving issuance/redemption with KYC counterparties. Current design does not allow that granularity.
None directly; attempt to exploit aborts. But the permissionless nature should be explicitly documented for the audit.
Operational inflexibility. Compliance officers lose granularity for partial-pause scenarios. Existing holders may be unable to exit when supply is paused for regulatory reasons.

Operationally surprising — a compliance officer toggling mint pause silently halts secondary market flow. Also the error message `EMintRedeemPaused` confuses users who were attempting a transfer, not a mint.

A pause intended to stop new issuance also blocks all peer-to-peer PAS transfers — possibly not the intended policy surface. Creates ambiguity between "I want to pause issuance while investigating" and "I want to freeze the entire coin."
Cannot independently control the two throttles. Error surface is also confusing: a user seeing 'mint/redeem paused' during a transfer will struggle to diagnose.
A regression in the whitelist assertion (e.g. checking sender twice instead of recipient) would ship undetected.
None — visibility is correct. Filed as info to document the cross-check passed.
Operational rigidity and conflated kill switches. Emergency pausing of mint also freezes a market.
Compliance bypass bugs could ship undetected.

Operational surprise during incident response. If a compliance officer disables mint/redeem (e.g. while investigating a mint-time issue), all on-chain PAS transfers for that coin abort with `EMintRedeemPaused` — unrelated users are affected. The error message `"Mint and redeem operations are paused for this PAS asset"` is also misleading when a user is just trying to transfer.
Design-consistent with spec; not a bug. However, institutions often need throughput controls. Surfacing this now avoids a later surprise.

Opaque framework abort on mis-routed Treasury; integrator confusion. Not a loss-of-funds bug because the subsequent `resolve_balance` still enforces upstream Policy approvals, but it means Hadron's layer does not fail-fast with its own error codes.

Operationally surprising: institutions expecting to halt issuance but let inventory flow among whitelisted counterparties will inadvertently freeze the secondary market. Increases blast radius of the pause cap.

Treasury cannot freeze issuance without also freezing secondary transfers; operational granularity is lost. Leads #4 of the orchestrator pre-read flags this as a design choice to confirm — it should not be reused as-is.

Policy ambiguity. Pausing mint/redeem to throttle supply also freezes the entire transfer graph. Conversely, unpausing transfers requires also unpausing mint/redeem. Either this conflation is intentional (document it) or a separate `is_transfer_paused` lever is needed.

title: pas.ts mintPasTokens/sendBalance use JS number for u64 amount — silent precision loss above 2^53
recommendations: Change the signature to `amount: bigint` and pass as `tx.pure.u64(amount)` (or the equivalent in the generated codegen). Apply the same fix to `full-setup.ts` where literals like `1_000_000` should become `1_000_000n`. Add a TypeScript lint rule forbidding `number` types for amount / balance parameters.

Change the function signature to `amount: bigint` and pass it directly; remove the implicit conversion. Same change for examples/full-setup.ts call sites (`mintPasTokens(..., 1_000_000)` becomes `mintPasTokens(..., 1_000_000n)`).

Change `amount: number` → `amount: bigint` and pass `tx.pure.u64(amount)` explicitly (or rely on generated client already doing so). Same fix for any other u64 amount param on the PAS path (confirm generated types from `sui-ts-codegen`).
Change amount to `bigint | number` (accept both; coerce internally to bigint) and BCS-serialize via `bcs.u64()` explicitly. Same remediation for any future amount-bearing operations.
Change `amount: number` to `amount: bigint | number` (or just `bigint`) and explicitly `BigInt(...)` before passing to the Move call. Document the unit (base units, not decimals).

Change the `amount` parameter to `bigint` in `mintPasTokens` (and `mintTokens` if phase-1 has the same issue — out of scope but worth flagging to the phase-1 reviewer). Serialize with `tx.pure.u64(amount)` and document the unit (smallest unit / decimals) in the JSDoc.
Change the amount parameter type to `bigint | number`, or strictly `bigint`, and pass `BigInt(amount)` through the SDK. The Sui TS SDK accepts `bigint` for u64 arguments.
Accept `amount: bigint` (or `string`) and use `tx.pure.u64(amount)` explicitly. Mirror `sui.js` conventions — pure(123n) for u64.

Accept `bigint | number`, pass through `BigInt(amount)`, and reject `number` values above `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER` with a thrown error. The `mintTokens` regulated-coin operation likely has the same issue and should be fixed in lockstep.

Change the `amount` parameter type to `bigint` (or `string | bigint`) and use `tx.pure.u64(BigInt(amount))` or let the codegen coerce. Update call-sites (full-setup.ts uses `1_000_000` which is safe; but the TYPE should not allow unsafe values). Apply the same fix to supply.ts (phase-1, but PAS flow depends on it).
descriptions: `mintPasTokens(..., amount: number)` passes `amount: number` straight into the Move call generator. JavaScript numbers are IEEE-754 doubles; integer precision holds only up to 2^53-1 (~9.007e15). For a 6-decimal coin, that limits safe values to ~9 billion tokens. At 9-decimal precision (typical for SUI-like tokens) the safe ceiling drops to ~9 million tokens. The same issue applies to `full-setup.ts` line 114 (`amount: 100_000`) and any caller passing a u64-range value. Amounts above 2^53 will silently lose precision.

`mintPasTokens(... amount: number ...)` and the internal `amount,` argument serialization rely on JS `number` for what is a u64 on-chain. Any amount above Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER (2^53-1 = 9_007_199_254_740_991) will silently lose precision on the JS side. For a 6-decimal token, the safe limit is ~9.007 billion whole tokens — reachable for institutional issuance. The @mysten/sui SDK accepts bigint for u64, so the right contract is `amount: bigint`.

`mintPasTokens(... , amount: number)` passes a JS `number` into the transaction builder for a u64 amount. For values above 2^53 (9,007,199,254,740,992), precision is lost silently. Institutional token issuance easily exceeds this if decimals are high (e.g. 6 decimals + 10B tokens = 10^19).

mintPasTokens has `amount: number`, and passes it directly to Move as a u64. JS numbers are IEEE-754 doubles, safe only up to 2^53 - 1 (~9.007e15). For a 6-decimal stablecoin, 2^53 base units is about 9 billion tokens — plausibly within operational bounds today but vulnerable if decimals increase or for low-decimals tokens. More importantly, the codegen-generated move call typically uses BCS serialization under the hood which may accept `bigint` or string; passing `number` is a latent foot-gun.

`amount: number` is a JavaScript `number` (double-precision float). The Move type is `u64`, which has values up to 2^64-1. For amounts above 2^53 (~9 quadrillion base units), precision is lost silently. For a stablecoin with 6 decimals, 2^53 base units ≈ 9 billion tokens — likely above practical limits today, but a bond-type asset with different decimals could exceed this sooner. The codegen-generated `pasSupply.mint` likely uses `bcs.u64()` via the SDK, which serializes the (potentially-rounded) JS number. Similar issue in the PAS-client `sendBalance` call in full-setup.ts (`amount: 100_000` — OK for this amount, but type system does not prevent overflow).

`mintPasTokens(treasuryId, coinType, accountId, amount: number)` types `amount` as JS `number`. Move-side the amount is a `u64` (max ~1.8e19). JS `number` is IEEE-754 double with safe integer range only up to 2^53-1 (~9.0e15). Amounts > `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER` will silently lose precision when BCS-serialized — and tokens use 6-decimal precision, so `1_000_000 * 1e6 = 1e12` fits today, but a malicious caller can pass `2^60` which will be serialized truncated. Same class of issue exists in example full-setup (100_000, 1_000_000 literals) and is not inherently broken, but the API should enforce bigint.

`mintPasTokens(..., amount: number)` accepts a JS `number`. The generated `pasSupply.mint` call takes `amount` by value; Move expects `u64`. JS numbers are IEEE-754 doubles with 53-bit mantissa, so any amount > 2^53 - 1 (≈ 9e15) loses precision. For a 6-decimal PAS coin, that caps the max safe mint at ~9 billion units — large, but reachable for institutional issuance.

`mintPasTokens` accepts `amount: number`. JS `number` is IEEE 754 double with 53-bit integer precision; Move u64 is 64-bit. Any amount ≥ 2^53 (~9e15) will silently lose precision. For a coin with 6 decimals, that's ~9 billion whole tokens — plausible in institutional bond issuance, exactly the use-case driving PAS.

`mintPasTokens(.. , amount: number)` uses JavaScript `number` for a u64 Move parameter. Above 2^53, precision is lost silently. With 6 decimals, 2^53 represents 9,007,199,254 'whole units' — plausible for institutional bond notionals. The downstream BCS serialization treats it as a u64 but the JS value has already been coerced.

`mintPasTokens(..., amount: number)` and the corresponding call arguments in `pasSupply.mint` pass a JS `number`. For PAS assets with large decimals (e.g. 6 decimals + large face-values), u64 amounts above `2^53 - 1` (9,007,199,254,740,992) will lose precision in the JS number path. The Mint Permission auth flow, BCS serialization, and the Move entry will all receive a silently truncated value.
impacts: Silent off-by-many error for large amounts. An institution minting `10_000_000_000_000_000` (1e16) into a PAS account will receive a different amount on-chain than expected. Bug is invisible in test flows using small amounts (1e6).
Silent amount truncation at scale; high-precision institutional mints can end up with the wrong on-chain amount without errors.
Off-by-one or silently-truncated mints at scale.
For large mints (rare but possible), precision is lost silently and the on-chain amount differs from the intended amount.
Silent mint of slightly-wrong amount for very large asks. More importantly, the external API invites callers to hit this bug.
Any future caller passing a large amount would silently corrupt it. Already a recurring foot-gun across the SDK ecosystem.
Silent precision loss for very large mint amounts. A mint of `1e18` (a plausible institutional bond issue) would truncate to the nearest double-representable value.
Silent incorrect mint amounts for large issuances. Phase-1 `mintTokens` has the same gap (`scripts/src/operations/supply.ts`), but this PR extends the pattern into PAS.
Large mint amounts silently round. No runtime guard.
High-value PAS mints silently produce incorrect balances. With 6 decimals, this is ~9T tokens which is reachable for corporate bonds or ETF NAV denominated in base units.

title: register_pas_asset lacks explicit is_pas_coin<T>() guard — opaque abort on double registration
recommendations: Insert an explicit early check `assert!(!treasury.is_coin_registered<T>(), ECoinAlreadyRegistered)` at the start of `register_pas_asset`, before `policy::new_for_currency`. This makes the error path deterministic and short-circuits the upstream call.
At the top of register_pas_asset, add: assert!(!treasury.is_coin_registered<T>(), ECoinAlreadyRegistered); (or a dedicated EPasAlreadyRegistered) before invoking any PAS upstream fn.

Document in pas_admin module comment: "The PAS Namespace is singleton; a coin type T can only be a PAS asset for a single institution. Registering the same T in two treasuries aborts the second with EPolicyAlreadyExists."
Add `assert!(!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), EAlreadyRegistered);` (and/or `!treasury.is_coin_registered<T>()`) as the first line after `auth.assert_is_valid_for_treasury`.

Prepend an explicit assert!(!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>() && !treasury.is_coin_registered<T>(), ECoinAlreadyRegistered) before the upstream policy::new_for_currency call, so the abort happens with a Hadron-local error before any caps get moved.

Prepend `assert!(!treasury.is_coin_registered<T>(), ...)` or a more specific `ECoinAlreadyPas` in register_pas_asset. Separately, consider distinct error codes for 'already registered as regulated' vs 'already registered as PAS' to make operator triage deterministic.

Add an early assert in `pas_admin::register_pas_asset` — e.g. `assert!(!treasury.is_coin_registered<T>(), ECoinAlreadyRegistered)` — so operators get one, predictable error code before any upstream policy state is touched.
Add `assert!(!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), EAlreadyPasRegistered);` as the first line of `register_pas_asset` to fail fast with a Hadron-layer error before any upstream mutation.

Either (a) remove `version` from `register_pas_asset`'s parameter list entirely since it is consumed only as an object-id baked into the template — obtain the version shared-object ID another way — or (b) add an explicit `version.check_is_valid()` at the top. `update_transfer_template` has the same issue at L113–121.

At the top of `register_pas_asset`, add explicit early checks: `assert!(!treasury.is_coin_registered<T>(), ECoinAlreadyRegistered); assert!(!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ECoinAlreadyRegistered);` — return Hadron's error consistently.

In each of the four whitelist mutator paths (and in `treasury::add_to_whitelist`/`remove_from_whitelist`/`set_whitelist_send`/`set_whitelist_receive`), add an explicit `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin)` check at the top. The error constant already exists in treasury.move L67. Alternatively, extend `auth::new_auth` with an `is_pas_coin` check when the permission is `WhitelistManagePermission` (less clean, conflates concerns).
descriptions: The happy path of `register_pas_asset` calls (in order): `policy::new_for_currency` (upstream abort-on-exists check in `pas/policy.move` L51), set required approvals, `register_transfer_template`, `policy::share`, then finally `treasury.add_pas_coin_caps` (which aborts on `ECoinAlreadyRegistered` if the Hadron treasury already has T registered via the regulated path). This means a second registration attempt against a coin already registered as regulated via `admin::register_coin` first creates and shares a Policy object for T in the PAS namespace (and writes a template), then aborts — rolling the whole tx back, so no state is persisted. This is semantically safe but the upstream-first ordering makes the error surface confusing when the conflict is actually in the Hadron treasury.

register_pas_asset<T> neither asserts !treasury.is_pas_coin<T>() nor !treasury.is_coin_registered<T>() before calling pas::policy::new_for_currency. A second registration attempt (or registration of a T already registered as a regulated coin via admin::register_coin) will abort either deep inside pas::policy::new_for_currency at EPolicyAlreadyExists, or inside add_pas_coin_caps at ECoinAlreadyRegistered. The surface abort happens after global PAS state has partially changed (policy derivation attempt). While atomicity of the PTB means nothing is committed, the error is not local and is harder to diagnose.

Upstream `policy::new_for_currency` asserts `!namespace.policy_exists<Balance<C>>()` (at policy.move L51). In Hadron's design, a single Namespace is global and shared across all treasuries, so a policy created by one institution's Treasury blocks any other Treasury from registering the same coin type T as a PAS asset. This is a single-tenant-per-coin constraint imposed by upstream PAS. Hadron does not document this anywhere; two treasuries attempting to register the same coinType T as PAS will see the second one abort at upstream EPolicyAlreadyExists. Since coin types T are 1:1 with OTWs (unique per package), this is unlikely in practice but possible if someone tries to reuse a T across treasuries.

The function does not call `treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()` at entry. If invoked twice, the first abort comes from upstream `pas::policy::new_for_currency` (`EPolicyAlreadyExists`) or, if T was previously registered as a regulated coin, from Hadron's `ETreasuryCapNotEmpty` / `ECoinAlreadyRegistered` deep in `add_pas_coin_caps`, after the PAS Policy / template command has already been created (and `policy.share()` scheduled). Because the whole tx aborts this is currently safe, but it wastes compute on work that will be rolled back and produces opaque errors.

register_pas_asset calls policy::new_for_currency first, which aborts with EPolicyAlreadyExists if a namespace-level policy already exists for Balance<T>. Only later does add_pas_coin_caps check the local DOF (ECoinAlreadyRegistered). If an operator accidentally attempts a second registration, the abort comes from upstream PAS code with a non-Hadron error; the local surface is opaque. Also, a second registration consumes the caller's TreasuryCap<T> into the function and aborts, potentially losing it in the aborted transaction.

On a second call for the same T, the function proceeds into `pas::policy::new_for_currency` which asserts `!namespace.policy_exists<Balance<C>>()` (upstream policy.move L51) and aborts inside the dep. Hadron's own Treasury would also abort in `add_pas_coin_caps` on `ECoinAlreadyRegistered`, but only after the upstream call already consumed work. An early `assert!(!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ...)` would surface a clean Hadron-owned error. Also relevant: if the coin was already registered as a regulated coin (phase-1 admin::register_coin path), this function aborts inside `add_pas_coin_caps` with `ECoinAlreadyRegistered` — the user-facing error does not distinguish 'regulated already' from 'PAS already'.

If `register_pas_asset<T>` is called twice for the same T (or after a non-PAS `admin::register_coin<T>`), the call sequence is: (1) `policy::new_for_currency<T>` aborts inside upstream with `EPolicyAlreadyExists` (policy.move L51), OR (2) succeeds (first PAS registration after a non-PAS registration) then Hadron's `add_pas_coin_caps` aborts with `ECoinAlreadyRegistered` on the treasury_cap_key DOF. Either path aborts the txn (no state leak), but the local error surface is confusing — depending on call order the operator sees a PAS error code or a treasury error code.

A second call to `register_pas_asset<T>` on a treasury that has already PAS-registered T will abort, but the abort happens deep inside `pas::policy::new_for_currency` (upstream asserts `!namespace.policy_exists<Balance<C>>()` at policy.move L51) before Hadron's own `add_pas_coin_caps` can run. If the upstream policy lives in a different namespace (e.g. admin passed the wrong `pas_namespace`), upstream may NOT abort — it could claim a fresh policy in the new namespace — and then Hadron's `add_pas_coin_caps` aborts with `ECoinAlreadyRegistered`. Mid-transaction state (policy + cap exist in PAS, but not wired to Hadron) would need to be cleaned up manually.

`register_pas_asset` takes `version: &Version` but never calls `version.check_is_valid()`. The only use is to pass into `register_transfer_template`, which also does not call `check_is_valid` — it only embeds `object::id(version)` into the PTB template. The version gate is implicitly relied upon via `TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>` construction (which does check version), but the signature implies a direct check that does not exist.

`register_pas_asset<T>` does not explicitly check whether T is already registered (either as regulated via admin::register_coin or as PAS via a prior register_pas_asset call). It relies on `policy::new_for_currency` aborting via `EPolicyAlreadyExists` (upstream policy.move L51) and/or `treasury.add_pas_coin_caps` aborting via `ECoinAlreadyRegistered` (treasury.move L169). Each abort surfaces from a different module, making failures hard to diagnose.

`add_to_whitelist`, `remove_from_whitelist`, `set_whitelist_send`, `set_whitelist_receive` accept `Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>`. `auth::new_auth` asserts `treasury.is_coin_registered<T>()` (i.e. any coin with a TreasuryCap DOF) — but NOT `is_pas_coin<T>()`. If a T was registered via `admin::register_coin` (regulated, non-PAS), an Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T> can be minted, and the call then aborts deep inside `dynamic_object_field::borrow_mut` because `WhitelistKey<T>` DOF does not exist. The abort surfaces as a framework error, not a Hadron error.
impacts: Minor ergonomics. Error message from `ECoinAlreadyRegistered` happens only after the upstream PAS side has been exercised. If the upstream `new_for_currency` had weaker guarantees (e.g., did not rollback cleanly), this ordering could leak state.
Opaque failure when the caller misconfigures registration; poor DX and harder debugging of failed partner integrations.
Cross-institution coin-type collision blocks second registration; opaque upstream abort. Practically rare but surprising.
Operational UX — callers get an error from deep in PAS code instead of a clean Hadron-level abort. Not a security issue because atomic rollback cleans state.
Opaque errors and non-intuitive failure mode for double registration. Low risk because the operator can inspect the tx and re-submit, but the UX is poor for a gated admin action.
Diagnosability: harder to tell whether registration failed at the Hadron layer or inside the PAS dep.
Minor UX; hard to diagnose misconfigurations.
Opaque abort surface on duplicate registration. Operator could burn gas on a failed registration that looks wasted but actually created dangling upstream state.
Future refactors that no longer require a `TreasuryAuth` (e.g. an admin-cap bypass) would silently lose version enforcement. The dead argument also adds gas cost and review burden.

Operational confusion. Different failure modes depending on whether the prior registration was as a regulated coin (Hadron-side abort), a prior PAS registration (PAS-side abort at `EPolicyAlreadyExists`), or both. Callers trying to re-register can't easily infer root cause.

Operational misdiagnosis: compliance officers get cryptic errors on mis-typed coin. More critically, the permission system's invariants become hazy — a whitelist-manage role nominally granted for "PAS operations" implicitly covers regulated coins too, just with non-functional outcomes.

title: treasury::is_whitelisted/can_send/can_receive abort on non-PAS coins (opaque DOF error)
recommendations: Guard each query with `is_pas_coin<T>(treasury)` and return `false` when the DOF is absent. Example: `public fun is_whitelisted<T>(treasury: &Treasury, addr: address): bool { if (!is_pas_coin<T>(treasury)) return false; let wl: &Table<address, WhitelistEntry> = dynamic_object_field::borrow(&treasury.id, keys::whitelist_key<T>()); wl.contains(addr) }`. Do the same for `can_send` and `can_receive`.

Guard with is_pas_coin: `if (!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()) { return false }` at the top of is_whitelisted / can_send / can_receive. Keep the asserting variants (`assert_can_send`, `assert_can_receive`) as the abort path.

Guard each predicate with an early `if (!is_pas_coin<T>(treasury)) return false;` (or assert `ENotPasCoin`). The choice depends on intent — for read queries returning false is more composable, for asserts hard-fail with `ENotPasCoin` is cleaner than a DOF abort.

Add an explicit assert!(is_pas_coin<T>(treasury), ENotPasCoin) at the top of is_whitelisted, can_send, can_receive — or return false when the DOF is absent. For the assert-variants (assert_can_send / assert_can_receive), the guard should also run so the abort reason is actionable.
Prepend `assert!(is_pas_coin<T>(treasury), ENotPasCoin);` to each of the 9 whitelist functions, or factor to a private helper `borrow_whitelist_mut<T>` that asserts and returns the table ref.

Add `assert!(is_pas_coin<T>(treasury), ENotPasCoin);` as the first line of each of: `is_whitelisted`, `can_send`, `can_receive`, `add_to_whitelist`, `remove_from_whitelist`, `set_whitelist_send`, `set_whitelist_receive`. (The public query functions `can_send`/`can_receive` could also simply return `false` without aborting to preserve query usability.)

Add `assert!(is_pas_coin<T>(treasury), ENotPasCoin);` (or call `policy_cap<T>(treasury)` which already has the assertion) as the first line of each of `is_whitelisted`, `can_send`, `can_receive`, `add_to_whitelist`, `remove_from_whitelist`, `set_whitelist_send`, `set_whitelist_receive`. `assert_can_*` can inherit the check through these. Consider that `is_whitelisted` as a pure query may want to return `false` instead of aborting when the coin isn't a PAS coin.

Early-return `false` if `!is_pas_coin<T>(treasury)`: `if (!is_pas_coin<T>(treasury)) return false;` at the top of each of the three view functions. Leave `assert_can_send` / `assert_can_receive` as abort-on-false.

Make predicates fail-safe: early-return false when `!is_pas_coin<T>()`. Example: `public fun is_whitelisted<T>(treasury: &Treasury, addr: address): bool { if (!is_pas_coin<T>(treasury)) return false; let wl = dynamic_object_field::borrow(...); wl.contains(addr) }`. Keep `assert_can_send/receive` strict (they already assert).
descriptions: `is_whitelisted<T>`, `can_send<T>`, and `can_receive<T>` unconditionally call `dynamic_object_field::borrow` on `keys::whitelist_key<T>()`. For any coin type T that is not a PAS asset (i.e., the whitelist DOF was never added by `add_pas_coin_caps`), this aborts inside the framework with an opaque `EFieldDoesNotExist` error instead of returning `false`. Callers (including off-chain indexers) expecting `is_whitelisted<T>` to be a safe predicate will crash.

`is_whitelisted`, `can_send`, and `can_receive` immediately call `dynamic_object_field::borrow<...>` on the whitelist DOF without first checking `is_pas_coin<T>()`. For a non-PAS coin T, the whitelist DOF does not exist, so these three read-only predicates abort rather than returning `false`. This breaks the expected read-side contract that boolean predicates are total. Off-chain callers / other modules cannot safely call `can_send` to compose behavior.

The three public read-only predicates `is_whitelisted<T>`, `can_send<T>`, `can_receive<T>` unconditionally `dynamic_object_field::borrow` the whitelist DOF keyed by `WhitelistKey<T>`. If coin T is not a PAS coin (DOF never created), the borrow aborts with a generic dynamic-object-field error. These are `public`, so external consumers (indexers, scripts, composing contracts) calling them on any non-PAS coin hit an opaque abort instead of receiving `false`.

Public functions is_whitelisted<T>, can_send<T>, and can_receive<T> call dynamic_object_field::borrow directly without first checking is_pas_coin<T>(). For a non-PAS coin type T (whitelist DOF absent), the borrow aborts with a framework-level dynamic_field abort code and no meaningful message. These functions are public and phantom-parameterized in T — any off-chain caller (SDK, indexer, explorer) or adversarial on-chain caller can trigger the abort for any arbitrary T and get an opaque failure. Worse, code inside pas_supply::mint indirectly reaches this via assert_can_receive — if a non-PAS coin slips into this call path (e.g. via a future refactor), the error surface is framework-level, not the intended ENotWhitelistedReceive.

`is_whitelisted`, `can_send`, `can_receive`, `assert_can_send`, `assert_can_receive`, `add_to_whitelist`, `remove_from_whitelist`, `set_whitelist_send`, `set_whitelist_receive` all call `dynamic_object_field::borrow(&treasury.id, keys::whitelist_key<T>())` without first asserting `is_pas_coin<T>()`. If T is not a PAS coin, the DOF does not exist and the borrow aborts with the generic `dynamic_object_field` abort code, not the well-defined `ENotPasCoin` code defined nearby. This makes error diagnosis harder and is a mismatch with `policy_cap<T>` which does assert via `ENotPasCoin`.

`is_whitelisted<T>`, `can_send<T>`, `can_receive<T>`, `add_to_whitelist<T>`, `remove_from_whitelist<T>`, `set_whitelist_send<T>`, `set_whitelist_receive<T>` all assume the whitelist DOF exists at `keys::whitelist_key<T>()`. If `T` is registered as a non-PAS coin (via `admin::register_coin`), the DOF was never created. Any call to these helpers aborts deep inside `dynamic_object_field::borrow` / `borrow_mut` with a generic framework error rather than the dedicated `ENotPasCoin`. Callers from `pas_admin` are protected because `Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>` construction requires `is_coin_registered<T>()` only — NOT `is_pas_coin<T>()`.

`is_whitelisted<T>`, `can_send<T>`, `can_receive<T>`, `assert_can_send<T>` and `assert_can_receive<T>` unconditionally `dynamic_object_field::borrow(&treasury.id, keys::whitelist_key<T>())` without first checking `is_pas_coin<T>(treasury)`. If a caller (or any downstream module like `pas_transfer::approve_transfer`) invokes these with a `T` that was never registered as a PAS asset, the DOF borrow aborts with a framework-level error — not `ENotPasCoin`. `is_whitelisted` is `public`, so external code can call it and will be greeted with an opaque dynamic-field error instead of a clean `ENotPasCoin`.

Public view helpers `is_whitelisted<T>`, `can_send<T>`, `can_receive<T>` unconditionally `dynamic_object_field::borrow` the whitelist DOF. When `T` is a non-PAS registered coin (or an unregistered coin), the DOF does not exist and the call aborts with a framework-level `EFieldDoesNotExist`, not a Hadron error. Off-chain tooling calling these as `devInspect` will receive an opaque abort code instead of a sensible `false`.

`is_whitelisted<T>`, `can_send<T>`, `can_receive<T>` borrow the whitelist table via `dynamic_object_field::borrow`. If T is not a PAS coin for this treasury, the DOF does not exist and borrow aborts — despite these being marketed as predicates that `return bool`. Any caller using them expecting a fail-safe false will instead abort.
impacts: Opaque aborts in read-only queries. Off-chain tooling and frontend dApps that probe whitelist state across arbitrary coin types cannot distinguish 'coin is not PAS' from 'coin is PAS but addr not whitelisted'. Forces every caller to first call `is_pas_coin<T>()` as a guard.
Predicates intended as safe read queries instead abort; breaks composability and requires every caller to check is_pas_coin first.

Abort-on-query rather than a clean `false` return. Breaks composability — any caller that generically queries whitelist state for a coin type without first checking `is_pas_coin` will abort. `assert_can_send`/`assert_can_receive` inherit the same foot-gun.
Opaque errors for integrators; tighter error contract is lost. A caller cannot distinguish "coin not registered as PAS" from "address not whitelisted".

Callers hit opaque abort for what is a well-typed usage error. Combined with the note in LEADS §2, a non-PAS coin with a crafted Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T> (possible because `auth::new_auth` only checks `is_coin_registered`, not `is_pas_coin`) aborts inside DOF rather than giving a clean error.

Ergonomic: the error surface is opaque. A compliance officer calling `add_to_whitelist` for a coin that was erroneously registered as regulated (not PAS) gets a low-level DOF abort instead of a clear `ENotPasCoin`.

Confusing abort surface for integrators; harder runtime diagnosis; latent footgun if any future callsite is added without the awareness that T must already be PAS-registered. Also means `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` for an accidentally-misrouted Request would abort deep in the framework rather than with a Hadron-layer error.

Poor UX for off-chain callers. Also creates inconsistency: `is_coin_registered<T>` correctly returns `bool`, but the sibling whitelist queries abort. Indexers and compliance dashboards that query blindly across all known coin types will fail on PAS/non-PAS mismatches.
Off-chain view call over non-PAS T aborts; on-chain check-before-mutate idioms can't be written safely (e.g. `if (treasury.is_whitelisted<T>(addr)) { ... }` cannot be used defensively).

title: deployPasTokenTemplate hard-codes bytecode hex without source-of-truth script or regen check
recommendations: Add a `scripts/src/deployPasTokenTemplate/build-bytecode.ts` that compiles `token_template_pas/` with `sui move build`, extracts the single module, and writes the hex to `getBytecode.ts` with a SHA-256 digest comment. Wire it as a prebuild step. Add a runtime assertion that the bytecode is valid (e.g., via `get_constants(bytecode)` returning a non-empty set).

Add a `pnpm build:bytecode` script that builds token_template_pas and emits the hex into getBytecode.ts. Add a CI step that rebuilds and `diff`s against the checked-in file. Document the dep list (0x1, 0x2) as a constant with a comment.

Either (a) publish bytecode regeneration instructions + a CI check that compiles `token_template_pas` and asserts the resulting bytecode matches this hex, or (b) compile at deploy time via the Move CLI wrapper and drop the hardcoded hex.

Either (a) add a script `bun run rebuild-pas-bytecode` that builds token_template_pas/ and writes the hex string; (b) add a CI check that diffs compiled bytecode against the embedded hex on PR and fails on drift; (c) at minimum add a comment block at the top of the file listing the source commit SHA and compilation command.

Add a top-of-file comment documenting the exact build command to regenerate the bytecode (e.g. `cd token_template_pas && sui move build && xxd -p build/token_template_pas/bytecode_modules/token_template.mv`). Consider a `bun run regenerate-bytecode` script and a CI check.

Add a `pnpm run regen-pas-bytecode` (or `bun run regen-pas-bytecode`) script that (a) runs `sui move build` in `token_template_pas/`, (b) reads the compiled `.mv` file, (c) hex-encodes it, (d) writes the result to `getBytecode.ts`. Add a CI check that this script is idempotent — i.e., running it produces no diff.

Add a `scripts/regenerate-pas-bytecode.sh` (or CI job) that runs `sui move build --path token_template_pas`, extracts the module bytes, hex-encodes them, and asserts byte-for-byte equality with `getBytecode.ts`. Fail CI on mismatch. Alternatively, generate the bytecode at runtime via `@mysten/move-bytecode-template` from the compiled artifact in `token_template_pas/build/`.

Add a build script (e.g. `scripts/build-pas-template-bytecode.ts`) that invokes `sui move build --path token_template_pas`, reads the produced `.mv` module, and emits the hex into `getBytecode.ts`. Add a CI step that runs it and fails on diff. Alternatively, read the bytecode from disk at runtime using `readFileSync` on the `.mv` output. Document the regeneration command in a comment block above the hex.

(1) Add a script `scripts/src/deployPasTokenTemplate/regenerate-bytecode.ts` that builds `token_template_pas/`, extracts the bytecode, hex-encodes it, and writes it to `getBytecode.ts`. (2) Add a CI check that re-runs the script and diffs against the committed `BYTECODE_HEX`. (3) In the meantime, add a header comment in getBytecode.ts: `// Regenerated from token_template_pas/ at commit <sha>. Do not hand-edit.`
descriptions: The bytecode is a long hex literal checked into source with no reproducible build process: no script or npm task to regenerate from `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move`, no hash/digest check at runtime, and no comment on the toolchain/sui version used to produce the bytes. Reviewers cannot verify the bytecode corresponds to the published `.move` source, and a future framework upgrade (e.g., coin_registry v2 signature change) that invalidates the bytes will silently produce an invalid-module publish error.

The PAS token template bytecode is hard-coded as a single hex string with no regeneration script, no comment tying it to a source commit, and no test that recompiles `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move` and asserts byte-equality. The companion Move source exists at token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move but there is no CI check that the hex matches the current source. If token_template.move is edited (new decimals default, new struct fields, new framework deps), the hex will drift silently and deployments will publish stale bytecode.

`BYTECODE_HEX` is a long hex blob with no accompanying script or doc for regenerating it from `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move`. If the upstream `coin_registry` API changes, or if the template source is edited, the bytecode drifts silently from the Move source. There is no CI check that the hex matches a freshly-compiled `token_template.move`.

The file embeds BYTECODE_HEX as a single-line hex string with no comment explaining: (a) which token_template_pas commit it was compiled from; (b) how to regenerate it; (c) what Move compiler version was used. If token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move is edited, nothing ensures the bytecode is regenerated. This silently decouples the on-disk .move source from the hex actually published. Same pattern exists in the phase-1 deployTokenTemplate but the phase-2 reviewer should flag this as a new instance perpetuating the anti-pattern.

The bytecode constant is a hand-pasted hex blob with no header comment describing how to regenerate it from `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move`. Any change to the template source requires a manual rebuild + re-pasting. There is no CI check that the bytecode hex matches the compiled module.

The PAS token template bytecode is a hand-pasted hex string with no script or CI check that regenerates it from `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move`. If the source `.move` is edited (e.g., constants renamed, new imports added) but the hex is not regenerated, `deployPasTokenTemplate` will publish a stale module whose bytecode does not match the source-tree expectation.

Bytecode is stored as a hex literal. The `token_template_pas` Move package (the apparent source) is checked in, but there is no build script or CI check that verifies the hex literal matches `sui move build` output for `token_template_pas`. Any drift between source and hex is undetectable at review time. Phase-1 token_template has the same shape, but the PAS template is new so the attack surface is new.

The 1.1 KB hex blob is a hand-pasted compilation artefact of `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move`. There is no comment, no script, and no CI check tying the hex to the .move source. If the template changes (e.g. add a new constant, change module structure), the script will silently deploy the STALE bytecode while the admin believes the new .move is in use.

`BYTECODE_HEX` is a single 1KB hex string embedded in source. There is no build script or comment linking it to the `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move` it was compiled from. Divergence between the Move source in the repo and the bytecode actually deployed is undetectable without manual disassembly.
impacts: Trust gap: the bytes could silently diverge from the .move source. A malicious PR could edit the hex blob to publish a different coin module than the source suggests. Operationally, drift between Sui versions will produce inscrutable publish failures.
Silent drift: the published PAS token template may not match the source-tracked Move module. Partners reading the source will see one behavior, the network will run another.

Operational / build-reproducibility risk. A source edit in `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move` is not reflected in deployed bytecode. Attacker with repo write could slip inconsistent template bytecode past a source-only review.

Source/binary drift: a future edit to token_template.move (e.g. adding an event, changing decimals default) is not reflected in deploy unless someone manually regenerates the hex. High-risk for compliance-sensitive deployments.
Silent drift between `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move` and the bytecode the scripts actually publish.

Source-bytecode drift risk. A maintainer making an innocuous change to `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move` will not see any build error; the hex simply doesn't reflect their change. The module could also diverge from `token_template/sources/token_template.move` (regulated variant) in unexpected ways.

Supply-chain: a future contributor could silently change `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move` without regenerating the bytecode, and deployments would continue to use the old bytecode. Worst case: malicious bytecode diverges from readable source.

Drift between `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move` and the compiled artefact goes undetected. Patched constants (symbol, name, etc.) rely on exact byte offsets — a recompile can shift these, making `patchConstants` silently corrupt the bytecode. There is no integrity check.

Audit gap: a reviewer cannot verify that the bytecode matches the in-repo source without recompiling and byte-comparing. Post-audit, a malicious contributor could update the Move source without updating the bytecode, so deployments continue using the old bytecode silently (or vice-versa).

title: scripts/src/operations/pas.ts: pkg() may return empty string if deploy-output.json missing
recommendations: Remove the `as any` cast; if there is a type mismatch between `@mysten/pas` and `@mysten/sui`, upgrade one side or file an upstream issue. Construct `PASClient` lazily inside each function to pick up the current client.

In `pkg()` or at the top of each exported operation (registerPasAsset, addToWhitelist, removeFromWhitelist, mintPasTokens, deployAndRegisterPasAsset), throw an explicit error when `pkg()` returns empty: `if (!pkg()) throw new Error('Package ID not set; run `bun run deploy` first');`.
Pin the SDK types properly (upgrade `@mysten/pas` to a version aligned with `@mysten/sui` v2.4.0 types, or drop the `as any` and file an upstream issue). Avoid `any` at the dep boundary.

Add a guard at module load or at first call: `if (!pkg()) throw new Error('Package ID is not configured — run bun run deploy first')`. Alternatively, treat pkg() returning '' as an error in a helper and throw there.

Investigate whether @mysten/pas exports a type alias that accepts SuiGrpcClient or a SuiClient interface. If not, file an issue upstream and remove the `as any` once supported. Alternatively, adopt the PASClient client-factory pattern shown in full-setup.ts `client.$extend(pas())`.

Lazy-initialize `pasClient` inside the functions that use it: `const pasClient = new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) })` inside each function, or memoize via a `getPasClient()` helper. Remove the `as any` cast by aligning SDK versions or by importing the correct client type.

In `constants.ts`, change `pkg()` to `throw new Error('Package not deployed — run bun run deploy first')` if both fields are empty. Or explicitly validate at the top of each PAS operation. Same issue likely affects other scripts under `operations/` but only the PAS ones are in scope.

In `pkg()` (constants.ts), throw an explicit error if both `latest` and `v1` are empty. Or add a guard at the top of each exported function in `pas.ts`: `const p = pkg(); if (!p) throw new Error('deploy-output.json missing — run \'bun run deploy\' first');`.
Remove the `as any`; import the correct client type from `@mysten/pas` and assert conformance. If the types genuinely disagree, file an upstream ticket against `@mysten/pas`.
Before calling `registerPasAsset`, dev-inspect `treasury::is_pas_coin<T>(treasury)` and short-circuit with a clear message if already registered.

Lazy-initialize per call, or accept the client as a parameter: `function getPasClient() { return new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as unknown as any }) }` and invoke inside each function. Investigate the `as any` cast — the correct fix is to align PASClient's expected SuiClient version with the one @mysten/sui 2.4.0 exports.
descriptions: `const pasClient = new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any })` is constructed at module load time and reuses the same PASClient for all calls. This makes NETWORK effectively compile-time. Worse, the `as any` cast hides that `PASClient` may expect a stricter SuiClient interface than `getClient` returns — any API mismatch is silently coerced and will crash at runtime.

Every PAS operation builds `package: pkg()` and type arguments like `${pkg()}::permissions::RegisterCoinPermission`. `pkg()` in scripts/src/constants.ts returns `CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1`, both of which default to `''` if deploy-output.json is missing. A call chain invoking `registerPasAsset(...)` without prior `bun run deploy` will silently construct a transaction with `::permissions::RegisterCoinPermission` (empty package) and an invalid target — the error only surfaces at SDK submission with a confusing message. There is no pre-check.

`new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any })` — the `as any` escape hatch hides an SDK-version mismatch between `@mysten/sui@^2.4.0` and whichever Sui client type `@mysten/pas@0.0.3` expects. If the PAS SDK drops `suiClient` and switches to `client`, or the shape of `getClient` changes, this blows up at runtime rather than compile time.

All operations (registerPasAsset, deployAndRegisterPasAsset, addToWhitelist, removeFromWhitelist, mintPasTokens) call `pkg()` — which falls back to '' if CONSTANTS.package.latest || v1 is empty (see constants.ts L63-65 and L53). If deploy-output.json is absent or parsed null, pkg() silently returns '' and the transaction is built against package '', which will fail at execution with an opaque effects error. No script-level guard asserts pkg() is non-empty.

`new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any })` coerces the client to `any`. Likely because @mysten/pas expected a different Client type than SuiGrpcClient. This papers over a real type-system mismatch and will not catch future API changes in either SDK.

`const pasClient = new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any });` is executed at import time. If `getClient(NETWORK)` throws (e.g., network env var missing), the entire `operations/pas.ts` module fails to load, breaking unrelated imports from `operations/index.ts`. The `as any` cast also suppresses SDK type-compatibility checks between `@mysten/sui` and `@mysten/pas`.

Every function in `pas.ts` calls `pkg()` (from `../constants`). `pkg()` returns `CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1`, both of which default to `''` if `deploy-output.json` is absent (see `loadFromDeployOutput()` in `constants.ts`). A missing deploy file yields a package ID of `''`, producing malformed `typeArguments` like `::permissions::RegisterCoinPermission`. The underlying move-call fails opaquely at tx build time.

Every call in `pas.ts` uses `pkg()` from `constants.ts` which returns `CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1`, both defaulted to `''` when `deploy-output.json` is missing (constants.ts L53, L64). No precondition guard. A user who runs `bun run setup` before `bun run deploy` (or with a malformed deploy-output.json) gets a signed transaction with `package: ''` which at best fails with a cryptic RPC error and at worst gets accepted by an adversarial fallback endpoint.

`new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any })` casts the Sui client to `any`. This suppresses type-checking between the Hadron TS world (which uses `@mysten/sui` v2) and the PAS client's expected client shape. If `@mysten/pas` tightens its type signature or shifts the client interface, the cast will silently hide the break until runtime.

The script does not query on-chain state before attempting `registerPasAsset`. If the coin type is already registered as PAS (second run of the full-setup example), the tx will abort with `ECoinAlreadyRegistered`, consuming gas and emitting a failure log with no remediation hint.

`const pasClient = new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any });` executes at module-import time. If `getClient(NETWORK)` throws (misconfigured network / env) or PASClient constructor has side effects, every consumer of `pas.ts` incurs that even if they only import a single function. The `as any` cast also hides SDK type-mismatch bugs.
impacts: (a) Scripts cannot easily switch network mid-run. (b) Any type-level contract drift between `@mysten/pas` and `@mysten/sui` versions is hidden by the `as any` cast and surfaces only at runtime.
Confusing error for first-time operators and automation; a missing deploy output results in a deep SDK parse error instead of a clear actionable message.
Loses TS type safety at a critical integration boundary.
CI/dev pipeline gets stuck on invalid tx with opaque error rather than a clear 'run bun run deploy first' message.
If PASClient's expected client interface drifts (e.g. adds a required method), the cast hides the mismatch until runtime.
Brittleness. A missing `NETWORK` env var will fail to load the whole operations barrel, breaking regulated-coin flows too even if the user never touches PAS.
Poor error message when the deploy step was skipped. The user sees an obscure SDK error instead of 'deploy package first'.
Operator error is silent and potentially costly (failed tx + gas, or confusing error surface). Not a direct exploit path but a real usability / safety gap.
Future runtime breakage on `@mysten/pas` upgrade; no compile-time signal.
Operational ergonomics only — no correctness impact. But first-time integrators will see a confusing failure.
Brittle import chain; harder-to-diagnose startup failures; type-safety escape hatch.

title: pas_admin::register_pas_asset / update_transfer_template do not call version.check_is_valid()
recommendations: Either (a) add an explicit `version.check_is_valid()` at the top of `register_pas_asset` and `update_transfer_template`, mirroring the pattern in `pas_transfer::approve_transfer`; or (b) drop the unused `version` parameter from the internal helper `register_transfer_template` and document at the call site that the version check is delegated to auth.
Add `version.check_is_valid();` as the very first statement in `register_pas_asset` and `update_transfer_template`, matching the pattern in `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` (which does check).
Add `version.check_is_valid();` as the second statement (after the auth assert) for defense-in-depth, mirroring `pas_transfer::approve_transfer`.
Add an explicit version.check_is_valid() at the top of register_pas_asset (and update_transfer_template). This mirrors the defensive pattern in treasury::create and pas_transfer::approve_transfer.
Call `version.check_is_valid()` at the top of `register_pas_asset` and `update_transfer_template`, mirroring the defensive style used in `treasury::create`. Cheap and makes the invariant local.

Add `version.check_is_valid();` as the first statement in `register_pas_asset` and `update_transfer_template` for symmetry with `treasury::create`/`approve_transfer` and to defend against future auth-creation refactors.

Leave as-is — the `mut` binding is required by Move's borrow checker to construct `&mut treasury_cap`. No change needed, but consider adding a brief comment explaining upstream takes `&mut` without mutating.
descriptions: `register_pas_asset` takes `version: &Version` but never calls `version.check_is_valid()` directly — the version check is only performed when the caller constructs the `TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>`. If that auth was constructed in an earlier PTB (unlikely for `TreasuryAuth` which has drop but not store, so lives within one PTB), the version-at-call-time may differ from version-at-auth-construction. This matters only if the version changes mid-tx, which cannot happen. But the parameter is only used via the nested `register_transfer_template` which also does not call check_is_valid. So effectively the parameter is documented surface without runtime effect in this function.

register_pas_asset takes `version: &Version` but never invokes `version.check_is_valid()`. The safety argument is that `auth.assert_is_valid_for_treasury` exists and the caller had to mint a `TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>` which internally calls `version.check_is_valid()` (see auth::new_treasury_auth). However, since TreasuryAuth has `drop`, a previously-built auth could hypothetically be reused within the same PTB across modules, and the defensive pattern in the rest of this codebase is to always re-check version when the module is entered. Lack of re-check here makes the function rely on upstream invariants of auth.move and weakens the emergency-pause semantics.

The function takes `version: &Version` but never calls `version.check_is_valid()` on it. The guarantee comes only from `auth.assert_is_valid_for_treasury` being paired with `auth::new_treasury_auth` which did check version at creation. This depends on the contract that treasury auths are created in the same PTB — which is true today but is an implicit invariant. Downstream modules like `supply.move` follow the same pattern and this is consistent with existing Hadron style, however the function signature explicitly accepts `version` implying it is used, yet it is only passed through to `register_transfer_template` which also does not call `check_is_valid`.

register_pas_asset takes &Version but never calls version.check_is_valid(). The upstream TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission> (via auth::new_treasury_auth) does check version at creation, so in the golden path the check is present. However, with RegisterCoinPermission held in a role, a caller can construct TreasuryAuth once and, if it has drop only (it does), reuse is not possible across TXs — so golden path is safe. Still, the module signature implies a version gate that isn't enforced locally, and update_transfer_template has the same gap (version arg, unused in the function body itself). If the Auth lifecycle changes (e.g. future refactor reifies it as key+store), version-gating breaks silently.

Both functions accept `version: &Version` and pass it to `register_transfer_template`, which does not use it either (only the object::id(version) is read to embed in the PTB template). The version invariant is enforced transitively because the caller must have constructed a `TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>` via `auth::new_treasury_auth`, which calls `version.check_is_valid()`. This works today but is fragile: a future refactor that changes how Auth is created (or introduces a test-only helper) could silently skip the version check on the PAS admin path.

`register_pas_asset` receives `version: &Version` but never calls `version.check_is_valid()`. Validation only happens transitively when `auth` was constructed (`new_treasury_auth` asserts version). Since the auth is built within the same PTB and is `drop`-only, this is sufficient today, but the pattern diverges from other state-mutating public entrypoints (e.g. `treasury::create` calls `version.check_is_valid()` explicitly).
