The `update_transfer_template` function has the same gap.

`register_pas_asset` takes `mut treasury_cap: TreasuryCap<T>` but only passes `&mut treasury_cap` once (to `policy::new_for_currency`) and then moves the cap into `add_pas_coin_caps`. The `mut` binding is only needed because `policy::new_for_currency` takes `&mut TreasuryCap<C>`. This is correct usage — but once upstream confirms (per its signature) that it does not actually mutate the cap (the parameter is just `_cap: &mut TreasuryCap<C>` in `policy.move` L48 — prefixed with `_` so it is unused), Hadron can pass `&mut` to satisfy the signature without the local `mut` binding. Minor polish.
impacts: Subtle invariant: the version check for this code path comes only from auth construction, not from the function itself. If any future refactor drops the auth-side version check, this function becomes unguarded. Also, `register_transfer_template` internal fn takes `version` but does not use it.
If version is blocked mid-PTB (theoretical) or if future refactors of auth drop the version check, register_pas_asset would not fail fast. Weakens defense-in-depth for emergency upgrades.
If a future refactor lets a caller re-use an older Auth across a version-mismatch boundary, this function would execute in a blocked version. Low risk today.
Fragile versioning discipline; a refactor that decouples auth creation from version check would silently bypass version for PAS registration.
Defense-in-depth gap; future refactors could regress version gating silently. No current exploit.

Defense-in-depth regression: if a future refactor changes how `Auth` proofs are minted (e.g. cached, re-used, or constructed via a test helper), a public state-mutating function could be invoked against an invalidated Version without detection.
Reader confusion: implies the cap is mutated when it is not. Purely stylistic.

title: pkg() returns '' fallback — all PAS TS operations silently compose invalid PTBs if deploy-output missing
recommendations: Make `pkg()` throw synchronously if both v1 and latest are empty: `if (!CONSTANTS.package.v1 && !CONSTANTS.package.latest) throw new Error('No package ID configured — run deploy first or set CONSTANTS.package')`. Or change the fallback semantics to force callers to pass a packageId.
In `pkg()`, `assert(!!CONSTANTS.package.latest || !!CONSTANTS.package.v1, 'Hadron package not deployed — run `bun run deploy` first')`.

Add an invariant helper: `export function assertDeployed(): string { const p = CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1; if (!p) throw new Error('Hadron not deployed — run `bun run deploy` first'); return p; }` and replace `pkg()` call sites with `assertDeployed()`.
Throw a clear error in `pkg()` (or at module load) if both `latest` and `v1` are empty strings. Alternatively, have `pkg()` assert a 66-char hex prefix.
Make `pkg()` assert: `if (!id) throw new Error('Hadron package not configured — run deploy first')`, with the same guard at `CONSTANTS.versionObjectId` usage sites.

Make `pkg()` throw if the package ID is empty: `export function pkg() { const p = CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1; if (!p) throw new Error('Hadron package not deployed — run `bun run deploy`'); return p; }`. Apply same to `CONSTANTS.versionObjectId` / `namespaceObjectId` fallbacks.
descriptions: `pkg()` reads `CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1` and returns `''` if neither is populated (i.e., deploy-output.json is missing). Every PAS operation in `pas.ts` uses this via template strings like `${pkg()}::permissions::MintPermission`, producing invalid type strings like `::permissions::MintPermission`. The on-chain transaction will fail with an obscure MoveCompilation/type-parse error rather than a clean 'deployment artifact missing' error.

`pkg()` returns `CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1` which defaults to `''` when `deploy-output.json` is absent. No guard warns the operator that the empty string is being passed as a package ID to `@mysten/sui` transaction building. All `pas.ts` entrypoints (`registerPasAsset`, `deployAndRegisterPasAsset`, `addToWhitelist`, etc.) call `pkg()` and pass it into `package: pkg()` arguments. If empty, the SDK will produce an invalid type tag or a confusing RPC error.

`pkg()` falls back to `CONSTANTS.package.v1` which defaults to `''` if `deploy-output.json` is missing or the field is empty (line 53). Every `tx.add(...)` call in pas.ts uses `package: pkg()` — if pkg() returns '', the produced move call has a blank package id. Some SDKs error sensibly, others produce a cryptic Sui RPC error. There is no up-front check that deploy data was loaded.

`pkg()` returns `CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1`. Both default to empty string (lines 52-55: `deployed.package ?? { v1: '', latest: '' }`). If `deploy-output.json` is missing or malformed, every caller silently constructs move calls with `package: ''` — producing a Transaction that will fail at sign-time or publish-time with a vague validation error. No guard throws a human-readable error.

`pkg()` returns `CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1`, both of which default to `''` when the deploy-output file is missing or unreadable. Every PAS operation script (`registerPasAsset`, `addToWhitelist`, etc.) calls `pkg()` and passes the empty string directly into `package:` of Move calls. Downstream, `@mysten/sui/transactions` will either error obscurely or emit a call to the zero-package address.

`pkg()` returns `CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1`. Both default to `''` if `readDeployOutput()` returns nothing (see L52-55). All PAS script functions (`registerPasAsset`, `addToWhitelist`, `mintPasTokens`) construct moveCalls with `package: pkg()` — if `pkg()` is `''`, the PTB is assembled with an empty package address. This will fail at execution time with an unclear error, rather than a clean upfront check.
impacts: Confusing UX when the script is run before a successful deploy. The actual error occurs only at signAndExecute, far from the root cause.
Poor operator UX — silent misconfiguration shows up as an opaque SDK error.
Poor developer experience; misleading errors when a user runs operations without having deployed first.
Operator footgun; every PAS script will fail with a confusing error when deploy output is missing, instead of a clear "deploy-output.json not found or missing packageId" message.
A misconfigured deploy-output results in an immediate tx failure that looks like a network or SDK problem, not a config problem. No early fail-fast.
Operator debugging time: a missing `deploy-output.json` surfaces as a deep PTB error instead of "run `bun run deploy` first".

title: Whitelist addition on non-whitelisted-before address has no initial-state event fidelity
recommendations: Add `account_id: ID` to PasBurned (and PasSeized) so the event is self-contained. Same concern as R2-010.
Add `treasury_id: ID` to all PAS whitelist events, following the RoleCreated/RoleAssigned precedent.

Add an `admin: address` (or `sender: address`) field to `PasBurned` and `PasSeized` and populate with `ctx.sender()` inside `pas_supply::burn` / `pas_supply::seize`. Also consider adding the auth'd permission type as a phantom marker for filter-ability.

Add `treasury_id: ID` field to every PAS-emitted event struct and thread it through the corresponding `emit_*` helpers. Similar issue may exist in phase-1 events but that is out of scope — the PAS events are new, so fix them here.
Fold the initial booleans into `WhitelistAdded<T> { addr, can_send, can_receive }`. Leaves the other two events unchanged.

Add `minter: address` to `PasMinted`, `redeemer: address` to `PasBurned`, `seizer: address` to `PasSeized`. Update emit helpers and call-sites in pas_supply.move to pass `ctx.sender()`. This may require threading `ctx: &TxContext` through to the event helpers (already available in burn/mint; seize takes no ctx — add one).
descriptions: `PasBurned<T> { from: address, amount: u64 }` records only the amount burned. For audit trails and reconciliation, it's useful to know the account's balance before/after. `from` here is the wallet owner (from `request.data().owner()`), not the account ID. Again, like R2-010, indexers cannot distinguish which account was burned from without walking the Account objects.

`WhitelistAdded<T>`, `WhitelistRemoved<T>`, `WhitelistSendUpdated<T>`, `WhitelistReceiveUpdated<T>` carry only `addr` (and for the updated variants `enabled`). They do not include the `treasury_id`, so indexers tracking compliance across multiple institution treasuries cannot route the event. Role events (L29-39) include `treasury_id` — so there is precedent in the codebase for this shape.

`PasBurned<T>` records `from: address` and `amount: u64` but does not record the admin address (i.e. `ctx.sender()`) that invoked `pas_supply::burn`. `Redeemed<T>` (non-PAS) does include `sender`. This inconsistency complicates admin-action audit: there's no on-chain way to distinguish which compliance officer executed a given forced burn.

Every PAS event (PasAssetRegistered, WhitelistAdded/Removed, PasTransferApproved, PasMinted, PasBurned, PasSeized, WhitelistSend/ReceiveUpdated) is parameterized only by phantom T. The namespace-scoped Hadron design allows multiple Treasury instances (one per institution). If two institutions register the same coin type T (e.g. a shared template asset), subscribers cannot distinguish `WhitelistAdded<T> { addr }` between institutions.

`WhitelistAdded<T>` carries only `addr`. `WhitelistRemoved<T>` is identical. The `WhitelistSendUpdated<T>`/`WhitelistReceiveUpdated<T>` events carry `enabled`. Consumers reconstructing state from events cannot tell the difference between 'addr was added and defaulted can_send=true' and 'addr was added and never had send toggled'. (Related to R9-010 above.)

`PasMinted<T>` carries `recipient, amount` but not the minter. `PasBurned<T>` carries `from, amount` but not the redeemer. `PasSeized<T>` carries `from, to, amount` but not the seizer. Non-PAS equivalents `Minted<T>` include `minter: address` (events.move L79-82). This inconsistency makes PAS activity harder to audit.
impacts: Weaker audit trail; regulator / compliance indexers need extra reads to reconcile.
Indexer / compliance-reporting friction.
Compliance / audit traceability. Not security-critical, but regulatory reviews usually want to identify the acting admin.
Observability / auditability gap: front-end dashboards, compliance systems, and regulators cannot attribute state changes to the correct Treasury. Correct behaviour but under-specified events.
Low — off-chain indexers can query on-chain state. But event-sourced architectures take a hit.
Weakened audit trail for regulated operations exactly where accountability matters most.

title: pas_supply::mint does not assert is_pas_coin — relies on missing whitelist DOF for non-PAS fail-closed
recommendations: Move `assert!(amount > 0, ...)` up to be consistent with `pas_supply::mint` which asserts amount first.

Drop the pause gate on seize (it's an admin/enforcement action). Keep it on mint and burn. Alternatively, document that seize is always allowed regardless of pause, and enforce a SeizePermission that is never held by the same role as pause to avoid unintended gating.

Either (a) split into separate `BurnPausePermission` / `SeizePausePermission` flags that are independent of the mint/redeem pause, or (b) explicitly scope the pause to mint-only in burn/seize and document the design. Current spec text (context §7.2) requires `burn` to check `not paused` but does not specify which pause.
Drop the `assert_mint_redeem_enabled` precondition in seize, or gate it behind a separate `SeizePause` flag. Document why the chosen pause scope is correct.
Remove the `assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>()` check from `seize`, or gate it behind a separate `assert_seize_enabled<T>()` flag that defaults to enabled and is controlled by a different permission.
Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin)` at the top of `pas_supply::mint`, `pas_supply::burn`, and `pas_supply::seize` for parity with supply.move's guard.
descriptions: `pas_supply::burn` reads `request.data().owner()` and `funds().value()`, then `assert!(amount > 0)`, then `request.approve(ClawbackApproval<T>())`, then `clawback_funds::resolve`. The checks come BEFORE approve. OK here. But `approve_transfer` (pas_transfer.move L48) calls `request.approve(TransferApproval<T>())` AFTER the whitelist / amount asserts — that pattern is correct. In `seize` (L76-93), same order. However the `amount > 0` assert in `burn` protects against burning zero and emitting a spurious `PasBurned` event — but note that `clawback_funds::resolve` itself does not reject zero balances. If `amount == 0`, burn would abort before consuming the request, but since `request` is a hot potato passed by value, the abort correctly rolls back. This is correct; flagging as observation — consider moving the `amount > 0` check as the very first statement before `request.data().owner()` reads, for predictable error order.

Both burn<T> and seize<T> assert !treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>(). Burn is the PAS equivalent of redeem — OK to pause. But seize is a compliance/enforcement action (e.g. suspend a sanctioned address). If compliance has paused mint/redeem (e.g. while investigating), they cannot seize funds from a malicious actor until they unpause — which is counter to operational intent. Usually an enforcement action should be available exactly when the system is paused.

burn asserts `treasury.assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>()`. Under normal Hadron semantics this is consistent with regulated `supply::redeem`. However, pause is often triggered during an incident precisely when an emergency burn is most needed (e.g. to destroy funds that were seized from a sanctioned account). Coupling burn to the mint/redeem pause means the same flag that stops minting also disables the remedial action. This is the same class of concern as LEADS §4 but applies to the burn path, not just approve_transfer.

seize calls `treasury.assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>()`. Seize is a compliance action used against de-whitelisted holders — it should continue to work even when ordinary mint/redeem is paused for operational reasons. Coupling it to the same pause flag creates a DoS against compliance during a pause. Note the ticket design intent (context §7.2) says 'seize can extract funds from de-whitelisted addresses' which implies seize is a control-plane operation distinct from mint/redeem throughput.

`seize` calls `treasury.assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>()` at entry. Operationally, mint/redeem pause is a *commercial* lever (stop issuance) while seize is a *compliance* lever (regulator order, sanctions). Conflating the two means a treasurer who pauses issuance of a PAS asset also loses the ability to seize funds — the typical operational sequence when an incident is detected is the exact opposite (pause first, seize second).

`mint<T>` requires `Auth<MintPermission, T>`, which is mintable for any registered coin — PAS or regulated. If somebody pushes a regulated coin T via pas_supply::mint, the flow: (1) `assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>` OK, (2) `assert_can_receive<T>(account.owner())` aborts in `dynamic_object_field::borrow` because `WhitelistKey<T>` DOF does not exist for a non-PAS coin. Fail-closed, but with an opaque framework error. The complementary `supply::mint` does have `assert!(!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), EPasCoinNotAllowed)` (supply.move L35). The reverse check is missing.
impacts: No correctness bug; ordering is a minor readability issue.

Operational lockout: pause + seize are mutually exclusive. In an incident-response scenario this can force a dangerous ordering (unpause to seize, then re-pause, with a window where the attacker can act).

During an incident pause, a compliance officer with RedeemPermission cannot destroy PAS balances even after they have been clawed back from a bad actor. Seize-to-treasury-then-burn becomes impossible.
Pausing mint/redeem also disables the most likely use of seize (recovering funds from a now-blocked holder).

Compliance officer cannot execute a seize while mint/redeem is paused, delaying regulatory action. Workaround requires unpausing, seizing, repausing — widening the window where attackers can mint/redeem.
Inconsistent error handling symmetry: PAS coin mint via `supply::mint` → clean `EPasCoinNotAllowed`; regulated coin mint via `pas_supply::mint` → cryptic framework abort.

title: pas_transfer::approve_transfer validates wallet sender/recipient, but balance goes to recipient_account_id
recommendations: Document explicitly in pas_transfer.move that the whitelist is wallet-scoped and enforced via PAS required_approvals + template autoresolve. Consider also validating `sender_account_id` / `recipient_account_id` belong to the same namespace (defense-in-depth).

Add a module-level comment in pas_transfer.move documenting that 'sender'/'recipient' are wallet OR object addresses (quoting upstream) and that object-owned account whitelisting requires adding the object-synthetic address, not the account ID.

Add a cross-check assertion verifying that recipient_account_id is the derived account ID for the claimed recipient address: e.g. `assert!(namespace::account_address_from_id(namespace_id, recipient) == recipient_account_id.to_address(), ...)`. If not feasible without &Namespace arg, document the reliance explicitly in the module docstring.

Add a comment block in pas_transfer.move noting: 'Sender/recipient here are WALLET addresses per pas::send_funds. The upstream `resolve_balance` sends funds to `recipient_account_id = derive(namespace, recipient)`. Whitelist enforcement against the wallet address is correct iff account-id derivation is a pure function of (namespace_id, wallet_address). See pas::account::internal_send_balance.' This documents the boundary for future reviewers.

Add an explicit comment at top of `approve_transfer` pointing to upstream `send_funds::resolve_balance` semantics and documenting the wallet ↔ account-ID assumption. Consider, belt-and-braces, asserting `request.data().sender_account_id() == pas::namespace::account_address_from_id(namespace_id, sender).to_id()` — but this would require `&Namespace` which is expensive. Today's design is acceptable but fragile if upstream drifts.

Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin)` at the top of `approve_transfer`. This makes the precondition explicit and fails-closed with a clean error. Also add a comment referencing upstream `policy::new_for_currency` L51 which enforces one-policy-per-type-via-namespace.

Add a defensive assertion that the recipient_account_id equals the derivation of recipient: assert `request.data().recipient_account_id() == namespace_account_id(namespace_id, recipient)` or at least document the invariant. Also validate `sender_account_id` similarly. Reference /Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/account.move L170-185 and send_funds.move L62-71.
descriptions: approve_transfer checks `treasury.assert_can_send<T>(sender)` and `assert_can_receive<T>(recipient)` using the wallet addresses from `SendFunds::sender()` and `::recipient()`. Per upstream `send_funds.move` L69-70 (`/Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/requests/send_funds.move`), resolve_balance sends funds to `recipient_account_id.to_address()`, NOT the wallet address. This is consistent with upstream PAS intent (wallet address = logical identity; account_id = storage address), but means the whitelist is a wallet-level whitelist and a de-whitelisted wallet could still have its PAS account receive funds if the caller bypasses the approval. Because the autoresolve template always invokes approve_transfer, this is safe in the happy path — but if Templates is ever modified, a direct SendFunds construction could bypass the whitelist.

Upstream send_funds.move documents that sender/recipient are the wallet or object addresses, NOT the account addresses, and `resolve_balance` sends funds to recipient_account_id.to_address() (the PAS account shared object ID), not to recipient. Hadron's whitelist is keyed by wallet address (good — matches spec), but the mapping between wallet address and PAS account is not enforced here. Because PAS accounts are permission-less to create (pas::account::create), anyone can create an Account for any address; approve_transfer validates the wallet side, but the balance lands in the account object at object::id(account).to_address(). For a UID-owned account (object owner), the wallet address is a synthetic uid.to_inner().to_address() that must also appear on the whitelist. This is documented in lead #10 but not in code — operators must know this to whitelist object-owned accounts.

The SendFunds<T> struct exposes sender_account_id() and recipient_account_id() but Hadron's approve_transfer uses only sender()/recipient(). A hostile PTB constructor could present a well-formed send_funds request where sender (wallet) is whitelisted but recipient_account_id points to an account owned by a different (possibly adversarial) wallet. Upstream send_funds::resolve_balance deposits into recipient_account_id.to_address() — i.e. the account shared object — so the account's owner cannot silently redirect funds. However, this means the whitelist check on `recipient` (the wallet address) only validates the ostensible recipient, while the actual balance lands in whatever PAS Account object was referenced; if those two diverge (malicious caller passes a mismatched recipient/recipient_account_id pair), the wallet-address whitelist check can pass while funds end up at a different account.

Hadron validates `request.data().sender()` / `request.data().recipient()` which are the WALLET addresses per upstream send_funds.move L22/L24. But `send_funds::resolve_balance<C>` (upstream send_funds.move L69-70) pulls funds to `recipient_account_id.to_address()` — the PAS Account ID, NOT the wallet address. This is correct per upstream design (LEADS §§1, context §6.1): the wallet is whitelisted, funds go to the wallet's PAS account. But the mismatch is subtle and worth a module-level comment explaining the invariant. A malicious namespace.move upstream change that decouples account-id-from-wallet-address would silently break the whitelist guarantee.

Upstream `pas::send_funds::resolve_balance` uses `recipient_account_id.to_address()` to deposit the balance, not the wallet address (`/Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/requests/send_funds.move` L69-70). Hadron's `approve_transfer` validates the **wallet** `sender`/`recipient` against the whitelist, not the account IDs. This is the intended upstream pattern (wallet addresses are the compliance surface; account IDs are deterministic from namespace + wallet), but a reviewer should confirm the wallet ↔ account ID mapping is bijective and uniquely derived per namespace, otherwise a compliance gap exists.

`approve_transfer<T>` is permissionless (intentional — autoresolve callable by the PAS SDK). It receives `treasury: &Treasury` and consults its whitelist for sender/recipient. There is no assertion that THIS treasury actually issued PAS T. If no treasury issued T as PAS, the underlying `dynamic_object_field::borrow` inside `assert_can_send/assert_can_receive` will abort. But more subtly: if a `Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>>` exists for a T issued by some other institution's treasury but an attacker points `approve_transfer` at a different treasury that also happens to hold a whitelist DOF keyed by T, the behavior is undefined at the treasury-ownership level. In practice, only one Treasury can hold `TreasuryCap<T>` at any time, so only one treasury can carry the whitelist DOF keyed by `WhitelistKey<T>` — upstream OTW uniqueness rescues this. But the code does not document or assert this invariant.

Hadron's `approve_transfer` validates `request.data().sender()` and `request.data().recipient()` — these are wallet/object addresses. Upstream `send_funds::resolve_balance` deposits funds at `recipient_account_id.to_address()` (see /Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/requests/send_funds.move L69-70). The recipient_account_id is set when the Request is created by `account.send_balance` as `namespace::account_address_from_id(from.namespace_id, to)` — deterministically derived from the wallet `to`. So the wallet check is effectively equivalent. HOWEVER, `account.unsafe_send_balance` (upstream account.move L111-123) lets the caller pass an arbitrary `recipient_address`, which becomes both the wallet field AND the derivation seed for the recipient_account_id. The wallet check still holds in that case. So the design is sound under the current upstream contract, but it is implicit and could silently break if upstream changes the relationship between `recipient` and `recipient_account_id`.
impacts: Relies on PAS Templates + required_approvals chain to enforce whitelist; a malicious namespace-admin-like attack or a future upstream refactor could split these guarantees.
Whitelist usability foot-gun when object accounts are involved. Not a security break — upstream account::deposit_balance goes to object::id(account) regardless — but a documentation gap.

Depends on upstream invariants: account::send_balance internally sets recipient_account_id = namespace::account_address_from_id(namespace_id, to) — which is derived from `to` — so the pair is consistent. But unsafe_send_balance and direct request::new callers may not maintain this. If the upstream API exposes send_funds::new publicly (it is public(package) inside pas), the pair is safe. Surface as low because the upstream API currently enforces consistency.
No current bug. Defensive concern if upstream derivation changes. Spec in context §6.1 flags this as the intended pattern.

Theoretically: if an adversary could present a Request whose `sender`/`recipient` are whitelisted wallets but whose `sender_account_id`/`recipient_account_id` belong to different wallets (not the derived ones), funds could land in unintended accounts. Upstream `account::send_balance` and `unsafe_send_balance` construct account IDs deterministically via `namespace::account_address_from_id`, so the bijection holds — but future upstream changes could break this.

Silent reliance on OTW uniqueness. If any future code path allows a treasury to hold a stray `WhitelistKey<T>` DOF without owning TreasuryCap<T>, the whitelist of that treasury could gate a policy that a different treasury owns. Current code prevents it, but the surface is fragile.

Tight coupling to upstream PAS internal invariants. If upstream introduces a send variant where `recipient` and `recipient_account_id` can be decoupled (e.g. a sponsor-address variant, or a forwarding account), Hadron's whitelist would enforce on the wrong address without local code changes.

title: treasury.move pas fns use `let wl: &Table<address, WhitelistEntry>` at 4 sites — factor out
recommendations: Introduce private `fun wl<T>(treasury: &Treasury)` and `fun wl_mut<T>(...)` helpers that (a) assert is_pas_coin and (b) return the Table reference. Refactor the 6 public functions to use them.
Add a table-driven test that walks through each allowed transition and each forbidden one, asserting both state and the emitted event.
Remove the `policy_cap<T>` function, or if it is meant to support a future admin operation (re-setting approvals), gate it behind `Auth<RegisterCoinPermission, T>` and document the intended use.
descriptions: Six functions (is_whitelisted, can_send, can_receive, add_to_whitelist, remove_from_whitelist, set_whitelist_send, set_whitelist_receive) each re-fetch the Table<address, WhitelistEntry> via dynamic_object_field::borrow[_mut]. Factor out two helpers — `fun wl<T>(treasury: &Treasury): &Table<address, WhitelistEntry>` and `fun wl_mut<T>(treasury: &mut Treasury): &mut Table<address, WhitelistEntry>` — both of which assert is_pas_coin and return the Table. This would reduce duplication and enforce the is_pas_coin check centrally (also fixing R4-003 and R4-004 in one place).

The whitelist state machine (add/remove/set_send/set_receive) is net-new and stores `WhitelistEntry { can_send, can_receive }` per address. No unit tests exercise transitions such as remove-then-add, set_send on already-removed address (expects `ENotInWhitelist`), set_receive after set_send, double-add (expects `EAlreadyWhitelisted`).

`policy_cap<T>(&Treasury)` is defined as `public(package)` but has zero call sites in the Hadron codebase. The PolicyCap is stored in `add_pas_coin_caps` but never retrieved — Hadron's entire clawback/transfer flow resolves via `pas::clawback_funds::resolve` and `pas::send_funds::resolve_balance`, which take a `&Policy<T>` shared object, not the PolicyCap.
impacts: Code quality / maintainability. Reduces the attack surface of forgetting to add the is_pas_coin check to a newly-added whitelist operation.
State-transition bugs ship undetected.

API surface bloat. If the getter is later called and the caller mutates the policy (e.g. `set_required_approval`), it would bypass Hadron's permission model since anyone with package access could call it. Removing it tightens the attack surface.

title: supply::mint / redeem PAS guard is post-condition to cap acquisition check order
recommendations: No change required. Optionally move the `is_pas_coin` check before `amount > 0` so that generic-type validation happens before input validation (more common convention).

Add tests: register a PAS asset for coin T then attempt `supply::mint<T>` and `supply::redeem<T>` — both must abort with `EPasCoinNotAllowed`. Also add the positive-path test: a non-PAS regulated coin works as before.
descriptions: In both `supply::mint` and `supply::redeem`, the sequence is: `amount > 0` → `is_pas_coin` → `assert_mint_redeem_enabled` → cap borrow. The is_pas_coin check is fine, but note: an already-registered regulated coin cannot become a PAS coin (registration paths are mutually exclusive by the treasury_cap DOF uniqueness check). So the practical effect of the EPasCoinNotAllowed guard is to hard-fail when someone calls supply::mint with a T whose policy_cap DOF was set via add_pas_coin_caps. Correct. The minor quality concern is naming: the guard phrases PAS registration as a "not allowed" error rather than clarifying the dispatch rule. Consider a `#[deprecated]` / redirect suggestion in the error. (Already does in the error message — acceptable.)

The `!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()` guard was added to both `mint` and `redeem` in `supply.move` to ensure PAS coins are forced through `pas_supply`. This is a new control boundary and its positive/negative paths are not tested.
impacts: None from a correctness standpoint. Pure readability suggestion.
If a future refactor removes the guard, the regression ships silently. Also no test that `EPasCoinNotAllowed` fires with the expected string.

title: register_transfer_template uses TransferApproval<T> package address — post-upgrade templates go stale
recommendations: (1) Document in the module header that post-upgrade `update_transfer_template` MUST be called per asset; provide a script that enumerates PAS assets in a treasury and calls update for each. (2) Consider emitting a `TemplateUpdated` event so indexers can flag stale templates. (3) Longer-term: explore a derived-object-based template pointer that is recomputed from current package at resolve time (requires upstream PAS cooperation).
descriptions: `register_transfer_template<T>` computes `approval_type.address_string()` from `TransferApproval<T>` and hard-codes it into the template's `package_id`. When Hadron is upgraded to a new package address, `TransferApproval<T>` resolves to the new address but the already-registered template still points to the old package. `update_transfer_template<T>` exists to fix this, but it's not automatic: after any package upgrade, an operator MUST call `update_transfer_template` for every PAS asset or the SDK autoresolve will call the old package. If the old package still works, users silently bypass any new logic; if it's been made no-op or reverted, transfers fail.
impacts: Post-upgrade operational hazard. Without tooling to batch-update templates for every registered PAS asset, some assets will have stale templates. If audits focus on the new package, stale-template transfers evade inspection.

title: WhitelistManagePermission collapses four spec permissions — loses least-privilege granularity
recommendations: Either (a) document and sign off on the decision to collapse the permissions, or (b) split into `WhitelistAddPermission`, `WhitelistRemovePermission`, `WhitelistUpdatePermission` to align with the spec. If collapsed, rename the permission to match the spec's semantic intent (e.g., `WhitelistAdminPermission`).

Either (a) split back into `WhitelistAddPermission` + `WhitelistRemovePermission` (+ optionally `WhitelistUpdatePermission`) to match spec, or (b) update the design spec in Notion to reflect the single-permission decision and flag in release notes.

Either (a) split into `WhitelistAddPermission`, `WhitelistRemovePermission`, `WhitelistToggleSendPermission`, `WhitelistToggleReceivePermission`, or (b) explicitly document in the PR and spec that the collapse is accepted. Mark the spec as updated.

Either (a) restore WhitelistAddPermission and WhitelistRemovePermission as separate permission types and wire through pas_admin, or (b) document in the module docstring that WhitelistManagePermission is intentional and explain the access-control trade-off.

Either (a) document the simplification in the module doc with an explicit note that collapses the spec'd permissions, or (b) split into two permissions to match the spec. If split, update scripts/src/constants.ts to expose both.

Either (a) leave as-is and update the spec / Notion to match code, or (b) split into `WhitelistAddPermission` and `WhitelistRemovePermission` (and `WhitelistSetFlagsPermission`) if segregation-of-duty is on the roadmap.

If the intended operational model is truly single-permission, confirm with the design owner and close the spec drift in documentation. Otherwise, split into `WhitelistAddPermission` / `WhitelistRemovePermission` and pass each into the corresponding `pas_admin` entry-point. Same consideration for `set_whitelist_*` — arguably a third `WhitelistTogglePermission`.

Either (a) split into `WhitelistAddPermission`, `WhitelistRemovePermission`, `WhitelistSetSendPermission`, `WhitelistSetReceivePermission` per spec, or (b) keep the single permission but document the deviation in the module and confirm with the product team that the simplification is acceptable.
Either (a) split into two permission markers to match the spec, or (b) update the spec and the PR description to acknowledge the rename and justify the consolidation.

If the spec drift is intentional (simpler operations model), document in the module header with a comment that `WhitelistManagePermission` is deliberately coarse-grained. If finer granularity is desired, split into three permissions: `WhitelistAddPermission`, `WhitelistRemovePermission`, `WhitelistModifyPermission` (for set_whitelist_send/receive). Align with orchestrator spec excerpt (section 7.2).
descriptions: The 'PAS Modules' Notion spec defines distinct `WhitelistAddPermission` and `WhitelistRemovePermission` for `add_to_whitelist` / `remove_from_whitelist` / `set_whitelist_send` / `set_whitelist_receive`. The implementation collapses these into a single `WhitelistManagePermission` granting all four operations. This is a deliberate code/spec divergence documented in the context bundle (§7.2 'Spec drift note'), but it is a compliance-relevant decision worth explicit sign-off from the spec owner: a single role with 'add+remove+set' loses the separation-of-duties pattern where one team can whitelist but not de-whitelist (or vice versa).

The Notion spec defines `WhitelistAddPermission` and `WhitelistRemovePermission` separately; this PR introduces a single `WhitelistManagePermission` covering both add and remove (and set_send/set_receive). This is a valid simplification but drifts from the specification and reduces the granularity operators can use to model a separation-of-duties role (e.g., onboarder-only vs. offboarder-only).

The Notion spec (§7.2) calls for `WhitelistAddPermission` and `WhitelistRemovePermission` as separate permissions. The code collapses both (plus set_whitelist_send/receive toggles) into one `WhitelistManagePermission`. This reduces RBAC granularity — an operator role that should only be able to onboard (add) addresses also gains the ability to remove and de-toggle. This may be intentional for simplicity, but it is spec drift.

Per _context.md §7.2 (Notion spec drift), the original spec defined separate WhitelistAddPermission and WhitelistRemovePermission. Code collapses both to WhitelistManagePermission. This is a knowingly documented drift, but it removes the operational distinction between 'onboarding operator' (add-only) and 'exit/compliance officer' (remove-only). Least-privilege role design is lost.

Per the PAS Modules spec (context §7.2), `add_to_whitelist<T>` and `remove_from_whitelist<T>` require separate `WhitelistAddPermission` and `WhitelistRemovePermission`. The code collapses them into a single `WhitelistManagePermission` which is used for add, remove, set_send, and set_receive. This is a deliberate simplification but diverges from the spec and reduces the granularity available when assigning compliance roles.

The PAS spec (per `_context.md` §7.2) defines distinct `WhitelistAddPermission` and `WhitelistRemovePermission`. The implementation uses a single `WhitelistManagePermission` for both add/remove and for `set_whitelist_send`/`set_whitelist_receive`. This is a deliberate simplification and aligns with the Hadron Blueprint "Permissions" naming, but any segregation-of-duty policy (e.g., "junior compliance officer can add, only senior can remove") is not expressible.

Per the Notion PAS Modules spec, `add_to_whitelist` should take `Auth<WhitelistAddPermission, T>` and `remove_from_whitelist` should take `Auth<WhitelistRemovePermission, T>` — i.e., separate permissions for add and remove. The code collapses both into a single `WhitelistManagePermission` that also controls `set_whitelist_send`/`set_whitelist_receive`. This is consistent with the LEADS file noting spec drift.

Notion spec for Hadron phase 2 defines two permissions: `WhitelistAddPermission` and `WhitelistRemovePermission`, separating the power to add from the power to remove (so a role can grant additions without granting revocations). Code collapses them into a single `WhitelistManagePermission` that also controls `set_whitelist_send`/`set_whitelist_receive`. This is a conscious deviation but surfaces as a compliance-policy question for the institution.

The Notion spec for PAS modules splits whitelist authority into `WhitelistAddPermission` / `WhitelistRemovePermission` (and treats `set_whitelist_send/receive` as `WhitelistAddPermission`-gated). The code collapses these into a single `WhitelistManagePermission`, which is semantically broader.

Per the design spec distilled in the orchestrator context: `add_to_whitelist` should require `Auth<WhitelistAddPermission, T>`; `remove_from_whitelist` should require `Auth<WhitelistRemovePermission, T>`; `set_whitelist_send/receive` should require `Auth<WhitelistAddPermission, T>` (implicitly a modify-permission). The implementation uses a single `WhitelistManagePermission` for all four. This is a documented spec-drift — the consolidator should surface it as info/low.
impacts: Reduced principle-of-least-privilege granularity. An operator with whitelist-add authority also has whitelist-remove and whitelist-modify, which may not be the intended RBAC split per the design spec.
Operational: institutions cannot separate the permission to add addresses from the permission to remove them, which is a common regulatory pattern.
Coarser RBAC than spec intends. If a regulator or compliance workflow differentiates onboarding vs offboarding responsibilities, this conflation is not possible.
Cannot enforce separation between onboarding and offboarding roles; onboarding staff can also de-whitelist. In regulated deployments this blurs audit trails.
Operators cannot issue a role that can add-but-not-remove from the whitelist. Not a security bug — just reduced expressiveness.
Design drift from spec. Blocks finer-grained compliance role design.
Organizations that want segregation of duties (e.g., one team adds, another removes) cannot express that in the current role model without introducing a new permission type.

Reduces flexibility in role design; a compliance officer role granted permission to add cannot be prevented from also removing. Institutions with separation-of-duties requirements may need to model both.

Operational risk: a role scoped 'add-only' in spec is necessarily clawback-capable in code. Audit findings and compliance documents that cite spec role names will not map 1:1 to on-chain roles. Documented as a known spec drift per §10 leads #1 of the context bundle.

Operators cannot assign add-only, remove-only, or modify-only whitelist roles. A compliance officer who should only add addresses can also revoke addresses, which may conflict with internal separation-of-duty policies.

title: add_to_whitelist defaults both can_send=true and can_receive=true — surprising default for receive-only use cases
recommendations: Either (a) extend `WhitelistAdded<T>` to include can_send, can_receive fields, or (b) guarantee in a doc comment that default is (true, true) and add a reminder to update the event if the default changes.
Add a variant `add_to_whitelist_with<T>(treasury, addr, can_send, can_receive)` to support atomic onboarding with custom capability bits.

Change the signature to `add_to_whitelist<T>(treasury, addr, can_send, can_receive)`. Wire through pas_admin::add_to_whitelist with two bools (default true/true for backward compat via a keyword or separate entry).

Overload `add_to_whitelist<T>` with an `entry: WhitelistEntry` form, or add a variant `add_to_whitelist_with_permissions<T>(can_send, can_receive)` that lets the operator set the initial state atomically. Alternatively, document that add+set must be batched into the same PTB.

Overload or extend: `add_to_whitelist_with_flags<T>(treasury, auth, addr, can_send: bool, can_receive: bool)` that sets both bits atomically. Keep the current function as a sugar wrapper that calls the new one with (true, true).

Provide an `add_to_whitelist_with_flags<T>(treasury, auth, addr, can_send, can_receive)` entry in pas_admin.move that initializes the entry with explicit flags in a single call. Keep the default-both `add_to_whitelist` for the common case.
descriptions: `add_to_whitelist` inserts `WhitelistEntry { can_send: true, can_receive: true }`, and `pas_admin::add_to_whitelist` emits `WhitelistAdded<T> { addr }`. The event does NOT carry the initial send/receive booleans. Consumers watching only add/remove events cannot reconstruct the full whitelist state without also watching `WhitelistSendUpdated` / `WhitelistReceiveUpdated`. Because the default is `(true, true)`, current-state reconstruction is possible, but if the default is ever changed to `(false, false)` or a future change lets callers pass custom defaults, event consumers will silently miss state.

New whitelist entries default to `{ can_send: true, can_receive: true }`. There is no variant that onboards an address as receive-only (common for newly-created sub-accounts that should accept tokens but not yet send). A compliance officer wanting a receive-only onboarding must add-then-toggle-send-off in two transactions, during which the address is briefly able to send.

`add_to_whitelist<T>` inserts `WhitelistEntry { can_send: true, can_receive: true }` with no parameter for the caller to pick. If a compliance flow wants to whitelist an address for receive-only (e.g. a cold wallet that must not send out), the caller must first `add_to_whitelist` (granting both), then `set_whitelist_send(false)`. This is a two-tx flow that briefly puts the system into a state where the address has send-power it shouldn't. Better: take `can_send: bool, can_receive: bool` as parameters (or add a `add_with_flags` variant).

`add_to_whitelist<T>` always sets both `can_send` and `can_receive` to true on initial insertion. There is no API to atomically whitelist with only one direction enabled. A compliance officer who needs to onboard a receive-only address must first `add_to_whitelist` (grants both), then `set_whitelist_send(false)`. Between the two calls, the address transiently holds send permission.

`add_to_whitelist<T>` hard-codes `WhitelistEntry { can_send: true, can_receive: true }`. There is no constructor that lets the admin add an address with only one of the two permissions enabled. Callers must add-then-disable, which costs two transactions and emits two events (one WhitelistAdded + one WhitelistSendUpdated or WhitelistReceiveUpdated).

`add_to_whitelist` always initializes `WhitelistEntry { can_send: true, can_receive: true }`. There is no variant to add an address as receive-only (e.g. for an entity that should receive distributions but not transfer out). A compliance officer who wants to add a receive-only address must call `add_to_whitelist` then `set_whitelist_send(addr, false)` — two transactions (unless batched in a PTB), with a brief window where the address has send privileges it should not have.
impacts: Weak event observability; fragile if defaults change in a future revision.
Operational window where a newly added address has full send+receive before it is locked down, if the intent was receive-only.
Operational race: between the two transactions, a malicious actor controlling the newly-whitelisted address could send before set_send arrives. In a multisig flow this race is harder but nonzero.

Operational — a two-step flow where the intermediate state is more permissive than the desired end state. Not exploitable by external actors (both calls require `WhitelistManagePermission`), but a compliance officer error or a PTB that only includes the first call leaves the address inadvertently empowered to send.

Operational friction; transient state where an address is more permissive than the admin intended (between the add tx and the disable tx). Small race-condition attack surface if mint is permissionless (it isn't here — MintPermission is required — so the actual risk is just ops overhead).
Temporary over-privilege if the two operations are not bundled in a single PTB; inconsistent with granular permission design elsewhere in the module.

title: token_template_pas/Move.toml has empty [dependencies] — implicit Sui framework deps rely on 1.45+
recommendations: Optional: add a comment `# MoveStdlib and Sui framework are implicit in Sui 1.45+` inside the `[dependencies]` block.

Either (a) add explicit framework deps to token_template_pas/Move.toml so sui move build works standalone, or (b) add a README.md explaining the bytecode regeneration flow. Match edition string with root package.

Pick one edition and use it consistently across all packages in the repo. Given the rest of the repo uses `2024.beta`, consider updating `token_template_pas/Move.toml` to match, OR migrate everything to the stable `2024` edition.
Align both `Move.toml` files on the same edition (prefer stable `"2024"` for both, or `"2024.beta"` for both). Document the chosen edition in CONTRIBUTING.

Add a top-of-file comment in `token_template.move` noting: (1) this package is published per-asset via patchConstants, (2) the OTW name is rewritten via `update_identifiers`, (3) the bytecode hex lives at `scripts/src/deployPasTokenTemplate/getBytecode.ts`.

Either (a) add explicit `Sui = { git = ..., rev = ... }` and `MoveStdlib` deps for broader toolchain compatibility, matching the pattern of the hadron root package; or (b) add a README section / CI step that asserts `sui --version >= 1.45`.
descriptions: token_template_pas/Move.toml declares `edition = "2024"` (good) but has an empty `[dependencies]` section. Since Sui 1.45+ the MoveStdlib and Sui framework deps are implicit, so this is technically correct. Consider adding a comment clarifying this for reviewers unfamiliar with the implicit-deps convention, since the main hadron Move.toml explicitly lists external deps.

token_template_pas/Move.toml declares edition = "2024" (correct for stable) while the top-level hadron Move.toml is still "2024.beta". They should agree. Also, [dependencies] is empty — ok because the bytecode is patched and the Sui framework deps are added at publish-time. However, the lack of an explicit Sui framework dep means `sui move build` run locally (to regenerate hex) will fail without an implicit framework resolution. Document this.
Root `Move.toml` declares `edition = "2024.beta"`. `token_template_pas/Move.toml` declares `edition = "2024"`. Same project, different editions. This is visible drift between the two packages.

The root `Move.toml` declares `edition = "2024.beta"`. `token_template_pas/Move.toml` declares `edition = "2024"`. These are treated differently by the Move compiler: `2024.beta` permits experimental features; `2024` is the stable release edition. For a template that deploys alongside Hadron, consistency would reduce future breakage risk when 2024.beta semantics diverge from 2024.

The template's Move.toml uses `edition = "2024"` (stable, good) but has no `version`, no git-tag, and no comment pointing at `scripts/src/deployPasTokenTemplate/getBytecode.ts`. A reviewer coming to this file has no way to know it is exclusively consumed as a WASM-patched blob.

`token_template_pas/Move.toml` declares `edition = "2024"` with empty `[dependencies]`. This requires Sui 1.45+ for implicit framework deps. Older toolchains will fail to build. No CI or README callout of this requirement.
impacts: Documentation / readability only.
Inconsistency across packages; source of confusion for contributors regenerating bytecode.
Minor. Both resolvable in the Sui toolchain, but inconsistent. Future edition-gated features may behave differently across the two packages.

Maintenance risk: bytecode generated from 2024 may not accept a construct the regenerator (`sui move build` in 2024.beta mode) emits. Low practical risk today because the template is simple, but it's a latent inconsistency.
Documentation gap only.
A fresh environment running an older `sui move` will fail to build the PAS token template. No explicit error pointing to toolchain version.
evidence:

name = "token_template_pas"
edition = "2024"

[dependencies]

title: scripts/full-setup: SIGNER_1_PK parsed without null check — env-missing crash is unhelpful
recommendations: Pick one — the `$extend(pas())` pattern is the 2.0 SDK convention per Sui TS SDK docs. Migrate pas.ts to use `getClient().$extend(pas()).pas` and remove the `as any`.
Either reuse `publicKeyFromEnv` from `utils.ts`, or add an explicit `if (!pk1B64) throw new Error('SIGNER_1_PK missing in .env')`.

Extend `full-setup.ts` (or create a sibling `pas-advanced.ts`) that demonstrates: disable send for sender, attempt transfer ⇒ observe abort, re-enable, transfer succeeds. Also demonstrate `removeFromWhitelist` and a seize round-trip via `pas_supply::seize`.

Replace `!` with an explicit check: `if (!process.env.SIGNER_1_PK) throw new Error('SIGNER_1_PK required for full-setup')`. Validate the decoded length equals Ed25519 pubkey length + flag byte (33 bytes).

Either (a) remove `moduleName` from `TokenTemplateValues` type since it's unused, or (b) use `moduleName` as the source-of-truth for `update_identifiers` in `patchConstants.ts` and stop deriving from `symbol`. Option (b) is more flexible but requires care to keep OTW struct name aligned with module name.
descriptions: operations/pas.ts uses `new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any })` (classic constructor), while examples/full-setup.ts uses `getClient(NETWORK).$extend(pas())` (plugin pattern). Two parallel patterns for the same SDK complicate maintenance and diverge on behaviour (e.g. caching, lifecycle). Standardize on one.

`const pk1B64 = process.env.SIGNER_1_PK!;` uses the non-null assertion (`!`) and then immediately calls `fromBase64(pk1B64).slice(1)`. If `SIGNER_1_PK` is unset, `pk1B64` is `undefined` and `fromBase64` will throw an unhelpful error. Inconsistent with `utils.ts::publicKeyFromEnv` which has a proper label-tagged error.

The example calls `addToWhitelist` for both sender and receiver, which per `treasury::add_to_whitelist` defaults can_send=true, can_receive=true. That is enough to make the transfer example work. However, the example never demonstrates `set_whitelist_send` / `set_whitelist_receive` toggles nor `removeFromWhitelist` — meaning operators copy-pasting the example will miss the finer-grained controls.

`const pk1B64 = process.env.SIGNER_1_PK!;` uses non-null assertion and will produce an obscure crash (`fromBase64` on undefined) when the env var is missing. No validation that it is base64 of an Ed25519 pubkey of the expected length.

The `TokenTemplateValues.moduleName` field is declared but not consumed by `patchConstants.ts` — the module name is derived from `symbol.toLowerCase()` (stripped of non-word chars). With `symbol: 'HBOND'`, the deployed module name will be `hbond`, not `hadron_bond`. Anyone reading full-setup.ts reasonably assumes the Move path would be `<pkg>::hadron_bond::HBOND` but it's actually `<pkg>::hbond::HBOND`.
impacts: Maintenance cost; potential subtle differences in plugin vs constructor behaviour go unnoticed.
Developer ergonomics. The example is the primary onboarding path.
Onboarding gap: documentation-by-example is incomplete for the split send/receive model introduced in this PR.
Example script — low blast radius, but copy-pasted into production onboarding scripts could hide a misconfiguration.
Developer confusion when reproducing the flow. Not a security issue; a docs/UX issue.

title: PasAssetRegistered event emits no metadata — no indication of clawback flag, treasury, or caller
recommendations: Add fields: `treasury_id: ID, policy_id: ID, registrant: address` to `PasAssetRegistered` and pass them at emit site.

Add `can_send: bool, can_receive: bool` fields to `WhitelistAdded<T>` and emit current values from `add_to_whitelist`. Also consider emitting a combined `WhitelistFlagsUpdated<T>` event from add/remove for consistency.

Extend `PasAssetRegistered<T>` to include `treasury_id: ID, registrar: address, policy_id: ID`. Emit from `add_pas_coin_caps` or (better) from `pas_admin::register_pas_asset` where all three are available.
Emit initial `can_send: bool` and `can_receive: bool` on `WhitelistAdded<T>`. Adjust `treasury::add_to_whitelist` and `pas_admin::add_to_whitelist` callers accordingly.
Decide (and document) the intended lifecycle. If PAS coins are permanent, state so in the module docs. If deregistration is expected to be supported in a future version, file a follow-up.

Extend to `PasAssetRegistered<phantom T> { treasury_id: ID, admin: address }` and thread `auth.treasury_auth_addr()` / `object::id(treasury)` into the emit call. Do the same audit on `PasMinted`, `PasBurned`, `PasSeized`, `PasTransferApproved`, `WhitelistAdded/Removed`, `WhitelistSend/ReceiveUpdated` — none currently include `treasury_id`, which means one subscribed indexer can't tell which institution's treasury an event belongs to when multiple institutions register the same T.
Add `can_send: bool, can_receive: bool` fields to `WhitelistAdded<T>` and `emit_whitelist_added<T>` signature; have `pas_admin::add_to_whitelist` pass them through.
Include `treasury_id: ID, registrar: address, clawback_allowed: bool, policy_id: ID` in `PasAssetRegistered<T>`. Update pas_admin::register_pas_asset to pass them through.
descriptions: `PasAssetRegistered<T>` is parameterized only by the phantom type — no `treasury_id`, no `policy_id`, no registrant. Indexers cannot tie a PAS registration to the treasury/institution that performed it without correlating with `emit_treasury_created` by timestamp.

`WhitelistAdded<T>` only contains `addr`. Since `add_to_whitelist` sets both flags to true unconditionally today, an off-chain indexer must assume the default. If `add_to_whitelist` is later extended (see R5-024) to take flag parameters, the event becomes insufficient without a schema change. Adding the two booleans now future-proofs the event and matches the pattern of `WhitelistSendUpdated` / `WhitelistReceiveUpdated`.

`PasAssetRegistered<T>` is emitted with an empty body. It does not include the treasury_id, the registering admin's address, or the policy id. For a compliance-critical registration, indexers and auditors typically want the treasury_id and the actor for at least traceability. Compare with `TreasuryCreated` which includes treasury_id, label, and admin.

`WhitelistAdded<T>` carries only `addr`. Off-chain indexers must assume the default (both true). If the default changes in the future or if `add_to_whitelist_with_permissions` is introduced (see R7-010), consumers of this event will have an ambiguous state.

`PasAssetRegistered` exists, but there is no corresponding deregistration event or function. Once `add_pas_coin_caps` has stored the caps, there is no admin path to unwind the registration. This mirrors the regulated-coin model (also no deregistration) but is worth noting because PAS introduces additional per-coin state (policy, whitelist table) that cannot be reclaimed.

`PasAssetRegistered<phantom T>` carries no fields — it is a pure marker. Downstream indexers need the treasury_id at minimum and arguably the registering admin to correlate this event with which institution on which network registered which coin. Compare with `TreasuryCreated { treasury_id, label, admin }`.

`WhitelistAdded<T> { addr }` emits only the address. The entry is created with both flags true, but that invariant is implicit. If `add_to_whitelist_with_flags` is added (see R10-012), the event must carry the flags anyway — and even today, emitting `{ addr, can_send, can_receive }` makes the event self-contained for indexers and forensics.

`PasAssetRegistered<T>` is emitted as an empty struct (phantom T only). Registration is a major state transition that sets clawback policy, establishes the whitelist, and assigns caps — but the event records none of this. Compared to `TreasuryCreated { treasury_id, label, admin }`, PAS registration is under-instrumented.
impacts: Indexer integration friction. For multi-institution deployments this is worse because multiple treasuries can register the same coin type across institutions (subject to pas::policy uniqueness though).
Event schema drift risk; off-chain consumers must duplicate the on-chain default.
Weak audit trail. Consumers must correlate the event via transaction sender, which only works for non-multisig senders and breaks for sponsored transactions.
Minor data-modeling issue. Downstream compliance reporting would have to join `WhitelistAdded` with immediate-following `WhitelistSendUpdated`/`WhitelistReceiveUpdated` events to reconstruct state.
Long-term storage growth. Registrations are monotonic.
Indexer must join on package ID + type T to derive the treasury, which is indirect. Operator forensics harder — no audit trail of which admin triggered registration.
Indexers cannot reconstruct the can_send/can_receive state at time of addition without replaying subsequent `WhitelistSendUpdated`/`WhitelistReceiveUpdated` events; complicates compliance reporting.
Indexers see "a PAS asset was registered" but must query additional state to learn which treasury, who called, and the policy settings. Makes event-driven compliance pipelines brittle.
