title: pas_admin events: no event emitted from register_pas_asset for template registration
recommendations: Add a `TransferTemplateUpdated<phantom T>` event with the package address and approval type, and emit it from both `register_pas_asset` (through the internal helper) and `update_transfer_template`.
Add an event `PasTemplateRegistered<T> { package_id: address }` emitted by `register_transfer_template`. This also helps detect when an older package version is still templated (R2-012).

Add a `TemplateUpdated<T> { treasury_id: ID, new_package: address }` event and emit it at the end of `update_transfer_template`. Bonus: also emit from `register_pas_asset` so the first registration is recorded the same way.
descriptions: `update_transfer_template<T>` changes the Templates registry entry (via `pas::templates::set_template_command`), which alters how the SDK autoresolves a transfer — but Hadron emits no event on the update. `register_pas_asset` emits `PasAssetRegistered<T>` indirectly via `add_pas_coin_caps`, but there is no `TransferTemplateUpdated<T>` emission. Off-chain tooling and auditors must cross-reference the upstream Templates DOF state to detect a template change.

`register_transfer_template` writes to the Templates registry (shared state) but emits no event. `update_transfer_template` (which re-registers after an upgrade) also emits no event. This means indexers cannot observe template registration / re-registration on-chain. The only PAS event for registration is `PasAssetRegistered<T>` emitted from treasury via `add_pas_coin_caps`, which confirms cap storage but not template setup. For operators tracking upgrade consistency this is opaque.

`update_transfer_template<T>` is a significant operational event (redirecting autoresolve for a PAS asset after an upgrade) but emits nothing. Other state-changing paths in this module (register, whitelist mutations) all emit events.
impacts: Observability gap. A malicious or buggy upgrade path that re-registers the template pointing to a different package (e.g., via package upgrade shuffling) is undetectable by Hadron event consumers.
No on-chain evidence of template registration; upgrade compliance / staleness detection requires reading Templates DOF directly.

Indexers and compliance pipelines cannot detect post-upgrade template redirection. An adversary with RegisterCoinPermission could silently update the template to point to a malicious package — while other state changes are all logged.

title: pas_supply::mint: account.owner() is raw address — object accounts not explicitly documented
recommendations: Add a module-level doc comment to `pas_supply` and `pas_admin::add_to_whitelist` explaining that for object-owned PAS accounts the whitelistable address is `object::id(parent_obj).to_address()`. Reference the upstream account.move L132-134 behavior.
Add a doc-comment on `pas_supply::mint` (and on whitelist mutators) explaining that for object-owned PAS accounts the whitelist must hold the object's derived address (`uid.to_inner().to_address()`).

Add a module-level doc comment in `pas_supply.move` (and mirror in `pas_admin.move` for whitelist functions) that explicitly calls out: `account.owner()` for UID-owned accounts resolves to the object UID's address, and that is the value that must appear in the whitelist.
descriptions: `pas_supply::mint` calls `treasury.assert_can_receive<T>(account.owner())`. Per upstream `pas::account::new_auth_as_object` (pas/account.move L132-134), an Account that is owned by a UID-owned object uses `uid.to_inner().to_address()` as its owner — a synthetic address. For that case, the compliance team needs to whitelist the synthetic address, not the wallet that controls the parent object. This is undocumented in Hadron's module docs and will bite operators trying to mint into object-owned accounts.

`mint` uses `account.owner()` as the whitelist key. Per upstream `account.move` L132-134, an `Account` may be owned by a `UID` (object) via `new_auth_as_object(uid)` — in that case `owner` is `uid.to_inner().to_address()`, a synthetic address. Whitelisting object-owned accounts therefore requires knowing that synthetic address. Not inherently wrong, but undocumented on the Hadron side.

`mint` calls `treasury.assert_can_receive<T>(account.owner())`. When the Account is owned by a UID (per `pas::account::new_auth_as_object` upstream), `account.owner` is the synthetic address `uid.to_inner().to_address()`. Admins whitelisting an object-owned account must whitelist that synthetic address, not the wallet that authorized the object. This is non-obvious and unmentioned in the module docs.
impacts: Operational confusion. A compliance officer trying to onboard an object-owned account (e.g., a DAO treasury modeled as a Sui object) must know to compute `object::id(parent).to_address()` and whitelist that synthetic address rather than any wallet — a non-obvious convention drawn from the upstream PAS SDK.
Potential integrator confusion when an object is the asset holder.
Operator error: mint aborts unexpectedly on object-owned accounts because admins whitelist the wrong address.

title: PAS supply mint/burn/seize emits events after external calls — no reentrancy but ordering check
recommendations: Add `account_id: ID` to `PasMinted` (and `PasBurned`, `PasSeized` to and from) so indexers can link events to account objects without needing upstream PAS knowledge. Alternatively, document this in pas_supply.move.

Add a comment in pas_transfer.move clarifying that PasTransferApproved is emitted only within the same PTB as send_funds::resolve_balance. Subscribers should not treat this event as the final transfer — use the PAS-emitted settlement event if one exists.
Also include `account_id: ID` (the PAS account object ID) in `PasMinted<T>` so indexers can disambiguate. The deposit itself lives at `object::id(account)`, so the account ID is the source of truth.
descriptions: pas_supply::mint calls `account.deposit_balance(balance)` which, per upstream `account::deposit_balance`, forwards the balance to `object::id(account).to_address()` — i.e. the PAS account object address, not the wallet owner. However, the emitted `PasMinted` event records `account.owner()` as `recipient`. Off-chain indexers inspecting event-only data will see the owner address, not where the balance actually lives. For regulatory reporting, both should be exposed (or only the account object address if that's the point-of-truth).

mint, burn, and seize all emit events at end after balance deposit / coin burn / resolve. Good pattern. However, approve_transfer emits PasTransferApproved *before* upstream resolve_balance is called (resolve is a separate SDK step). A re-subscriber counting emitted events as transfers completed could overcount if the subsequent resolve_balance never fires (caller aborts the PTB, though the emit is in the same PTB so effects are atomic — if the resolve aborts, the emit is also reverted). Net: correctness-safe under Sui's atomic PTB semantics, but document the coupling.

`account.owner()` returns a wallet OR a `uid.to_inner().to_address()` (the synthetic address of an object-owned account per `pas::account::new_auth_as_object`). The `PasMinted<T>` event stores this address as `recipient`, and the whitelist was presumably populated with the same synthetic address. Off-chain observers interpreting the event as a wallet address will mislabel object-owned flows.
impacts: Off-chain reconciliation ambiguity: the event's `recipient` is the human-visible owner, but the on-chain balance sits at the derived account ID. Reconciling via Sui balance queries by address will not match events unless indexers know the deposit semantics.
None under Sui transaction atomicity. Informational only.
Misleading off-chain data. Compliance dashboards may flag or misattribute UID-owned balances.

title: pas_supply::burn/seize do not re-check version — only the auth-creation path does
recommendations: Either add `version.check_is_valid()` explicitly at the top of `pas_supply::mint/burn/seize`, OR document (via a module-level comment) that the version gate is delegated to auth creation. Similarly remove the explicit check in `approve_transfer` if the convention is to delegate, or add it everywhere.
Optional: emit a `PasSeizeAttempted` event BEFORE the clawback resolve, or document that failed seizes are visible only via tx effects failure. Low priority; file as info.

Either (a) take `version: &Version` and call `check_is_valid()` at entry, mirroring `pas_transfer::approve_transfer`, or (b) document the rationale for relying on Auth-time validation in a module-level comment.
descriptions: `pas_supply::burn` and `pas_supply::seize` do not call `version.check_is_valid()` directly. They rely on the auth being freshly constructed in the same PTB (which does call `version.check_is_valid()` via `auth::new_auth`). This is usually fine because auths have `drop` and cannot be stored, but `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` DOES call `version.check_is_valid()` explicitly (line 38) — which creates an inconsistency. Moreover, because `Auth<P, T>` is `drop`-only and freshly minted, the guarantee holds, but future refactors that relax Auth's drop-only property would silently break the gate.

`seize` assumes the clawback `Request` is valid; if `clawback_funds::resolve` aborts (e.g., upstream `policy.is_clawback_allowed()` is false, or approvals don't match), the entire txn aborts and no event is emitted — the PAS accounts and treasury state are unchanged (correct), but an off-chain indexer tracking attempted seizures gets no signal. Combined with the fact that seize is gated on `assert_mint_redeem_enabled` (which itself aborts silently if paused), failed-seize attempts are invisible on-chain.

`mint`, `burn`, `seize` do not call `version.check_is_valid()` directly. They rely on the fact that `Auth<P, T>` construction in `auth::new_auth` performed the check. This is fine for a single-PTB flow, but if an Auth is constructed and a subsequent migrate call blocks the old version in the same PTB, the downstream mint still proceeds because Auth has `drop` and the caller holds a reference.
impacts: Defensive consistency. Low risk today; fragile on future refactor.
Observability. Compliance teams auditing seize activity won't see attempted-but-failed seizes. Not a security issue.

Theoretical — requires a malicious PTB author to race migrate + mint. Low likelihood because version migrate requires DeployerCap. Still, the normal pattern across the codebase is to re-check version at every public entrypoint.

title: approve_transfer does not emit an event when request is approved but resolve_balance fails
recommendations: Rename the event to `PasTransferApprovalStamped<T>` or `PasTransferApprovalGranted<T>` to reflect that it records the approval step, not the final transfer completion. Document in the event struct that resolution is handled by upstream PAS and emits no Hadron-side event.

Either (a) also emit `sender_account_id` and `recipient_account_id` (available via `request.data().sender_account_id()` / `.recipient_account_id()`) on `PasTransferApproved`, or (b) clearly document in the event struct's comment that these addresses are logical (wallet) and that the PAS account IDs must be derived off-chain.
Document in a comment that `PasTransferApproved<T>` only fires if the full PTB (including `send_funds::resolve_balance`) commits, so event-sourced indexers can treat it as authoritative.
descriptions: `approve_transfer` emits `PasTransferApproved<T>` immediately after stamping the approval witness on the Request. But approval is a precondition — the actual movement of funds happens in `pas::send_funds::resolve_balance<C>` (upstream, packages/pas/sources/requests/send_funds.move L62-71), which the SDK calls in a later PTB command. If `resolve_balance` aborts (policy versioning check, required_approvals mismatch) the approve event is still emitted because events are not rolled back on same-transaction abort within the same PTB — wait, they actually ARE rolled back on tx abort. So this is only misleading if the SDK builds a PTB that approves but never resolves (partial transfer — technically impossible with the hot-potato Request).

`emit_pas_transfer_approved<T>(sender, recipient, amount)` carries wallet/object addresses (from `request.data().sender()` / `.recipient()`). Upstream `pas::send_funds::resolve_balance<C>` actually routes the balance to `recipient_account_id.to_address()` (the derived PAS Account address), not the recipient wallet. The emitted event therefore shows the intent (wallet-to-wallet) but not the ledger destination (account-to-account).

`approve_transfer` emits `PasTransferApproved<T>` at the end, after `request.approve(...)`. But the actual balance move happens later in `send_funds::resolve_balance` (called by the SDK autoresolve, not by this function). If a PTB contains both `approve_transfer` and `resolve_balance` and the latter reverts for reasons outside Hadron's control (policy versioning, approval mismatch), the event was emitted but the transfer did not occur — except the whole PTB reverts atomically, so the event is also rolled back. OK.
impacts: Minor observability polish. The event name `PasTransferApproved` suggests 'approved for transfer' not 'transfer successful'. A consumer seeing the event should understand it means 'approval stamped, resolution pending/imminent in the same tx'. Misnamed events cause dashboard confusion.

Off-chain consumers that build balance ledgers by subscribing to `PasTransferApproved` events will track wallet-level balances that do not match PAS account-level balances. For indexers trying to reconcile on-chain state, this can cause drift.
None — atomicity protects the invariant. Flagging as info because the inverse (emit after resolve) is structurally cleaner but cannot be done from this module since resolve happens upstream.

title: approve_transfer uses `&Treasury` — OK, but check ordering emits event after approve
recommendations: Document in the function doc comment that it is public and permissionless by design, gated only by the whitelist. No code change.

If rate limits are in scope for a later phase, consider designing a `TransferLimits<T>` DOF (per-address, per-period) now to avoid an invasive refactor later. Out of scope for this PR; track as a future enhancement.
Consider adding a comment that explicitly marks `approve` as the state-committing point.
descriptions: `approve_transfer` is a public permissionless entrypoint. Anyone can obtain a `Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>>` by calling `pas::account::send_balance` on an account they own, then call `approve_transfer<T>` repeatedly with zero-address recipients and other permutations. Because `assert_can_send` aborts when sender is not whitelisted, the tx aborts and no event fires — BUT if the attacker is whitelisted (even temporarily), they can splitter-burn gas and emit `PasTransferApproved` events with arbitrary amounts up to their balance. This is standard permissionless-autoresolve behavior and cannot be avoided for the PAS UX.

approve_transfer is permissionless (anyone can submit a tx calling it, subject to whitelist checks) — this matches PAS design intent for autoresolve. However, the only rate-limit surface is the per-address whitelist bit (can_send / can_receive). There is no global throughput cap, no per-address per-period cap, no per-transfer amount cap. For a regulated stablecoin flow this may be insufficient for compliance rules that require e.g. 'no more than $X per day per address'. Spec §7.2 does not require this, so surface as info.

Order: version check → read sender/recipient/amount → amount>0 → pause check → send/receive checks → approve → emit. Good ordering: approve is last-but-one. If any assert after version check aborts, the request is not mutated (no approval added). No silent state leakage.
impacts: Event-log spam / indexer noise; no loss of funds.
Not a security bug. Informational — institutions considering PAS for KYC-gated but volume-capped flows will need additional off-chain or on-chain rails.
None — documenting positive behaviour.

title: add_pas_coin_caps does not validate metadata_cap corresponds to treasury_cap
recommendations: Consider extending `WhitelistEntry` with `{ can_send: bool, can_receive: bool, added_at_ms: u64, expires_at_ms: Option<u64> }` and pass `&Clock` into `add_to_whitelist`. Alternative: rely entirely on the event stream and document the audit-trail model explicitly.

Add a module comment on `add_pas_coin_caps` stating: `/// All three caps are bound to the same T via type parameterization; no runtime check is needed.` Consider `treasury_cap.total_supply() == 0` is the only runtime precondition — keep that assertion.
descriptions: `WhitelistEntry` stores only `can_send: bool` and `can_receive: bool`. There is no timestamp (added_at, updated_at), no expiry, no reason code, and no associated off-chain identifier. For a compliance-regulated asset, audit trails typically need an added_at / last_modified_by / expiry field for KYC periodic-refresh cycles.

`add_pas_coin_caps` stores `treasury_cap`, `metadata_cap`, `policy_cap` as DOFs but does not assert that `metadata_cap` corresponds to the same currency as `treasury_cap`. A malicious caller (with RegisterCoinPermission) could in principle pass a TreasuryCap<A> and a MetadataCap<B> (B != A) if they have both. The generic `<T>` forces both to be parameterized by T, so the type system rescues this — you cannot have MetadataCap<T> that refers to a different T. OK — the type-level binding suffices. But note: upstream `policy_cap` is `PolicyCap<Balance<T>>` which binds to Balance<T>, not T directly, so the type-level binding is slightly indirect.
impacts: No on-chain record of when an address was whitelisted or by whom (beyond the WhitelistAdded event which is an event, not queryable state). Compliance audits requiring 'show me the whitelist as of date X' must replay events rather than query state.
No adversarial path through current typing. Worth a comment stating the type-level invariant for future reviewers.

title: treasury::add_pas_coin_caps does not destroy the unused DenyCap slot — DOF layout divergence
recommendations: Consider removing `copy` from `WhitelistEntry` (keep `drop, store`). Copy adds no semantic value for a two-bool record stored in a Table.

Consider extending `WhitelistEntry` with an expiry timestamp and optional per-address send/receive caps, gated by the same `WhitelistManagePermission`. If the scope is intentional, document the rationale in the module doc.

Callers of `deny_cap_mut<T>` should first assert `!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()` and return a clean `EPasCoinNotAllowed`-style error. Alternatively, expose `is_regulated_coin<T>()` as the counterpart to `is_pas_coin<T>()` for callers.
descriptions: `WhitelistEntry { can_send, can_receive }` has `copy, drop, store`. Using `wl[addr].can_send = enabled;` (L345) in `set_whitelist_send` works only because Move 2024 generated index-assign accessors on `Table`. If the Table indexer ever returns by-value (copy) instead of by-mut-ref, the assignment would be a no-op on a discarded copy. With `copy` ability this would compile silently. Currently this is safe because `sui::table` index-assign is mut-ref.

The PAS spec does not require expiry or rate-limit fields on whitelist entries, but the current design (two booleans: `can_send`, `can_receive`) is very coarse. There's no expiry, no daily/monthly transfer limit, and no audit trail beyond the `WhitelistAdded`/`WhitelistRemoved` events. For a compliance-grade regulated asset this is a thin model.

Non-PAS `add_coin_caps` adds three DOFs: `treasury_cap_key<T>`, `deny_cap_key<T>`, `metadata_cap_key<T>`. PAS `add_pas_coin_caps` adds `treasury_cap_key<T>`, `metadata_cap_key<T>`, `policy_cap_key<T>`, `whitelist_key<T>` — NO `deny_cap_key<T>`. This is by design (PAS uses whitelist not denylist), but means `is_coin_registered<T>` returns true for both paths while `deny_cap_mut<T>` would abort on PAS coins with `ECoinNotRegistered`. A caller that only knows `is_coin_registered<T>` cannot tell which cap scheme is in use.
impacts: Defense-in-depth — removes the risk that a future refactor accidentally drops the `&mut` borrow semantics.
Not a bug — observation for design review. Compliance regimes (KYC/AML) often demand transferrable limits with time bounds.
Surface ambiguity; no security impact today but any future code that assumes `is_coin_registered<T>` implies `deny_cap_mut<T>` is available will abort on PAS coins.

title: pas_supply::seize does not require source to be whitelisted — design choice, needs explicit doc
recommendations: Add an explicit `/// NOTE: source is intentionally NOT whitelist-gated — seize must work on de-whitelisted addresses.` comment on `seize<T>` (and likewise on `burn<T>`).
Add a `source_whitelisted: bool` field to PasSeized<T> (capture treasury.is_whitelisted<T>(from) before the resolve) — or emit two distinct event types. Minor but useful for downstream indexers.
descriptions: `seize` validates `to.owner()` is whitelisted (destination), but does not validate `from` address. This is deliberate per spec (§7.2) so seize can extract funds from a de-whitelisted address. It is not documented in the module or function doc comment; a maintainer could later 'fix' this asymmetry by adding `assert_can_send<T>(from)`, which would break the seize-on-blocked-address flow.

seize emits PasSeized<T> with from/to/amount. The 'from' account's whitelist status (or de-whitelisted status) is not captured. A compliance audit tracing fund movements needs to know whether a seize hit a whitelisted or de-whitelisted account. The design-intent note in _context.md §7.2 explicitly says 'seize can extract funds from de-whitelisted addresses' — if so, the event should flag this.
impacts: Future maintainer could accidentally add a symmetric whitelist check that would break the compliance clawback path.
Audit/compliance tooling cannot distinguish 'seized from whitelisted account' (routine extraction) from 'seized from de-whitelisted account' (enforcement) from on-chain events alone.

title: Duplicate EMintAmountZero error constant across supply and pas_supply
recommendations: Either unify in a shared `hadron::errors` module, or make PAS-specific names (`EPasMintAmountZero`) to avoid confusion in logs.
Consider lifting shared amount-zero errors into a `hadron::errors` module, or accept the duplication and silence the code review.
descriptions: Both `hadron::supply` and `hadron::pas_supply` define `#[error] const EMintAmountZero: vector<u8>` with identical strings. Clients catching by error code cannot disambiguate which path emitted. Same duplication for `ERedeemAmountZero`/`EBurnAmountZero` (different names but similar semantic).

`pas_supply::EMintAmountZero` duplicates `supply::EMintAmountZero` verbatim. Since the two modules now coexist and operate on disjoint coin sets (enforced by `EPasCoinNotAllowed`), consolidation would reduce duplication.
impacts: Minor — debuggability; indexers filtering by module are unaffected.
Low — same abort code value means same error behaviour.

title: Request resolve contract: upstream uses `zip_do_ref` over VecSet ordering — witness set must match exactly
recommendations: Add an assertion that the passed policy's cap-id matches the treasury-stored PolicyCap. PolicyCap is derived from the policy via `derived_object::claim(&mut policy.id, PolicyCapKey())` upstream, so the PolicyCap id is a deterministic function of the Policy id — you can check this via `object::id(policy)` and `object::id(treasury.policy_cap<T>())`. Apply the same check to `seize`.

When adding multi-witness actions, document the canonical ordering and add a Move test that asserts the observed ordering matches expectations. Alternatively, push upstream PAS to compare by set-equality instead of zip.
descriptions: `burn<T>` takes `policy: &Policy<Balance<T>>` from the caller. The type system enforces the generic match, but Hadron stores its OWN `PolicyCap<Balance<T>>` in the Treasury via `add_pas_coin_caps`. There is no assertion that the passed `policy` matches the Treasury's policy_cap (e.g. `policy_cap.policy_id == object::id(policy)`). In practice there is only one `Policy<Balance<T>>` per T because upstream `new_for_currency` asserts `!policy_exists<Balance<T>>()`. So today this is safe by external invariant. But the invariant is load-bearing and not asserted locally.

Upstream `pas::request::resolve` (at /Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/requests/request.move L44–52) uses `VecSet.into_keys().zip_do_ref(...)`, which compares approvals and required_approvals in insertion order. `pas_admin::register_pas_asset` sets exactly one required approval per action (`TransferApproval<T>` for send_funds, `ClawbackApproval<T>` for clawback_funds) — so there is only one slot and zip-do-ref is trivially satisfied. If Hadron ever adds a second approval to either action, the insertion order on Hadron's side MUST match the order on the policy's side, or `EInsufficientApprovals` will abort.
impacts: If upstream PAS ever allows multiple policies for the same T (e.g. via a different namespace path), `burn` could resolve a request against a different policy than the Hadron-registered one.
Future hazard: second-approval additions will produce hard-to-debug `EInsufficientApprovals` aborts if insertion order diverges.

title: Duplicated EMintRedeemPaused string literal across supply.move, pas_transfer.move
recommendations: In `pas_transfer::approve_transfer`, replace the local `assert!(!treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>(), EMintRedeemPaused);` with `treasury::assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>(treasury);`. Delete the local `EMintRedeemPaused` constant.

Either (a) route pas_transfer's pause check through treasury::assert_mint_redeem_enabled to share the same error (`EMintRedeemDisabled`) and drop `EMintRedeemPaused` from pas_transfer; or (b) unify by exporting the error from treasury and re-using it. Option (a) is preferred.
descriptions: `pas_transfer::EMintRedeemPaused` ("Mint and redeem operations are paused for this PAS asset") and `treasury::EMintRedeemDisabled` ("Mint and redeem operations are disabled for this coin") describe the same state with slightly different words. The pause-check in `approve_transfer` uses its own local `EMintRedeemPaused` constant rather than reusing `treasury::EMintRedeemDisabled` via `assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>()`. Code divergence: two error codes for the same semantic.

pas_transfer defines its own `EMintRedeemPaused` error constant duplicating the one already defined in treasury (`EMintRedeemDisabled`). Worse, the two use different names (`Paused` vs `Disabled`) for what appears to be the same concept. This creates two error codes for logically-the-same failure condition, depending on where the check fires. Off-chain consumers decoding abort codes will see different error strings for "mint/redeem paused/disabled", which complicates monitoring and error-handling UX.
impacts: Monitoring/alerting treats the two errors as distinct when they are the same condition. Minor maintenance burden.
Inconsistent error surface; harder to categorize aborts; two strings for the same underlying state.

title: update_transfer_template does not assert coin is a registered PAS asset
recommendations: Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin);` (or similar) at the top of `update_transfer_template`. Same check could be added for the initial `register_pas_asset` path as a defensive early abort, but that one is implicitly checked by `add_pas_coin_caps`.
descriptions: `update_transfer_template<T>` only asserts that the `TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>` is valid for this treasury — it does **not** assert `treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()`. A holder of `RegisterCoinPermission` can therefore register a stale autoresolve template for a type `T` that was never registered as a PAS asset in this treasury, polluting the global `Templates` registry with dead pointers.
impacts: Low-severity operational hygiene issue. No funds at risk. However, because `templates::set_template_command` overwrites any existing entry keyed by `type_name<TransferApproval<T>>()`, a malicious-or-buggy caller could overwrite a correct template (if two treasuries share a coin type — which should not happen but is not structurally prevented). More realistically, it clutters the Templates registry.

title: register_pas_asset takes &Version but never checks it directly
recommendations: Either drop the unused `version: &Version` parameter from `register_pas_asset` (keeping it only in `update_transfer_template` where it's equally unused), or add `version.check_is_valid();` at the top of both functions for defense-in-depth. Currently `update_transfer_template` also has this inconsistency.
descriptions: `register_pas_asset<T>` signature includes `version: &Version` but the function body never calls `version.check_is_valid()`. The version check is reached indirectly via `TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>` creation (which does check) and via `register_transfer_template` (which does not). If a caller fabricates an auth proof via `new_treasury_auth` in an earlier txn and reuses it via PTB composition in a later txn after version invalidation, the `assert_is_valid_for_treasury` check still passes (no version re-check) and registration proceeds. Auth proofs have `drop` only — they don't persist across txns — so this is not exploitable in practice, but the function receives a version reference it never uses, which is style-inconsistent with other hadron entrypoints.
impacts: Stylistic / defensive coding. No exploit — `Auth`/`TreasuryAuth` do not have `store` so they cannot cross transactions.

title: Doc comment in `register_transfer_template` mis-orders args
recommendations: Fix the comment to read `approve_transfer<T>(treasury, request, version)` to match the actual signature.
descriptions: The comment says the command calls `hadron::pas_transfer::approve_transfer<T>(treasury, version, request)` but the actual `approve_transfer` signature is `(treasury, request, version)` and the template args built here are in signature order `[treasury, request, version]` (lines 139-143). The comment contradicts both the real signature and the emitted template.
impacts: Maintenance hazard: a future reader could trust the comment and reorder template args, which would then break SDK autoresolve silently (or at runtime with a type mismatch).
evidence:
/// Register the PTB template command that tells the SDK how to auto-resolve transfers.
/// The command calls hadron::pas_transfer::approve_transfer<T>(treasury, version, request).

title: WhitelistEntry lacks event for state change on add (defaults are implicit)
recommendations: Have `emit_whitelist_added` carry `{ addr, can_send, can_receive }` (defaulting to `true, true`), or emit both `WhitelistAdded` and the two initial `WhitelistSendUpdated`/`WhitelistReceiveUpdated` events.
descriptions: `add_to_whitelist` emits `WhitelistAdded<T>(addr)` but not the fact that `can_send=true, can_receive=true` is the default. Indexers that rely on the last `WhitelistSendUpdated`/`WhitelistReceiveUpdated` event will miss the initial state and show `false` until an explicit `set_whitelist_send` call fires. Similarly, `remove_from_whitelist` emits a generic removal event without the prior send/receive booleans, making reconstruction from the event stream lossy.
impacts: Off-chain state reconstruction is incorrect without querying on-chain state directly. Subtle but adds non-trivial indexer complexity.

title: WhitelistManagePermission on a non-PAS coin yields opaque abort in DOF borrow
recommendations: Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin)` at the top of each whitelist mutator in `treasury.move` (`add_to_whitelist`, `remove_from_whitelist`, `set_whitelist_send`, `set_whitelist_receive`), and similarly on the read helpers (`is_whitelisted`, `can_send`, `can_receive`) so callers fail with a clear error.
descriptions: `auth::new_auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>` only asserts `treasury.is_coin_registered<T>()` (i.e. TreasuryCapKey<T> exists). That check is also satisfied for non-PAS coins registered via `admin::register_coin`. The caller can then build `Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>` and call `pas_admin::add_to_whitelist<T>` / `remove_from_whitelist<T>` / `set_whitelist_*`, which internally call `dynamic_object_field::borrow_mut<WhitelistKey<T>>(...)` on a DOF that was never created. The transaction aborts deep inside sui-framework with an opaque "field does not exist" error rather than a clean `ENotPasCoin`.
impacts: Developer ergonomics / observability. No security impact, but a legitimate operator mistake surfaces as a cryptic abort instead of `ENotPasCoin`.

title: assert_mint_redeem_enabled abort message mismatches seize/transfer context
recommendations: Either rename the shared error to something like `EPauseFlagActive` with message `"Coin operations are paused (mint/redeem/transfer/seize)"`, or introduce separate pause flags per operation family (see R7-004).
descriptions: The error `EMintRedeemDisabled: b"Mint and redeem operations are disabled for this coin"` is reused (via `assert_mint_redeem_enabled`) in `pas_supply::mint`, `pas_supply::burn`, `pas_supply::seize`, AND indirectly in `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` (which defines its own `EMintRedeemPaused` string but checks the same underlying `is_mint_redeem_paused<T>`). Users seeing this error after a seize or transfer attempt are given a misleading message implying mint/redeem failed.
impacts: Confusing error surface. Operators investigating a failed seize or transfer may not realize the root cause is the mint/redeem pause flag.
evidence:
#[error]
const EMintRedeemDisabled: vector<u8> = b"Mint and redeem operations are disabled for this coin";

title: treasury error code ENotPasCoin defined but underused
recommendations: Use `ENotPasCoin` in `is_whitelisted`, `can_send`, `can_receive` (via early-return with false), and in `add_to_whitelist`, `remove_from_whitelist`, `set_whitelist_send`, `set_whitelist_receive` (as an explicit assert).
descriptions: `ENotPasCoin` is declared but used only in `policy_cap<T>` (L273). All whitelist helpers and mutators (which should also check `is_pas_coin<T>`) rely on opaque framework aborts instead. See R9-004 and R9-005 for the correctness impact.
impacts: Inconsistent error surface; code's declared errors don't match observed runtime aborts.
evidence:
#[error]
const ENotPasCoin: vector<u8> = b"Coin type is not registered as a PAS asset";

title: No tests for permission-coverage invariants involving new permissions
recommendations: Add a small test that inspects the admin role's permission set post-bootstrap and asserts that `WhitelistManagePermission` and `SeizePermission` are NOT present, documenting the intended operational model.
descriptions: Two new permissions (`WhitelistManagePermission`, `SeizePermission`) were added but the bootstrap `admin` role in `roles::create_admin_role` does **not** include them. That is a deliberate design choice, but there are no tests confirming the intended assignment flow (admin must create a new role and add these permissions explicitly) nor any test preventing a future maintainer from accidentally including them in the admin bootstrap.
impacts: Design intent is not mechanically enforced.

title: supply.move::mint/redeem: `EPasCoinNotAllowed` ordering makes error messages less specific
recommendations: Reorder assertions: put `assert!(!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), EPasCoinNotAllowed)` first so callers get a structural-error-code-early. Same for `redeem`.
descriptions: In `supply::mint`, the assertion order is: (1) `amount > 0` → `EMintAmountZero`, (2) `!is_pas_coin<T>()` → `EPasCoinNotAllowed`, (3) `assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>()`. Moving the PAS-coin check before the amount check would give better diagnostics (the PAS vs non-PAS mismatch is a programmer error; amount=0 is a user error). Minor.
impacts: Diagnostic quality only.

title: @mysten/pas 0.0.3 — unstable pre-1.0 SDK dependency
recommendations: Pin to an exact version (`"0.0.3"`) and add `@mysten/pas` to a manual upgrade-audit checklist until the SDK hits 1.0.
descriptions: `@mysten/pas": "^0.0.3"` pins a pre-1.0 SDK with caret semver — any `0.0.x` release will be picked up on `pnpm install`. Combined with the `@mysten/pas` SDK being developmental, automatic patch upgrades can introduce silent API changes.
impacts: Build reproducibility risk; surprising breakage from a patch-level upgrade.

title: full-setup.ts grants RegisterCoin via sender only — no separate register-coin role in demo
recommendations: Verify that the bootstrap admin has `RegisterCoinPermission`; if not, extend the demo's role list with an admin role carrying `['registerCoin', 'updateMetadata']`. Also add a `seizeOfficer` role so the full PAS surface can be demonstrated end-to-end.
Add a commented-out block in full-setup.ts that demonstrates the expected failure: "// Attempting supply::mint on a PAS coin will abort with EPasCoinNotAllowed". Or split into two example files.

Either (a) add `seize` to `compliance_officer`'s permission list in `full-setup.ts`, or (b) add a dedicated `seize_operator` role example even if the full-setup doesn't exercise seize, and include a comment explaining when each is used.
descriptions: The demo creates only two roles — `minter` (mint, redeem) and `compliance_officer` (pauseMintRedeem, unpauseMintRedeem, denylistAdd, denylistRemove, whitelistManage). Neither role has `registerCoin` or `seize`. The `registerPasAsset` call later in the flow relies on the bootstrap admin (sender) having `ManageRolesPermission` — which includes `RegisterCoinPermission` via the role? Actually no: `bootstrap_admin` only gets `ManageRolesPermission` (phase-1 convention). So this demo, as written, will fail at the `registerPasAsset` step when the sender's bootstrap admin role cannot satisfy `RegisterCoinPermission`. Unless the phase-1 role setup implicitly grants registerCoin to the bootstrap admin (out of scope to verify here).

The full-setup example creates a single treasury and then registers BOTH a regulated coin (HUSD via deployAndRegisterCoin) and a PAS coin (HBOND via deployAndRegisterPasAsset). This is probably intended as documentation that a single treasury can hold both coin kinds, which is good. However, the example omits any negative-path demonstration (e.g., "what happens if you try supply::mint on a PAS coin"). An onboarding engineer may not realize the guards are there.

The `RoleSetup` in the full-setup example assigns `whitelistManage` to `compliance_officer`, but `seize` is not assigned to any role. As a result, after running the example, seize is unreachable — no address has `SeizePermission`. Any operator following this example as a template for production will miss this capability entirely.
impacts: The example may not run end-to-end without manual role assignment, making it a poor reference for integrators.
DX / documentation. Not a correctness issue in the example itself.

Onboarding / documentation. A compliance action (seize) is included in the module but not demonstrated in the canonical setup script. Operators copying from the example will silently lose seize capability.

title: remove_from_whitelist does not clear in-flight approval state — but WhitelistEntry has no state so OK; document
recommendations: No change; document the intended flow (add, then toggle) in the module header.
Add a comment above the function: `/// Fully removes the entry; any future stateful fields in WhitelistEntry must also be cleared here.`
descriptions: The toggle functions abort if the address is not already in the whitelist. This is arguably correct (prevents accidental onboarding via toggle), but combined with R3-026 it means there is no atomic 'create with specific flags' path. Confirming intended.

`remove_from_whitelist` fully removes the entry (no soft-revoke). Since `WhitelistEntry` carries only `can_send, can_receive` (no timestamps, rate limits, or in-flight transfer state), there's no leftover state to clean. A re-addition will create a fresh entry with both flags default-true. Intentional but worth documenting to prevent future additions of stateful fields breaking this assumption.
impacts: Minor ergonomics.
None today. Future-proofing documentation.

title: token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move line 24 marked public(package) but deployed as 0x0:: — access modifier is moot
recommendations: Either (a) unify the two templates into one, gating `make_regulated` on a boolean constant so the bytecode layout is identical, or (b) add a CI check that regenerates both hex strings from the compiled artifacts and fails on manual drift.
Mark as `fun` (private) since it's only called within the module, or remove if unused outside tests. Align with Move best-practice: use the least-permissive visibility.
descriptions: Both `token_template/` (regulated) and `token_template_pas/` (PAS) use `module 0x0::token_template` and `public struct TOKEN_TEMPLATE has drop {}`. OTW uniqueness is per-package, so independent publications of the same module are fine. However, both templates share the identical bytecode constants layout (SYMBOL/NAME/ICON_URL/DECIMALS/DESCRIPTION) — only the `make_regulated` vs non-regulated step differs. Consolidating into one template with a flag would reduce drift risk (only one hex string to keep patched).

`init_treasury` is `public(package)` but the template module is published as a standalone package (`module 0x0::token_template`). No other module in this standalone package calls `init_treasury`. The visibility modifier has no effect (other than making the fn non-public-exported). This mirrors the phase-1 template pattern but is worth noting for cleanliness.
impacts: Maintenance and patch-consistency risk. The patcher `patchConstants` is shared — if regulated and PAS templates' bytecode diverge silently, one patch could corrupt the other.
None (behavior is unchanged). Code-quality polish only.

title: pas_admin::register_pas_asset passes &mut treasury_cap to policy::new_for_currency but keeps ownership
recommendations: Optional: add a comment noting why the local binding is `mut treasury_cap` (required by upstream's `&mut` even though upstream ignores the cap).
descriptions: `policy::new_for_currency` takes `_cap: &mut TreasuryCap<C>` — per upstream `/Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/policy.move` L46-50, the cap is an unused witness (`_cap`). Hadron's caller correctly passes `&mut treasury_cap` and retains ownership, then later transfers ownership to `add_pas_coin_caps`. Correct. However, the upstream signature uses `&mut` but the argument is prefixed `_cap` (unused). This is a flaky upstream API contract — if upstream ever narrows this to `&TreasuryCap<C>` (reasonable, since it's unused), Hadron would not need `mut` on the local binding. Low-priority stylistic concern.
impacts: None. Stylistic / future-compatibility note.

title: pas_admin::update_transfer_template takes &Treasury, not needed
recommendations: Either keep `&Treasury` for future-proofing (and document the reason) or switch to `treasury_id: ID` derived from `auth.treasury_auth_treasury_id()`. The latter lets the function run without contending on the Treasury shared object.
descriptions: The function takes `treasury: &Treasury` and uses it only to read `treasury_id()` for `auth.assert_is_valid_for_treasury(...)` and then passes it to `register_transfer_template<T>` which only reads `object::id(treasury)` to embed in the PTB. The treasury id is already stored in the TreasuryAuth proof, so passing the Treasury object mainly exists to get its ID for the template. Acceptable, but it is idiomatic to take the bare ID when that is all that is read.
impacts: Minor verbosity; forces a read-locked Treasury input for a function that only needs its ID.

title: pas_admin::register_pas_asset hardcodes clawback_allowed=true without doc justification
recommendations: Either expose `clawback_allowed: bool` as a parameter on `register_pas_asset`, or document explicitly in the module-level doc-comment that Hadron-deployed PAS assets are ALWAYS clawback-enabled and why (e.g. regulatory requirement).
descriptions: The call `policy::new_for_currency(pas_namespace, &mut treasury_cap, true)` passes `clawback_allowed = true` unconditionally. This is a permanent property of the PAS Policy — there is no upstream function to rescind clawback once enabled (see `/Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/policy.move`). Operators who do not want their token to have clawback authority cannot express that intent through the Hadron API.
impacts: Design rigidity. A non-clawback PAS asset cannot be registered via Hadron.

title: pas_admin::register_transfer_template uses byte-string literals over a `const`
recommendations: Extract `PAS_TRANSFER_MODULE: vector<u8> = b"pas_transfer";` and `APPROVE_TRANSFER_FN: vector<u8> = b"approve_transfer";` as module-level consts.
descriptions: The module and function names (`"pas_transfer"`, `"approve_transfer"`) are embedded as byte-string literals inline. If the module is ever renamed, the rename will succeed silently and the PTB template will reference the old name.
impacts: Refactor hazard.
evidence:
let cmd = ptb::move_call(
        approval_type.address_string().to_string(),
        b"pas_transfer".to_string(),
        b"approve_transfer".to_string(),

title: No post-registration API to rotate PAS policy approvals
recommendations: Expose a `pas_admin::rotate_transfer_approval<T, NewApproval>` function gated by `TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>` that: (1) borrows `policy_cap<T>`, (2) calls `policy.set_required_approval::<_, NewApproval>(...)` and `remove_action_approval` as needed, (3) re-registers the transfer template. Document the V1→V2 upgrade flow.
descriptions: `treasury::policy_cap<T>` returns `&PolicyCap<Balance<T>>` (immutable borrow only). There is no public accessor for `&mut PolicyCap<Balance<T>>`, and upstream `pas::policy::set_required_approval` requires `&PolicyCap<T>` (immutable) but `remove_action_approval` also only needs `&PolicyCap<T>`. Since `PolicyCap` is used immutably, Hadron's `policy_cap` accessor is sufficient for approval-rotation — BUT Hadron exposes no such endpoint, so rotations require reaching into the DOF raw or writing a new admin entry-point.
impacts: Operational. If Hadron needs to rotate the `TransferApproval<T>` type (e.g., to a `TransferApprovalV2`), there's no documented admin path to do so. Operators would have to write a custom upgrade PTB or a new admin function per rotation.

title: Table-based whitelist storage may grow unbounded without GC hook
recommendations: Consider adding an `unregister_pas_asset<T>` admin function that (a) asserts policy allows it (e.g. required_approvals empty), (b) drains and drops the whitelist Table, (c) extracts and returns TreasuryCap / MetadataCap / PolicyCap for institutional custody. Requires consultation with upstream on PAS Policy teardown.
descriptions: Whitelist is a `Table<address, WhitelistEntry>` stored as a DOF. `remove_from_whitelist<T>` clears individual entries, but there is no bulk-remove, no enumeration, and no "revoke everyone" admin. For a PAS asset lifecycle that ends (e.g. bond matures and is fully redeemed), the Table persists, along with its DOF wrapper. Also no path to delete the PAS asset entirely — `add_pas_coin_caps` has no inverse.
impacts: Long-lived treasuries accumulate dead state. Not a storage-fee blocker today but surface as info for product team.

title: pas_supply::seize takes treasury: &Treasury — assert_mint_redeem_enabled on an immutable ref works but semantics confusing
recommendations: No action required. Document the rationale in the module header if readers might be confused why seize is `&` and burn is `&mut`.
descriptions: `seize<T>` takes `treasury: &Treasury` (immutable). Good — it doesn't need to write to treasury state. But `burn<T>` takes `&mut Treasury` only because `treasury_cap_mut` is needed. `mint<T>` takes `&mut Treasury` same reason. This is consistent but note: `seize` can thus run concurrently with other `&` readers (e.g. read-only queries), while `burn`/`mint` serialize. For a high-throughput seize workflow this is fine; just worth confirming the treasury's DOF layout allows `&Treasury` to borrow the policy/whitelist (it does — both are DOFs accessed via `dynamic_object_field::borrow` which only requires `&UID`).
impacts: None at rest; informational.

title: pas_transfer::transfer_approval_permit public(package) — no doc on internal::permit defining-module rule
recommendations: Expand the doc-comment to cite `std::internal::Permit`'s defining-module rule and to note that `TransferApproval` and `transfer_approval_permit` MUST remain in the same module. Add a similar note in `pas_supply` for `ClawbackApproval` even though it's not currently permitted externally.
descriptions: `transfer_approval_permit<T>()` returns `internal::Permit<TransferApproval<T>>` and is marked `public(package)` so `pas_admin::register_transfer_template` can construct it. Per `std::internal` (see `/Users/alilloig/workspace/sui/external-crates/move/crates/move-stdlib/sources/internal.move` L34-38 — "Instances can only be created by the module that defines the type T"), only the module defining `TransferApproval` can call `internal::permit<TransferApproval<T>>()`. The doc-comment mentions this rule but without referencing the stdlib contract. A future refactor that moves `TransferApproval` to a different module will silently break the template-registration pathway.
impacts: Refactor hazard. A naive rename could produce a cryptic compile error far from the actual issue.

title: scripts/constants.ts: PermissionTypes adds whitelistManage / seize but no test that they match Move
recommendations: Generate PermissionTypes from Move via the existing codegen pipeline, or add a runtime sanity check at startup that verifies each permission struct exists at the target package.
descriptions: `PermissionTypes` enumerates all permission type fully-qualified names as strings. Adding `whitelistManage` and `seize` requires typing them in sync with the Move source. There is no codegen-enforced linkage; a typo or stale permission type will produce a parse error only at TX submission. The rest of the codebase uses sui-ts-codegen (see package.json `codegen` script), but PermissionTypes is hand-maintained.
impacts: Future Move refactor that renames a permission leaves `PermissionTypes` out-of-sync; silent drift caught only at runtime.

title: No DeepBook / display / standards-compliance audit trail for PAS coin deploy
recommendations: Optionally wire a sui::display::new_with_fields call during deploy — or document explicitly that Display registration is out-of-scope and must be set up separately.
descriptions: The PAS token template deploys a non-regulated coin via coin_registry::new_currency_with_otw. Unlike the regulated template, no Display object is created, no kiosk/transfer policy is wired. Per Sui coin standards docs, coins typically also register Display for wallet rendering. Missing Display means PAS tokens may show as unnamed/iconless in wallets.
impacts: UX impact only; PAS tokens may not render nicely in third-party wallets.
